=== Dokploy vs Coolify // Docker Swarm changes the game! ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SIn42DgS5cc

Hey guys, it's Christian and today we'll take a look at the two self-hosted app deployment platforms, Koolifi and Docloy. Many of you really liked my recent Koolifi video, but wanted to see a direct comparison against Docky because some of you thought that might be the better solution. They both let you deploy containerized applications and services on your own hardware, but without you having to manually set up the infrastructure or manage the deployment processes. So on paper they seem to do the exact same thing but as always the devil is in the details. There are a few important differences where I personally think Doy is better than Cudify but also some others where Cudify might be better than Docloy depending on what matters most to you. One of them might be a much better fit for your setup. So in this video we'll take a look behind the curtain and compare them side by side. Quick note, it is not a full tutorial. So, here I'm mostly just talking about the features and I will tell you my personal opinion where the one or another solution is better. If you're looking for a step-by-step guide, again, I've already done one for Coolifi. I leave you a link in the description. But if you want a dedicated docloy tutorial as well, then just leave a comment and of course I can make one for you. Let's quickly cover what they both have in common because there is obviously a need for a platform like this that gives you the convenience of a heroko or versal style workflow where you just push your applications to a git repository and the platform then is magically turning this into a live service deployment. Both tools contain a reverse proxy that's handling the incoming traffic, managing TLS certificates and they also can automatically update the application where the code is changing. They basically manage the infrastructure under the hood entirely for you but on your own hardware. That's probably the biggest difference between tools like Dock Ploy and Coolifi and cloud service platforms like Heroku or Versal. Here it's all happening on your local infrastructure. So either your HomeLab virtual servers or if you have a favorite hosting provider of course you can install Dockly or Koolifi on these servers as well. The installation is also super simple. Both tools have a simple oneliner command that you just copy and then paste in the terminal of your virtual servers and they just start doing the installation completely for you. They ask you the one or another question after the setup procedure where you create your Ratman accounts. They are both very simple and easy to set up and also very intuitive to get started. Now after the setup procedure you can log into a nice and clean web interface on docklo or on the koolifi platform and then you can start managing your servers and start configuring it. And here is where they start to diverge a little. But before we start talking about the differences and the exact features of docu and coolify I quickly want to talk about another incredible tool called monday.com. Some of you might already know it, but they just launched some gamechanging AI features that take it to the next level. For example, what caught my attention is their AI assistant called Sidekick. This thing can create boards, write documentation, research online, and so much more to help you not only manage your tasks and projects, but also run your entire operations and workflow to save you valuable time and expand what your teams can deliver. Also, the new VIP apps are amazing. There you just describe what you need in plain English like a buck tracker, incident response workflows or a software release planner and it builds the entire custom application in seconds including columns, views, automations, everything. It is really incredible. And the AI workflows orchestrate entire processes end to end where you can create repeatable automations that happen on specific events like analyzing the severity of incoming tickets and routing this to the right team or summarizing meeting notes and other tasks. The possibilities are really endless. So if you're interested in trying out monday.com for free and you want to check it out, of course you can do that with the link in the description box down below. All right. So now let's come back to dock ploy and koolify. So here I'm logged into my doc plos and koulifies testing instance in my home lab. Currently I'm not running any of them in a production setup. I will later tell you why I'm not doing this but I've been running these two platforms side by side for a few weeks and trying out different things like connecting different git providers, trying out different build types, deploying docker compost stacks, deploying some noode.js JS applications, making some updates on the git repo and seeing how that translates into a live environment. And I personally think that although Cudify is a nice platform, in some ways Docky is much much better. First and foremost, Docy is a swarm first platform. That's probably the strongest argument against Koolifi. Even though that Coolifi has some experimental flex for swarm, if you go to servers management and go to swarm experimental, you can make this node a swarm manager or swarm worker. There's also a documentation that this is an experimental feature. But again, you can see already that cool was not built with Docker Swarm in mind. The installation instructions recommend you that you set up at least one dedicated management server of Koolifi and then add at least one deployment server that has a little more resources where you deploy your applications. And of course you can add more than one server to the Koolifi platform. But still these servers are operating individually. You have to manage the reverse proxy and all the settings on each of them. In dock ploy this is completely different. Once you go through the installation script of DockP, it will automatically enable the swarm mode and create a new cluster on the server you install. And then once you enabled a container registry, you can add more than one worker or manager node to this swarm cluster. It supports the two primary ways to scale your servers. Vertical scaling by adding more CPU or memory into the machines or horizontal scaling by adding multiple servers. Sorry for the light background. I didn't make this presentation by the way. And it also has a multi-node routing model with the traffic reverse proxy under the hood and a load balancer that automatically forwards or redirects the incoming traffic to the actual service applications on your workers. So that is not existing or at least not existing in that same way on the Koolifi platform. And this is in my opinion such a big plus for the docloy platform because if you're using a platform like this you probably expected to have some kind of horizontal scaling method where you can add more than one replica for your application deployments. Also I think that docloy has a much better and simpler user interface and user experience. I know this is very subjective but that's just my honest and humble opinion. I think that the Docloy's user interface looks a lot more professional and find settings for. Of course, Coolify also has a nice web interface and it has dark mode and it of course isn't terrible, but I think there are a lot of different nested menus that are somehow laid out a little weird and there are these annoying sponsorship popups in the Cudifi dashboard. I know that free and open source projects like this, they kind of need a little support and monetization, but yeah, why not just leave a simple link in the dashboard or on your Git repository? You don't actually need an annoying popup that always asks the user to donate money. The Doc doesn't have anything like this. Again, it looks a lot more cleaner and a lot more professional and is in my opinion the better user experience or the better user interface. Also, Docy has many more git providers and options than Koolifi. So, here you can add GitHub, GitLab, both self-hosted and cloud. By the way, you can add Bitbucket, Gy, Docker, a generic git or upload any deployment files. Coolify, you can add a public repository. So, a generic git repo. You can also add a private repo with GitHub apps or a private repository with deploy keys. So that might work on the one or another git platform. For example, in GitLabs, a self-hosted version. I've tried it with deploy keys. But honestly, I think if you don't want to use deploy keys that give the application instantly access to the entire Git platform, you want to use a different way to connect private repositories with some form of API token or SSH authentication. This is not easily possible here in Kify, at least as far as I know. So you can add public repositories or private repos with deploy keys. But here in dock ploy for example you can add a generic g repository and add an ssh key authentication to it. And of course it supports bitbucket and gt that is not even possible in koolifi. So in my opinion this is much better and also other integrations like build type support. Honestly, for me, it is not so uh terrible. Overall I think Doc play sounds like the better solution at least to me. Looks more professional, has better user interface and more integrations, more provider supported but there are still a few things that Koolifi still does better than dock ploy. And of course I want to mention them. First of all, Koolifi has many many uh preconfigured or pre-defined settings and management utilities. One of them might be Cloudflare tunnel. You cannot see this on the local host server. But if you add another deploy server to the Koolifi's platform, you would have a separate menu with Cloudflare tunnels where you just add your credentials and then Koolifi is managing the Cloudflare demon that automatically connects to the Cloudflare servers and initiates a secure tunnel to your local server where you could publish applications to the public internet in a secure and simple way. Doc play also has some integration guide that explain what Cloudfare tunnels is and how to integrate and use Cloudfare tunnels in Doc's environment. It is another guide that you have to run through in Koolifi. This is much better integrated and much simpler to use. Also, what is much better in Coolify than in dock ploy is that it supports multiple reverse proxies. So here for example in the proxy settings you can switch your proxy from the defaults traffic reverse proxy to caddy for example if you prefer that or you could also completely disable the proxy integration. So then you could use your own traffic instance and use whatever is installed on the remote server. Honestly, this is super annoying in the Dockplace platform because Docy uses traffic under the hood and you can also configure it by unblocking the files and jumping in and configuring stuff, but it only supports traffic and only in a very specific way. So, I tried to modify the traffic uh compos files and the traffic configurations in Docky and it was a real pain in the ass. So, I would recommend if you're using doc ploy and that already comes with a preconfigured and pre-installed version of the traffic reverse proxy, just use it as it is and don't try to modify too much. Maybe add your own uh middleware configurations and maybe the one or another setting here and there, but don't try to replace the traffic reverse proxy with something else. This is not working great. instead in Koolifi. That is also not the easiest way to do, but you could do that. So, disable the traffic reverse proxy also and then do your own stuff and Coolifi will still attach the traffic labels to your Docker deployments. So, your custom traffic reverse proxy would pick that up. So it is in my opinion much much easier to manage your own custom reverse proxies or use different reverse proxies in Coolify than it is in dock ploy. So there it probably isn't even possible. Also what Coolifi does a little better if you go to projects and add a resource it supports more database types than dock ploy. Obviously it is not such a strong argument because there are just free others like key DB, Dragonfly and ClickHose. This is not possible in docys. So there you can go to projects and create a database service and choose from Postgress, MongoDB, Mariab, MySQL. Honestly I think yeah these five database types are more than enough. So if you want to deploy another database type well it's super simple on docker. You just create a service called docker compose deploying the database and that's it. But yeah again if you are using these type of platforms you're probably one of the users uh that don't want to set up databases on their own. So there might be one or another use case where you'll think, oh, I'm really into uh click host databases or KDB. Uh that's probably better in Kudify. Also, what Coolifi does a little better than Docky is that it has more guard rails and housekeeping maintenance features. What do I mean by this? Well, you find things in here like a Docker cleanup scheduleuler for example. So that automatically deletes unused volumes, unused networks. It just runs a docker purge command somewhere on a server. uh but that is really important because otherwise the docker images take too much space on your disks and at some point the server is getting full. Coolify does many many of these management tasks like disk usage check or notify you when the disk usage is more than 80%. In doc ploy there's also something like this existing. For example, if you go to a project, you can run any scheduled task here. So add any chron job expression and then run a simple bash or shell script there. You could basically do the same thing. For example, run a database backup or do a docker cleanup. So this is not as such a strong argument for cudify because these maintenance features are more for people that want to have a one-click solution. If you're familiar with server management, you know how to run bash scripts or how to work with chronoprops. There's actually no real reason to choose cooly over docklo just because it has this docker cleanup script. But it still might be interesting for the one or another person that coolify is a little simpler and takes you a little more by the hand when it comes to these type of u management features. Still, in my opinion, Docky is the much better system when you directly compare these two because first and foremost, it uses Swarm. It takes away a lot of the complexity of setting up a cluster, doing load balancing, setting up TLS, certificates, and it supports more integrations like Git providers and build types, and also has a cleaner and simpler user experience in design. So, yeah, if I would have to pick one of these two solutions for my home lab, it probably would be Doc. However, Kulifi seems to be like the more home lever's choice. Yeah, if you don't need Swarm, fine. You have many housekeeping scripts like the Docker cleanup, Cloudflare tunnel integrations, everything you can of course technically do in Docky as well with custom crunch jobs or scripts, but Koolifi makes it a little easier for the less technical person, I guess. And I don't mean this in an offensive or negative way. less technical just means that you probably don't have to manage so much and you just don't have to care so much about the infrastructure which is actually the main reason why you would want to use such a platform in the first place. Anyway, I still don't think that I personally will adopt either of them. Neither do norify and that for one simple reason. I don't like platforms that force you to use them only in the way they want it and abstract things by hiding it from you or making it less customizable. What do I mean by this? Well, just like I explained, they work pretty well if you install them on a fresh and new server. Yeah, set up a new virtual machine, run the oneline script installation command, and you'll be fine. But if you like to integrate either dock ploy or coolify into an existing environment like try to run this on an existing docker swarm cluster where you already have an established uh traffic configuration and everything is running fine and you just like to add dock ploy. Trust me guys, it's just a pain in the ass. Yeah, don't try this. So if you want to use docky or coolify, just set up a new virtual machine. Don't try to install them in an existing environment that doesn't really work well. For me, it's just creating a lot more complexity because I already have my own build process pipelines. I can build applications and container images by using CI/CD. I'm using GitLabci or GitHub actions. Then I seriously don't need a platform that does it all for me, but adds a lot more complexity because it doesn't integrate with the rest of my workflows. So that's why I still would prefer just using GitLab CI or GitHub actions. But again, still I think Docy and Koolifi are great systems and there is obviously a need and a great use case for that. I'm probably just not the main target audience, but maybe you are. And hopefully this video helped you to decide whether you want to use Doy or Koolifi and what is the better system for you. So I would love to hear your opinion about these two platforms. And if you would like to see more tutorials like this, then please don't forget to give this one a like and subscribe to my channel. Again, I could make a dedicated Doc Boy tutorial if you would want, or maybe you have another idea, then you can tell me in the comments. Thanks everybody for watching. A big thanks goes out to all of my supporters. You guys are really amazing. You make all these free tutorials possible. And of course, I'm going to catch you in the next one. Take care. Bye-bye.

=== Minisforum MS-R1 Review: The World's First ARM Mini Workstation ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=n_QUgxI4OfU

Hey guys, this is Christian and today we are reviewing the Minis Forum MSR1. This was recently sent to me by Minis Forum and honestly I was really excited about holding it in my hands because this little thing here is no regular x86 Intel or AMD CPU. It is the world's first ARM mini workstation with a proper UFI boot. You might be wondering why I'm so excited about it. Well, that's because ARM is well known for being incredibly power efficient. You probably are familiar with the Raspberry Pi. That's a great example for a small and power efficient ARMbased MIDI computer that's often used in home labs or for experiments. But here's the problem. When you need a little more power than a Raspberry Pi, you didn't really have many choices in the ARM world. Sure, there are enterprisegrade ARM servers that usually costs maybe more than $2 or $3,000, but until now, there wasn't really something in between that. And that's exactly where this Mini Forum MSR1 is supposed to fit in. It's a device that costs around like $500 to $600 depending on uh what upgrade you want. You can get it with 32 GB of memory or 64 GB of memory. That of course costs a little more. And yeah, honestly guys, that just sounded too good to be true. A powerful but efficient ARMbased system that could be just amazing for home labs. And yeah, after testing it, I can say it isn't quite what I expected. But yeah, let's start simple and just go over the hardware specs here. As I said, this is the world's first ARM mini workstation supporting UFI. Why is that a big deal? Well, that means you can just use this thing like a regular PC and just install any ARM compatible operating system on it like Ubuntu ARM, Debian ARM, whatever. Any ARM compatible applications will run on it. You can even run a custom version of Proxbox on ARM or even Windows on ARM if for whatever reason you want to do that. But yeah, the freedom is your choice. The special thing about this is that it has a Chinese ARM SOC. So this is the CIX CP8180 that comes with 12 cores, 12 threts, 2.6 GHz, and a 28 watt TDP. Honestly, that's already interesting because we usually see Qualcomm or Apple in this space. It also has an NPU for some AI stuff with 28.8 T ops. Honestly, I haven't tested how good the AI chip is on this little thing, but yeah, you might find some other benchmarks on this system somewhere on the internet as well. My take on this chip is that it sounds decent on paper, but it's a relatively unknown chip, so we'll need to test real world performance, and 28 W sounds efficient, but it has a small problem with idle power consumption. More about that later. It also comes with a 32 or 64 GB of LP DDR5 5500 MHz RAM which is of course soldered so you cannot upgrade it but it has ECC the error correction code on board which is really rare for any consumer mini PCs especially for building storage devices ECC support might be interesting for storage it has a dual M4 NVME slot both full size and this is really great so you can run any RAID systems or install separate operating systems or add storage hard drives. There's also a dual 10 GB Ethernet port on this thing which is using a realtech chip. I've used that same network chip for my open sense firewall, although many people told me to not do that, but uh for me it always worked completely fine. So, I never had any issues with that chip. It also has a full-sized x16 slot which is running on PCI Express 4.0. So, you can add a powerful GPU. I mean, good luck finding ARM drivers, though. But more importantly, you could add a quad port uh network card. You could add capture cards or whatever you want to extend this mini PC with. And this is really nice. It's a small. It sits nicely on a desk without taking too much space. The chassis is probably the best part on this system. You just open it by pressing a little button on the side and then you can just slide out the entire motherboard, giving you full access to all of the components inside. This is really great when you quickly need to swap storage or add a PCI Express expansion card or change anything inside. You quickly get access to all the components. And the build quality is again really solid. Absolutely love this. More importantly, I also ran a Geekbench. Here are the results. So, for single core score, it has 1,326 and a 6,510 multi-core score. Undoubtly much more that you would get on a Raspberry Pi. The Raspberry Pi 5 usually has like 700 single core and about 2,000 multi-core score performance. Actually, it's quite near to the Apple M1 Mac Mini. That, by the way, has a 2,000 single core and about 8,700 multi-core score performance. So, this is somewhere in between the performance of a Raspberry Pi 5 and an Apple M1 Mac Mini. Of course, more in the range of the Apple system, obviously. So, you get about like two or three times the performance of a single Raspberry Pi 5, but of course, you also have to take in consideration that you're probably paying like two or three times the price. I've also tested the power consumption and this is where things are getting a little weird here because honestly I was expecting a much lower idle power consumption. I've tested about like 19 watts on idle and when I was running Geekbench the power consumption went up to 32 watt. I really thought that it would have a much lower power consumption, but that probably depends on the Chinese SOC chip that is maybe not really optimized for power efficiency on idle. I've seen much lower idle power consumptions on equivalent x86 uh CPUs to be honest. So, for example, my open sense firewall, which has a similar minis forum system, runs the AMD Ryzen 5 8,500G, which has a single core score of 2,300 and a multi-core score of 9,444. I think the idle power consumption I've measured on this device is about, let me not lie, but I think it's about like 7 watts on idle or so. So, it's less than half of the idle power consumption of the Minis Fororum MSR1 and still has much more power. I think this is also a problem that Minis Forum itself noticed on this chip here because they just updated a Git repository where they share some guidance on how to reinstall the operating system or install Proxmox VE for ARM systems. They also have a specific page for optimize idle power where they admit that when the MSR1 was released, the idle power consumption was not adequately optimized. They say they are trying to optimize the power consumption and this documentation contains a link to a Google Drive share where you can download a new firmware and that would bring down the idle power consumption down to 1412.7 W. But this is still more than the AMD Ryzen CPUs are consuming. And I think it is also a little more than their more powerful MSA1 or MSA2 systems. So although I heard so many great things about ARM system being so power efficient when you compare this to another x86 mini PC, honestly this thing really falls behind in terms of power consumption, in terms of power efficiency, in terms of performance in general and of course in compatibility because we're still talking about an ARMbased system. So not every application runs on it. Although the support uh on ARM systems has really become better. But if you're thinking about getting this system here because you want a powerful but power efficient mini PC for your home lab, I generally would not recommend it because in my opinion, there are better x86 mini PCs that have a better compatibility and better power efficiency, better performance, and lower idle power consumption. However, I still think this system is not completely useless. Yeah, there are scenarios where you would definitely want to get one of these because if you're searching for a great ARM development or testing machine, this thing is obviously much more powerful than a Raspberry Pi, but it's not so expensive than these enterprisegrade ARMbased systems that you get from Nvidia or from other companies. So, if you need something more powerful than a Raspberry Pi, but it has to be ARM because you're using this for testing or development stuff, it's still great that we have this device because the fact that we don't have many options for ARMbased systems on the desktop is a chicken and egg problem. Some just needed to start and I really love that we have this thing. Maybe the second version of the Minis Forum R series is going to get better. Maybe these Chinese SOC uh boards are getting more efficient, are getting better with the next evolution. So, I'm definitely following up on this. So, yeah, that's my review on the Minis Forum MSR1. I hope it helped you to find out whether this thing might be something for you or not. So, thank you so much for watching. Again, a big thanks goes out to Minis Forum for sending me this device and to all of my supporters. You guys are really amazing. You are making all of this possible. And of course, I'm going to catch you in the next video. Take care. Bye-bye.

So here in this folder I have created a simple docker compos file that deploys a simple enginex web server. But you probably already noticed that the solution or the conversion with the config map for a web application that might contain more than a simple index.html file. It's not really the best tool for production ready applications. So therefore, I think compose is great when you have your own custom application, microservices. It's also great for quick migration of existing compos files and stacks or prototyping. So you just want to see how and if that's actually working on Kubernetes or maybe for niche internal tools without any published helm charts. But of course there are a few challenges with compose. It has limited support for advanced compose features. The generated manifests are very basic. You need manual tweaking for production just like we talked about the traffic labels. Volume handling can be tricky as we have seen with the config maps. Also the generated YAML files might not follow Kubernetes best practices without modifications. What do I mean by this? So here for example the deployment uses a simple service object but for a production ready application I probably would use different best practices with using a real reverse proxy maybe with integrating search manager to have trusted TLS certificates and all of that stuff. So that of course compose usually does not do for you. So therefore I think it is better to use Helm charts especially for thirdparty applications because they usually come with production ready configurations. They usually or most of the time should follow the best practices and are maintained by their communities or developers and they often have extensive config options with variables tested by the community have regular updates security patches and complex features built in. So that might include HA backups monitoring like the metrics endpoint and stuff like that. So if you're not familiar with Helm, I've made a tutorial a while ago about this, but this is an advanced package manager for Kubernetes. So that is so as they say, it is the best way to find, share, and use software built for Kubernetes. So yeah, what else would you say about your own application? Yeah, but anyways, it's really great. The new version 4.00 was just released for Helm. So there are a few new features. I don't want to go through these because I think it's not so interesting, mostly for developer stuff. But if you're searching for any Helm charts, for example, an EngineX web server, then you can also find these charts on the artifact hub. So there can search for something. There are a few different charts maintained by different uh developers or communities. I personally like the Bitnami charts the most. When you go to the templates, you can see that this Helm chart already creates all these objects like config maps, deployments, like all these manifests. But they have a sophisticated templating engine. As you can see, there are a lot of ifels statements. So depending on what values you give this tool, it will generate the manifests in a specific way that is tested that is audited and maintained by the developers or the creators of the Helm chart. So that might also include specific objects for reverse proxies like here for example is an ingress object that could picked up by the Android X or traffic reverse proxy. Here is a TLS secret for the certificates. So there is a lot of this stuff that you would have to customize otherwise when you're converting with a compost tool that is automatically handled here already. So if you want to simply deploy an engineext web server on Kubernetes, you can convert your old compos files if you want. But actually it is in my opinion still better to use Helm charts for this. === My awesome Grafana Dashboard for OPNSense! ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C93rACzeGJw

Hey guys, it's Christian and today I'm going to present you the new Graphana dashboard that I have created for my OpenSense firewall. After setting up my open sense monitoring stack, I wanted to have a comprehensive dashboard that shows all of the for me important metrics about the firewall system health and performance such as CPU, memory and disk utilization, but also important interface and network statistics such as the gateway up and down status, up table entries, TCP connection states, the received and transmitted bytes for each interface, as well as firewall statistics about past and blocked packets per interface, and some nice graphs that help me visualize, correlate, and compare all these metrics, which is absolutely necessary for troubleshooting issues on your firewall system. Now, some of you might wonder, why the heck did I create this completely by myself? Why didn't I pick one of the Graphana dashboards on their official website? Well, the problem is when you search for Open Sense, you actually don't find many dashboards and they really don't show all of the metrics that are important in one comprehensive dashboard. Also some of them have problems or they didn't work in my setup which is actually a problem that I have with graphana dashboards as a whole not only for open sets monitoring also when I search for container monitoring or server monitoring dashboard there's almost always something that doesn't quite work or that I would love to change to fit better in my environment or to show me better the matrix that I actually care about. So, I thought it's about time that I somehow familiarize myself with how to create these dashboards completely by myself instead of just relying on some of the published community dashboards. Now, honestly, building a custom Graphana dashboard completely from scratch always sounded intimidating to me at first, especially because I wasn't familiar with the Prometheus querial language. But, I've done some researching on it. I've spent a few hours juggling around with the different settings in Graphanas and playing around with PromQL queries, experimenting with what works and what does not work so that you don't have to do it yourself and go through the same trial and error. Of course, in the end, I will share you my OpenSense Graphana dashboard so you can easily import it in your own environment. However, I also thought that I'm going to use the opportunity to make a small tutorial about PromQL and how to build your own Graphana dashboards. Maybe not completely from scratch, but it is also super useful to know some of these basics if you prefer to import community Graphana dashboards, but sometimes you want to tweak a few small things here and there to make it fit better in your environment. And I think this is absolutely valuable. All right guys, so now let's get started with building custom graphana dashboards. Again, this is really tied to my OpenSense monitoring stack. Especially because in the beginning, I'd like to cover a few basics about PromQL, the Prometheus query language. Because the Graphana dashboards are really just a visualization of the raw data that is stored in a data source such as Prometheus. So here I'm collecting and aggregating all the important metrics from any of my systems not just open sense but also my servers my docker containers. You can see all the different metrics that are stored in there with all the raw values and therefore we need clever functions sometimes calculations or filterings to build our queries to see and gather exactly what we need. And then when you edit them, you can also change any of the visualization styles, colors, unit settings, tool tips and whatnot. It is a very complex system. Most of the stuff in here is quite intuitive. So I think we don't need to cover each and everything, but a few basics are in my opinion really important. Let's first of all start with the format. How the exporters provide the matrix for a scraping job either in Prometheus or by using Graphana alloy. So this is small service that we've used in my previous tutorial to scrape two exporters that will provide metrics for my open sense firewall. First of all the built-in OS node exporter. So this is mainly responsible for providing metrics about general system statistics that are mostly freebd standardized because that's what's using open sense under the hood. But I'm also using a small docker container. This is actually a community project called opensense exporter by attenna's mind and this will connect to the open sense API to gather more opensense specific metrics so that all can scrape these two exporters and ingest all the data into Prometheus and these usually start with a hashtag help. So this is a help text to to describe what this metric is about and then it continues in the next line with a hashtag type. Then comes the name of the metric and then the metric type. Note there are four different metric types that you should know. There are counters which are simple numbers that only increase. So they usually start somewhere at zero or at one or any other value and then increase over time and they sometimes reset when the exporter restart. So this is sometimes used to count the system uptime or in this case uh count the total scripes of the node exporter. So this is a number that always increases once you restart the exporter. It will reset to zero and then go on from there. The next metric types are gouges. I hope I pronounced that correctly. If not, you can correct me in the comments. But gouges are also simple numbers, but they can instead of the counters not only increase, they can go up and down. And these are used to, for example, uh expose the free bytes on the system memory. This is also used for disk utilization and stuff like that. There are also two other types, the histograms and the summaries. We're not getting into these because there are a little more complex and uh we actually don't need them for the open science monitoring dashboard. However, I also thought if you want to see a more in-depth tutorial into Prometheus or PromQL, then please leave me some comments and I could make a tutorial about creating histograms or summaries. I needed that once to create a dashboard for an anti virus malware engine to visualize the latency of the scanning times in a heat map. But these are more complicated visualizations. We don't need them right now. But I would say like 80 or 90% of all the metric types are either counters or gouges. And then of course you also have in the curly brackets the label sets. So as you can see here this metric types shows a simple counter of the received bytes in total and of course this counter is completely different for the network interfaces. So here you can see this metric has a device label that defines the network interface relevant for this value. And you can later use these label sets to create more custom filters to actually see a metric only for a specific interface. or you can use aggregation or transformation functions to summarize them or to calculate a rate over a specific time window and so on. But this is basically everything you have to know about the Prometheus format of the matrix. Okay, now let's go back to my Graphana dashboard. Then you can go back like an hour ago or you can uh zoom in, zoom out, go left, go right depending on what the time frame you want to see. So it's really flexible and these metrics automatically adapt to this time window that you select here. But as you can see here, I've also added multiple visualizations. I don't want to go through each and everything by detail but I think CPU utilization is the next that I should explain in detail because this has a more complicated query with some functions and some other filters. Let's first of all start simple. I want to show you uh this metric here the note CPU seconds in total because what this graph should visualize is the CPU utilization. Unfortunately, uh the kernel does not expose a metric or a gouch that will uh give us the CPU utilization in a simple percentage. Yeah, it's not as easy as that. So, this is actually a counter. So, a number that is only increasing, never decreasing. It's a counter that tracks the total accumulated CPU time since the system booted. And this exists for each CPU core. And each CPU core is always in one of four different modes at any microscond. So the kernel tracks the time the CPU spends in each of the different modes. It can be in the user mode. So these are regular applications like engine X, the firewall demon, whatever. Then there's the system mode where the CPU is executing kernel code. The interrupt mode where the CPU is handling hardware inputs and the idle mode. So that counts when the CPU is doing nothing. So it's just waiting for work. There's also something else, the nice mode that I needed to Google. So, this seems to be another user process with positive nice value. Honestly, I don't know what that is. And as you can see, it is always zero. So, it seems that this metric isn't actually relevant for this system. You can see this is for the BSD system. For a Linux operating system, there are other modes existing like IRQ, user system and so on. So you can see these metrics sometimes also are different uh depending on the operating system or yeah how it's actually uh presented or calculated by the system. Now for BSD these modes seem to be relevant and basically anything except the idle mode is processing something. So if you want to calculate the CPU utilization, it is important to know how fast this number is increasing given at that specific time window because then we can calculate the rate. So how much time did the CPU core spend in each of the modes and when it's spending more time in idle mode than in any of the other modes where it's processing, you know that the CPU utilization is much lower. So that's the logic how you calculate the CPU utilization again is a little complex and we need to use some advanced promql uh functions to actually calculate these rates and compare them against each other. So we will uh get the matrix TPU seconds in total for only my firewall instance when it's not in mode idle because only the idle mode this is doing nothing anything else is processing time. So when we get this query here and put that in Prometheus, we get all the values for each mode and each CPU core. So we get a lot of metrics here. And again, we don't need the raw numbers. We want to know how fast these numbers are increasing given at a specific time window. And therefore, we're using the rate function. So the rate function calculates the per second average rate of the increase of time series. And when you're using the rate function, you always have to give it a time window. This is added by these brackets here. And you can see that I'm using a specific function. This is using the rate interval function. This is a variable in graphana that automatically calculates the optimal time window for the rate calculations based on the dashboard's time range and refresh interval. This is actually recommended. You could also specify a hard-coded time window. Uh, for example, 1 minute. If you increase the time window, you get less granular output of this. So the rate is more flattened. If you want to see more spikes, you should use 1 minute. But I think it is always recommended to use the rate interval. So this will give you usually the best result in graphana. Now again, this would produce many different uh curves and that wouldn't help us anything. So I think the best is to summarize these values because interrupt nice system and user are all CPU modes that actually process something. I know it sounds a little confusing but I've tried to explain it the best way I can. Now this function here sum without mode actually just means that we are summarizing all these rates. So we are combining all the CPU modes user system interrupt and nice but we are keeping the CPU cause. So it is a little confusing that it sounds some without mode because at first I thought okay this would summarize anything except mode but it is the opposite around. So it's just removing the mode label. So anything that is in these brackets here will be removed. Let me demonstrate how that looks like when I run it in Prometheus. I I think that it's then a little simpler to understand. So then you can see that it just summarized all the rates from the different modes, but it still differentiates them on for each CPU core. So then you could actually see in the graph view if we uh I can remove this here as well. So then you can see a graph for each CPU core. Honestly, I think this actually doesn't provide much value because uh what I want to know is what is the average utilization of all the CPU cores. You can use the average average function without the CPUs. So then you get the average utilization across all the CPU cores and then we only have one curve that shows us the overall CPU utilization. === Coolify: The Ultimate Self-Hosted Platform as a Service? ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6IZF_VOlOJM

Hey guys, it's Christian and in today's video I'm going to show you Koolifi. This is an open- source platform that lets you easily deploy any type of applications, services or databases on your own hardware. It's actually in the same category as something like Heroku or Versal, which are both developer platforms that take your replications plus some settings and turn them into live deployments. Don't worry if you're not familiar with any of these platforms at all. In practice, it simply just means that you bring your application, which often is a NodeJS, Python or Go project. And these of course all need an environment with runtime dependencies, environment variables, sometimes a database. And that is exactly where these platforms like Hioko or Versal became so popular because instead of manually setting up servers or dealing with deployment steps and release workflows, you typically just connect a git repository where you upload the source code of your application and these platforms will handle all the other steps for you. So they will build your application often in a container image and then deploy it in a large scaled cloud infrastructure and they often also take care of domains, HTTPS certificates, versionings, roll backs and stuff like that. Cutify is something like this too, but with a few important differences that make it especially interesting for HomeLab fans or self-hosting nerds like us. First of all is that you keep control of everything. So the applications actually run on your own servers. So you get the convenience of a modern platform as a service or serverless architecture but without handing everything over to a third party cloud vendor. And that isn't just great for privacy and data sovereignty. In most cases, it is also simply much cheaper to run applications on your own servers at home or even on your preferred and super cheap VPS provider instead of having to pay the huge cloud vendor bills. So that's why I thought it might be super interesting to take a look at it together. So let's install Coolify on two of my testing servers. After all, I've also got so many things to say about it. Many good things, but also a few not so good things. So let's just run through the basics together. And at the end of the video, you will have a good understanding about what Cudify actually is and whether you want to give it a try in your home lab as well. Now, before we jump right in, I also want to show you another amazing tool that helps you to manage all of your tech and home lab projects. Because if you're trying to balance multiple projects and tasks like I do, it is really easy to lose track. And simple tools like spreadsheets or notebooks, they just don't do the job. And that is exactly where monday.com jumps in. It's an all-in-one work platform for managing complex projects for easy collaboration and also automating repetitive work. monday.com is incredibly easy to use. Yeah, you can very intuitively create any type of to-do lists, add custom fields or change priorities and so many other things of your tasks and projects. What I personally absolutely love are the canban boards because they provide you a great high-level overview of all the tasks and their status where you can easily drag and drop items and you can even create advanced workflows by linking boards and setting dependencies. This is really perfect for tracking open issuers, prioritizing or delegating tasks across technical teams. And what tech people will also love is that you can easily write markdown based documentation files directly in monday.com and share this with your technical team. With the free plan of monday.com, you already get up to two seats with free boards, unlimited docs, and eight column types, which is great for smaller projects. But of course, if your project grows over time or you want to use it in larger teams and organizations, you can easily upgrade to higher plans as well to increase the features and integrate monday.com into other systems like use it for coordinating emails or sending out files and Slack messages. So to get started, you can try out monday.com completely for free by heading to the link in the description box down below to get your projects organized today. And uh by the way, there's one thing that I probably should explain at first because when you're reading this website and you're reading all the good news about the open source philosophy, the self-hosting, running everything on your own hardware, you might be a little confused by seeing this button cloud here next to self-hosted. And you might be wondering how is this then different from something like a Hoko or Versal where you also have a cloud service offering. where the important difference is that although Koolifi has a cloud service version is that this actually just runs the main controlling platform of Koolifi. So the cloud service just costs $5 a month where you can easily connect two of your servers plus $3 per month for each additional server. So that means the main Coolifi platform runs on a managed infrastructure. But for the actual applications to be deployed and running, you always have to bring your own servers. So there's no way in the cloud version of Koolifi to deploy applications in a managed infrastructure. You always have to set up either your own servers or connect any virtual or dedicated servers you might be running on your favorite VPS provider. This might probably be the easiest way to run everything because that of course makes things like the certificate management and stuff like that easier. But if you prefer to run completely everything on your own hardware, not just the application servers, but also the main Coolifi platform, you can also choose to use the self-hosted version, which is by the way what we are doing in this video. So there you have full access to all features. There are no limitations or restrictions, but of course you need your own infrastructure for Koolifi, which means you need one server for the main Koolifi platform. Plus, you need at least one or more servers for the application deployments. Before you begin, you should check if your server meets the requirements. So, you need a server with SSH access. That could be a virtual private server somewhere on a cloud provider. It could be dedicated server virtual machine maybe running on your Proxmox environment. === OPNSense Monitoring with Prometheus, Alloy and Grafana ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F3mvWIPTPjY

Hello everybody, it's Christian and today I'm going to show you how to build a strong and powerful monitoring system for your OpenSense firewall using Prometheus and Graphana. Now, some of you might wonder why the heck do we even need this? Because OpenSense already has a built-in dashboard where you can see some statistics about the systems health and performance. Or there's even a reporting menu where you can check in the different graphs and change the granularity, scroll back and forth, zoom in and zoom out. So the question is why do we actually need a different system? Well, one of the biggest challenges or the use cases where you would want to take a look at these statistics is when there is a problem. So when you need to troubleshoot maybe a system outage or a degraded performance, maybe you have some high latency on some network interfaces or only on specific network protocols and you just want to find out what was causing this issue. So then it's really hard in the default dashboard because even in the detailed health reporting system, you can only see one statistic at a time. And for example, if there was a huge spike in the CPU utilization and you want to find out was that happening at a time where there was a high network traffic, you always have to go back to a different uh panel and you cannot really compare and correlate these metrics at the same time. So therefore, I think it is much better to have a centralized monitoring system where you can select time frames. For example, if there was a high CPU spike and it will automatically correlate this with other statistics where you could find out, oh, this was at a time where there was a high network traffic or maybe it was at a time where a service was failing. Maybe you even have multiple open sense firewalls that you all want to monitor at the same time and you even want to customize all these settings like changing these panels or even go into the details like changing queries and customize these dashboards. Well, this is exactly where Graphana and Prometheus come into play because they provide you a more centralized and more powerful and more customizable monitoring dashboard where you can much better troubleshoot what was going wrong. So, that's exactly the plan for today. Of course, as you can imagine, there are a few different technologies and components involved into making this possible. Here, I've created a highle diagram for you to better understand how these components all work with each other. Some of you might see some familiar names here like Prometheus and Kofana. I hope you all know because otherwise it might be a bit hard to follow this one. I still try to make it easy to follow but of course I'm also preparing a list with other video tutorials about all these other technologies. So check it out if you like. But to quickly explain what you're seeing here. So on the right side we have our monitoring stack with Prometheus. So this is the time series database where we can store all of these metrics that is later easy to search and easy to visualize with a tool like graphana. So this is the web interface where you can build all of these dashboards and panels. It's actually just accessing the database back end on Prometheus. And on the left side we have our opensense firewall where we want to gather some metrics about the CPU, memory, disk utilization or about network interfaces. These are very general system metrics that are usually the same for every Linux operating system. But there are also a few very open sense specific metrics like gateway events, services and so on. And to collect those events and get them into our Prometheus database so we can later visualize them, we need a few components. First of all, we will install the OS node exporter which is effectively just collecting all these metrics here above. I've also found a nice open-source community project there for collecting very open sense specific metrics that are not provided on the Linux kernel. So this will constantly connect to the opens API query the information and also provide them in a Prometheus standardized format and then we will use graphana alloy. This is a small service application that also runs on a collector endpoint and this will constantly scrape the metrics provided in this standardized format from both the operating systems node exporter as well as the API exporter and scrape this every 15 30 seconds and send these metrics all to the Prometheus database where there are ingested and stored over time. So that's roughly the plan for today and the system that I found out to be the most reliable one to scrape all these metrics. But before we jump right into this topic, I want to say a few words about the sponsor of today's video, Twingate. This is my absolute favorite tool for remote access and always my first choice when I need to log into my HomeLab servers over SSH or if I need to access my administrative tools like my Kubernetes clusters or my Git repositories. is a great defense against any form of web-based attacks. Supports DNS filtering, content filtering, and provides privileged access across your entire network, cloud, and application infrastructures. It's what we call ZTNA intake, a zero trust network access platform that establishes secure connections between all of your devices where every single request has to be verified and authorized, otherwise Twate will just block the request. And this technology even works through NAT devices and firewalls without any port forwardings or firewall exceptions required. And the best, it is completely free for up to five users, 10 remote networks, and 20 different resources. So you can just try that out in your own home lab. Of course, I've made several tutorials about Twinate and how to install this. So start making your network more secure and accessible. Of course, I'll drop you a link to Twinate in the description box down below. All right, guys. So now let's get back to open sense monitoring. === Self-hosted Cloudflare + VPN replacement! Pangolin Tutorial ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c8f-YPiUBZM

Hey everybody, this is Christian. Happy New Year to everybody. So, we are back in 2026 with a brand new tutorial. Today's one is about Pangolin, the open-source identity- based remote access platform that is built on the sophisticated VPN protocol WireGuard, and that enables you to securely and seamlessly connect to private and public resources. Honestly guys, once I heard some people in our community telling me about Pangoline, my first thoughts were, "Oh man, do we really need another one of these solutions?" Some of you might know my favorite remote access platform is Twate. I'm using this for such a long time. But we've already covered some other open source alternatives like Network for example. So then we had Tail Scale Zero Tier, not to forget Cloudfare Tunnels. So yeah, there are already so many solutions like this out there. Why the heck do we need another one in the Hoblap space? But hear me out. Once I took a look at Penguin, I found out that it does a few things a little different than these other platforms because it tries to unite three unique use cases. Yeah, it's a combination of a reverse proxy which is using traffic under the hood. So with this, you can securely give access to internal resources using trusted TLS certificates and HTTPS. Plus, it has VPN capabilities. So you can run the main reverse proxy part on a public server and then route the external clients connections to local resources running in your home network. Plus it has ZGNA capabilities. So that gives you granular access permissions and controls and that is really super cool. So the plan for today is I want to give you a quick architecture overview to explain how actually works this magic under the hood. Then we will install Pangolin on a small VPS server in the cloud and connect a site running in my local home lab. Now, why do I want to host this on a VPS and not entirely in my home network? Well, this is one of these projects where running all of the components at home can get a little annoying. By the way, we will later take a look at the architecture overview to get in the details, but just real quick, if you want to run the main controlling part of Pangolin, so this is where all of the clients, public and private authenticated clients need to connect to. If you want to run this at home, you need to have a super stable public connectivity, ideally with a static IP address because you need to have a DNS record for it. And you also need to open a few TCP and UDP ports because otherwise the clients can't connect. And this is why I'd recommend instead of running this critical service at home, install the main controlling part of Panggalin on a small VPS in the cloud. So then the platform is always up and running and then you can still install the small Pangolin connector on your local network. So then you don't need any port forwardings or firewall exceptions. So this makes this setup a lot easier, which uh by the way brings me to the sponsor of today's video, Hostinger. Because Hostinger makes it super easy to run and set up Panggalin. You can just spin up a new Linux VPS in a couple of minutes. Pick any data center close to you and then install whatever you want on it. Yeah, you can install any Linux distro. You can install the Docker host, Panggalin traffic and any other service you like. And what I personally love so much about Hostinger, their VPS are just super fast. Yeah, they use NVME storage and modern AMD epic CPUs. And they also include super helpful features like snapshots and backups, which is just perfect if you're just testing a few things and you want quick roll backs. And what is also super cool, Hostinger just added a brand new Docker management feature, which is, I think, really practical. If you're running a couple of containers on your VPS, but you don't always want to mess around with SSH into your server for every little thing, just access your hosting VPS control plane, select the Docker manager to quickly see what's running, and then just start and stop containers, and you can even deploy new containers or change things in your configuration. I think for homelap style projects like this where you still need a VPS for any remote access tool like Pengolin, it just saves you a ton of time and makes things a lot easier while you still have the full control over the operating system and how you want things to be installed. So, if you want to build this setup alongside me, then just go to hostinger.com/lamper and use my code for an additional discount on yearly plans. Of course, you will find the link and the code in the description box down below. All right. So now let's get started with Panggolin. First of all, uh I know this was already quite a long introduction, but I still think it is important to explain how exactly that works and talk a little about the official documentation. So by the way, this is really a great documentation where you get a lot of information about all the details about the different terminologies like sites, resources, clients, what the heck is that and how does it work. I don't want to run through every little detail, but I think this is actually pretty important. Just like I told you the penguin server, this is the main central coordinating component for your network. This is the main place where all of the external clients are connecting to. So that needs to be always up and running. This needs to have trusted TLS certificates and also a DNS record by the way which is later done all by the installation script. And then you can still install the sites which are using the N connector. So this is a little software that handles all the tunnel creation, the nut traversal and routing across firewalls so that you effectively don't have to touch any firewall rules or you don't have to create exceptions because it's initiating the connection from inside the remote network where you install the site connector to your public pengolin platform. It's also interesting to take a closer look at the system architecture. This is pengoline that's the main controlling part. It's running the web dashboard, the API and the management system, the brain of uh everything to control it. And then you have traffic, the reverse proxy that handles HTTP, HTTPS routing, SSL termination, the let's encrypt certificates and stuff like that. And then it's using a traffic middleware called better. So this is a plug-in that intercepts every connection coming from the users or clients and that makes sure that the connections are authenticated to pangulin before allowing access to the application. Then it's also using durable. So this is managing the wire guard tunnels between your remote sites and the panggalin server which is optional for some things but it is certainly recommended when the pangolin server is running on a cloud virtual private server and the edge server. So the site your local home lab is somewhere different. So there you client this will initiate a connection first to the traffic reverse proxy and the pangolid server to authenticate. It's using gerbble to create this wire guard tunnel and then when somebody access the resource to your cloud VPS it's then routing the connection through the tunnel to the internal resources securely and then you can of course connect to any servers like port 4 for free port 80 SSH. All right. Um so to install this thing there is a quick install guide that you can use. Uh we will also use this guide because I think it's it's a lot easier to run the installation script. I tried to walk my way through the Docker Compose files and try to customize it a little and then I found out well maybe it's a little easier to just use the preconfigured or the predefined installation scripts. Now there are a few prerequisites that you definitely need uh to have. You need a Linux server with root access and a public IP address. Now, the block list are also kind of similar to Pi Hole. Default Pi Hole lists are using the uh Steven Black list, which is a community blacklist. Here you can see all the domain names that it contains and it contains about like 80,000 entries which is a lot and it's great that it's community maintained. However, when we go to the AdCot system, you can see it's a lot more. And when you click on this, you can see an important difference because the AdCot home DNS filter does not just support plain domain names. It's also supporting URL path blocking. Plus, Adgard uses their own filtering lists which come from their browser ad blocker extensions. Yeah. And it is in general more comprehensive coverage. So this is in my opinion a big plus for Adgot. And what is also super cool if you go to the settings and go to DNS settings you can see that it automatically configures a DNS upstream server. So where the DNS requests are forwarded to. And this uses by default Quatt 9 which is a nonprofit organization for uh managing DNS queries. And the important difference here is that you see the HTTPS protocol in front of that. So that means uh Edgar home is by default using built-in DO support DNS over HTTPS which means all the DNS requests are getting encrypted. We will later talk about some of the technical details about this and why this is such a big deal. And uh what is also a big plus that is not possible in Pi Hole, you can even add per client configuration block lists and settings. So this is a little hidden under uh client settings. So here you can add persistent clients by using an IP address or MAC address filtering. And then you can enable or disable certain settings for just that specific device. And you can even set other upstream servers or block specific services. So this is also super cool. I will show you that in a different menu, but you can set this up um globally or per client. So this lets you block preconfigured services like JGPT cla so AI services, dating websites. If you want to prevent your wife from opening the Tinder app, for example, you can just enable this or you can block gambling sites, gaming, web hosting. So there are so much in here. Social networks. Yeah, this is really cool. So you can very easily block specific services uh maybe for your children. Yeah, it also has some parental control features. So in my opinion, this is just a much much better feature set compared to Pi Hole. And you can also manage uh custom DNS records. Um this is by using DNS rewrites. So here you can add a new rewrite for example to send a query to a specific domain which is super cool. For example uh anything that goes to home.clave.te I want to send to my local DNS server instead of routing this to the internet. So I know a long introduction but honestly I think at home is super great. Thank you so much for anybody who recommended this in the comments because I actually didn't know that this was existing but it's really nice. But now that this is running, you might wonder how do you actually configure your devices to start using this. Uh so that's the next critical step because currently this is just sitting there and ready to work. But it's not actually processing anything because it doesn't receive automatically any queries from your network. You have to tell all of your devices, hey for DNS resolving, don't ask Cloudflare or your ISP DNS server, just send everything to Adgard Home. And this is then doing the filtering and all of that stuff. So according to the Adgard's documentation, there are two recommended approaches for configuring your network to use Adgard. Your option one is to configure your router or DHCP server, which is the recommended way. And the option two is to configure individual devices. Of course, uh individual device is always a lot of work. So that's why I'm not explaining it. It doesn't actually make sense. You simply just configure your home router, whatever device it is, configure your DHCP server settings to send a DNS server configuration to all of your devices. So then when a client is connecting to your home router and asking for an IP address via DHCP configuration, the home router will send the DNS server which uh you configure to the IP address of the atgot home server to the client and then the client can make a DNS request to Adgot. Now this is super easy. It doesn't require any additional configuration on your devices. Yeah, in my example I'm using an open sets firewall that I also made a video about. So here I just go to my services, go to IC DHCP and then just go to DNS servers and set the IP address of the server where atgard home is running. So just to test how that would work, if you want to do an NS lookup maybe on tinder.com. Yeah, don't think that I'm actually on this website. I'm just using this as a simple example. Yeah. So if you send a DNS query, you get an answer from your at home server. And when we do a refresh, you should see the DNS query popping up here in that list here. And you can also go in the query log to see what it is. Now let's try out the filtering because I don't want anybody to open the Tinder's website. So I can uh click on blocking services and I can save this. Now when I do another DNS request, you see I get back an IP address 00 which is the black hole redirection. So basically to nowhere and then my client device cannot resolve the real IP address of the Tinder's server. And then you also see in the query lock that this here is blocked. By the way, uh the query lock also allows you to search for something. Yeah, specific P addresses or names. It is super easy and super cool. I really like this a lot. But yeah, that is just a normal DNS query. Now, if you want to have a little more protection for your DNS queries, as I said in the beginning, Adgot is by default using a DNS over HTTPS encryption. This is honestly the most powerful feature of Adgot. And this is where it really shines in comparison to Pi Hole. So, let's dive a little deeper into this. First of all, let me explain what these technologies are and why they matter. So, traditionally DNS queries are always sent in plain text over the internet on port 53. Anyone in between you and the DNS server, your ISP, your mobile carrier, even some random person on the Wi-Fi network you're on can exactly see what domains you're looking up. Yeah. And this is of course a huge privacy problem when your ISP can look up all of your requests. It can basically build a complete profile on your browsing habits just by watching the DNS queries. It doesn't need to encrypt the connection between the browser and the real web server. It can just do that by the DNS queries. So they can literally see every website you visit, when you visit it, how often you visit it and so on. Now that's why we came up with DNS over HTTPS or DNS over TLS to solve this problem by just encrypting DNS queries. Now it's important to understand the difference between the two. DNS over TLS DOT wraps all the DNS queries in TLS encryption typically on a separate port 853 which is a dedicated port just for encrypted DNS and there's also DNS over HTTPS do which sends the DNS queries just over the normal HTTPS port for for free which is the same one that is as used for secure web browsing which makes it effectively harder to block or detect because you cannot distinguish the DNS queries from other real browsing activity. So both technologies provide encryption but do has become the more popular choice because of that reason. It's also important to say that there are two places where DNS encryption can happen and they serve different purposes. The scenario one is this year. This is the default. When you configure Atgot home in your home lab and you configured your router to send your client via DHCP a different IP for the DNS server, your client will still make a plain unencrypted DNS lookup to the ATCO home server on port 53, but then the Adgot will send this to the upstream server by using DNS over HTTPS on port 4 for free. So that means your ISP in between cannot distinguish that traffic from real browsing activity. It's just seeing oh there is a new HTTPS connection port for for free to the Cloudflare server but they don't really see what DNS query you are making. Now this of course is by default out of the box a super secure setup. However, you can even take that a step further by also encrypting the local DNS queries over HTTPS. So that means your client will not send the DNS queries in plain text to Adgot. You can also send them over the HTTP port. And when you're using Adgot in combination with a traffic reverse proxy, you can also use the trusted TLS certificates on the port for free and just send the DNS requests to that traffic reverse proxy. It will forward it to at home. From there, it goes to the upstream DNS server. So the entire connection from your local device over your local network to your atgot home and from there is completely encrypted which is really great and in my opinion the most secure setup. Now if you go to the settings here if you want to enable this you need to go to settings encryption settings and enable encryption here. However there is a small problem that I encountered. You cannot really save this configuration because it's asking you for a certificate and this is because by default the atgot home server would normally uh understand that this is the terminating connections. So um it's it doesn't know that there is a traffic reverse proxy in front of it and usually you would have to send the DNS queries to the HTTPS part of the ATCOT home server and the ATCOT home server would terminate the TLS connection with a trusted TLS certificate that you need to somehow obtain uh upload to the ATC's home server and put in the path here with the private key. Now, this of course was a bit of a bummer when I saw it the first time, but luckily I found a way to overcome this, but it's a little hidden. You don't find it in the menu. Yeah, you just leave on the defaults and you need to go into the main configuration yl file of atgot home. So, this you can easily do by just uh going to the uh server where you have deployed atgardome and then run a super simple uh temporary container. For example, the netshoot container. This is a utility container that comes with a lot of network uh tools, but it also has a vim editor and a nano editor on board and is super simple uh efficient uh container where you can just hook into a container automatically mount the volumes from an existing container and open a bash shell. Uh okay. Uh probably I need to use a different name here because I made a typo in the beginning of this tutorial. All right. Anyway, so I will just replace it with this. Yeah. And then it will work. So now we are inside the atgot home container. And if you go into the /opt addg guard home conf directory, you will find the main add.l file. If you open this, you see this is the entire configuration of your system. And there are two sections that are really important. There is a TLS section where you need to enable TLS. And then there is a setting that is called allow unencrypted do. Set this to true. So then the traffic reverse proxy will receive the DNS query and it will send it to the port 80 of your ATC home server. And by setting this it will accept it. Now there's one other problem that I saw in the locks of the queries. You will not see the real client IP address because a client is sending it to the traffic reverse proxy. only traffic will see the client's IP address and it need to forward it to the ATC got home server otherwise ATC got home will just see the internal IP address of the traffic container because this is a standard reverse proxy uh flag that is sent in the HTTP request you need to enable this setting here trusted proxy so that the atcot home server trusts the connection from the traffic container you can easily do this by just adding all of the docker internal networks uh by using the 726000/12 subnet masks. So this is like all of the docker local internal networks and then uh you can exit this container. You just need to restart your atgot home server. So just do a docker compos up abdu for with force recreate or stop the container redeploy it. Uh you can do it either way. And then uh when the container has restarted and we refresh the page, this setting here is enabled and we don't need a certificate. All right. So now we can send a DNS over HTTPS queries uh from our local device to the Atcot home server. I will just demonstrate how that would work. You would need to go in your operating system settings. I think Windows no supports do. I don't really know because I haven't used a Windows device in the last two or three years anymore. But I know it's there somewhere and I hope Mac OS probably also does it. But I can also use a simple terminal command. I will use Dogo. This is a modern Rust uh DNS troubleshooting tool. Of course, I will again use the Tinder example here. And the query you need to send is at https at home and then /dns query. You see that the response immediately comes back with 0000. So that means the adcut home receives it and answers it with a black hole response. And when we go back to the UI, you should also see in the query lock blocked service tinder.com. So in comparison of the plain DNS query, you see that this is definitely working over the encrypted connection. And yeah, this is how that magic all works. You can of course test this with other pages. For example, Christian.e should immediately work. Uh because yeah, that's fine. And you see this in the query log. All right. So next the only thing that's left to do configure your router configure a DHCP server send all the DNS queries to adgard home and for all the devices where you want to encrypt the connection inside your local network configure DO on the operating system of your device or you can do it in the settings of your browser and configure the devices to send the DNS queries via DO to the adgards home DNS name and then you have completely encrypted DNS queries with a built-in at and tracker blocking, parental controls, custom filtering, block lists.
