=== Is the UGREEN NAS DH4300 Plus Your Next NAS? ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l8tP51HbDcE

Hey everybody, this is Christian and today I'm reviewing the UG Green NAS DH4300 Plus. It's a new value series device in the Ugra Nest family, so it comes with a few limitations for power users, but it is a pretty interesting deal for smaller environments because it has an efficient ARM CPU, a lot of memory, and some cool features like AI powered photo recognition, Docker support, and HDMI output for direct 4K playback. And honestly, I think the design is pretty slick. It fits perfectly on any desk without taking too much space because the engineers found a clever way of mounting hard drives. And because UG sent me this device for testing, today I'm giving you my honest and humble opinion on it. And more importantly, who I think this device is actually good for. So, let's get started. All right. So, now let's take a closer look at the hardware specs and more specifically on the disc layout because I think this is where UGRine did something different instead of traditional front loading trays. Because with the DH4300 plus, you easily slide in the drives vertically from the top. You just lift off the magnetic top cover where you get access to all of the four drive bays. By the way, each base supports either 3.5 in or 2.5 in SATA drives with a maximum capacity of 120 terabytes. So, four times 30 terbte drives. And of course, this NAS device supports multiple RAID configurations for redundant storage like Jbot, RAID zero, RAID 1, RAID 5, RAID six, and RAID 10. I personally would recommend for most home users or HomeLab nerds, RAID 5 because there you get the capacity of three hard drives. You got some decent performance and protections against one drive failure. But alternatively, you could also set up something different like grade 10, which gives you the best performance, but it also cuts your capacity in half. With a Ukraine nest, you got all the choices and it basically depends on your personal priorities. So, what matters more to you, speed, capacity, or redundancy? What's also interesting about this NAS, the heart of it is the Rock Chip RK 3588 processor, which is an 8 core ARM CPU with a hybrid architectures. It has four Cortex A76 cores, so these are the powerful ones, and four Cortex R55 cores, the more efficient ones. And yes, because of the ARM architecture, you lose some advanced features you might be missing from this NAS like you cannot run any virtual machines on it. You don't have ice cassi support and you don't get any 10 GB Ethernet or Thunderbolt ports. But for a small home nest, I personally think most people don't need that anyway. And on the other side, you have some advantages like you get low power consumption. I personally measured around 6 to 7 watts on idle even with all the drives plugged in. So when they're shut down so that you can just safely run this 24/7 and you don't have to care about power consumption at all. It also produces low heat and it quietly operates. And in the ARM space it is actually a very powerful device. Yeah, the clock speed of this CPU is around 2 GHz and it even boosts up to 2.4 GHz which is pretty impressive for an ARMbased NAS and it even comes with 8 GB of DDR4X memory. And yes, it's soldered on the board, so it's not upgradeable, but honestly, 8 GB is plenty for what this device is designed to do. And you still get one USBC port on the front, two USBA ports on the back, and a 2.5 GB Ethernet port, and also an HDMI port that supports 4K at 60 Hz where you can connect this directly to a TV or monitor. Plus, the operating system on this device runs from a dedicated 32 GB eMMC module, which means that all the data drives stay completely free for just storage. So, I think this is pretty decent. If you compare this to other competitors, for example, like the Synology DS 423 Plus that costs around like $4, $500, that only comes with a quadcore ARM CPU and only 2 GB of memory and just 1 GB Ethernet. or the QAP TS433 is around like $400 and it also has only a quad core ARM CPU, 4 GB of memory, and a 2.5 GB Ethernet port. So, at the end of the day, if you just compare the hardware specs of the UG Green DS4,300 plus, it's giving you more cores, more memory, and the same or better network speeds. And by the way, UG Green still has a Black Friday sale with about 25% discount where you can get this device for around $340. So that's why I personally think it's a really great deal. Of course, you will find a link to the UG's website and their offering in the description box down below. But of course, when we are reviewing this device, we cannot just compare the pure hardware specs because we also should take a look at the software. So, the device is running the UGR's proprietary NAS operating system, UGOS. And I honestly haven't found any instructions to flash the internal eMMC with a different system, probably because most systems won't support ARM anyways. So, I don't believe that you can easily install any other operating system of your choice, which might be a downside for some HomeLab people. But, I can say the Ukraine's operating system is pretty nice. It has a clean browser-based intuitive system that is super easy to use. The initial setup is also pretty easy and it of course has many many features for file management and sharing. It supports all the standard protocols like SMB, NFS and you can easily create folders with setting granular permissions, create user accounts, create groups and quotas and you can even set up share links with password protections and expiry dates so you can easily share data to somebody else. And it also has some clever backup options like automatic photo and video backup from your smartphone. It has scheduled backups from your PC or Mac. It even supports USB backup. So you just plug in an external hard drive and copy the files directly. And you can also synchronize to Google Drive, Dropbox, etc. And even supports NAST to nest replication for off-site backups. So I think that should fit most of the needs for a small home lab NAS. There's one thing that is pretty special about this device and this is the AI powered photo management. So, this is where the NPU in the RK 3588 chip comes into play because this allows the UGR NAS to do facial recognition. So, it can learn your faces and group photos automatically. I've just tested this with a few examples and that seems to work pretty great. And it also supports object and scene recognitions. So, if you upload your holiday photos and you search for beach or forest or something, yeah, it automatically finds all these photos and it also detects duplicate photos. And of course, it also has many multimedia functions like built-in apps for photos, music, and video. It has DNA, UPN media server, the HDMI output with theater mode where you can directly connect this to a TV and browse the media supporting 4K playback at 60 Hz. And if you want to stream videos or files to other devices, you can even install some small media Docker containers because yes, this device also fully support Docker, which I think is really great for home people. You can run any ARM compatible containers here like Portainer for example works great for container management and this really opens up a lot of possibilities like installing Home Assistant, installing Nexcloud or other media servers. Just remember that this is still an ARM architecture. So, make sure that the containers are ARM 64 compatible and you probably don't have a lot of memory to run an entire application server on this. But I think this is not really what this device is meant for. It's really a multimedia station with a solid and redundant storage. So, my honest take on the Ugree NAS DH4300 Plus is I think it's perfect for home users. their families, small offices, anyone who needs reliable storage without the complexity of the power consumption of bigger systems. Yes, it has some limitations. Yeah, it misses 10 GB Ethernet, no virtual machines, no icecazi. But let's be real, most people searching for a value series NAS device don't need that stuff anyways. And on the other side, you do get eight cores, 8 GB of memory, 2.5 GB Ethernet, Docker support, AI photo recognition, and an incredibly low power consumption. So, it's probably not the perfect device for everybody, especially power users probably want a little more. But for what it is, and for the price and the target audience, I think it's pretty hard to beat. But of course, you can now let me know in the comments what are your thoughts about this device. Do you like it? And as always, thank you so much for watching. Thank you so much to all the supporters of my channel. You guys are really amazing. And I'm going to catch you in the next video.

=== MCP Tutorial: Connect AI to your HomeLab (and more…) ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AydDDYn09QA

Hey everybody, this is Christian and today I'd like to show you how to easily connect AI to your HomeLab DevOps workflows and many more systems using MCP, the model context protocol. And guys, this is a true gamecher. I've also been pretty skeptical about integrating AI into my home lab and tech workflows because sure GPT can write you some code, answer questions, but when it comes to actually doing something useful in your infrastructure like accessing technical documentation, maintaining Git repositories, or evaluating your automation workflows, most AI assistants are pretty much useless because they just don't have access to your systems, your infrastructure, and your knowledge. But MCP changes this. It's like giving AI the ability to see and interact with your entire tech stack. And the best part, MCP is an open protocol. And there's already a massive ecosystem of communitybuilt servers for nearly every tool out there that you can think of like Proxmox, Terraform, Cloudflare, N8N, GitHub, Docker, and some of them. And of course, also how I'm using them in my day-to-day workflow I'd like to show you in this video. Now, before we start, if you want to work with MCPS and AI and you just need a tool or program to use it, I'd like to show you my absolute favorite tool that I honestly use every single day for AI coding, but also throughout all of my videos. It's called Warp, the sponsor of today's video, a fully agentic development environment that unites CLI and IDE features into one single application. You can use Warp as your main CLI program. Use the built-in AI agent to autonomously create and execute commands, analyze your CLI output to troubleshoot and fix issues, and you can even write code that is tailored to your codebase and build, ship, and deploy sophisticated applications. It's absolutely fantastic. For example, I'm currently developing my own CLI program called boiler plates where I heavily used warp for agentic AI coding to write large parts of the codebase and it helps me to fix bugs and issues, review the code and maintain GitHub issues with the built-in MCP server support. There are tons and tons of features in WP to make your life as a developer or tech nerd easier. And if you haven't tried it out yet yourself, then you definitely should do right now because you can easily get started completely for free with no card required and no sign up needed. And if you like it and you need more AI tokens or access to some of their pro features, you can of course also upgrade to higher plans. I personally highly recommend it. Of course, I will put your link to it in the description box down below. All right, guys. So now let's get started with MCP server. So before I show you how the magic is working, I just want to recap what is actually the problem with dump AI assistance and how to fix this. So here I'm using Raycast, one of my other favorite tools that I'm using on Mac OS to work with several AI models. Some of them are very sophisticated just like the Claude 4.5 Sonnet. This is an absolutely fantastic AI model, but still this has many many flaws when it comes to realworld usage. If I'd like to create some Cloudflare Terraform resources to manage DNS records, I can ask the AI model to generate some example code. And as you can see here, it will immediately produce an output and some code examples. But if we take a closer look at these results from the AI model, you can clearly see that this is not using the updated resources and the latest versions of the Cloudflare Terraform provider because this comes from some old and legacy training data where the version 4 was the latest version of the Cloudflare's Terraform provider. But Cloudflare just did a massive refactoring of their Terraform provider and they use completely different names for the resource object. So you can hear it's Cloudflare DNS record. In our example from the AI model, it's just Cloudflare record. So the old legacy data and this will happen many many times when you need to use AI models for doing something actually useful on your infrastructure. Sure, you can iterate over this. You can now tell the AI here is the link of the documentation. Please use these examples. But there is a much easier way to do this because when I say can you use context 7 one of my MCP servers to check if this is actually correct it will gather the information from the technical documentation of the cloudflare's terapform provider completely by itself and it will update the examples to use the latest version 5 with the updated resource names and the correct syntax and all the parameters that it gathered from the official documentation. And when I know go on and say something like, "Hey, do you know where and how I'm managing my Cloudflare DNS records in my home lab?" Of course, the AI model doesn't have any information about my specific HomeLab setups or how I'm managing this in my environment. Of course, how should it? But when I say just try this again using my notion MCP server and my GitLab MCP server and say, "Hey, create a simple table for all the DNS records of Cloudflare that I have in my current environment." It can gather this information by using my technical documentation platform and my git repositories to provide the actual results from my production system. Now the AI model suddenly became a much more useful assistant and I could even now delegate entire configuration tasks or changing tasks to AI assistants using MCP server. This is absolutely fascinating. All right. So now of course you want to know how this magic is happening. What is MCP and how does it actually work? First, a few theoretical things. So, MCP stands for model context protocol and it was created by Anthropic, the company behind CLA. So, the AI model that we've just used and they built it because large language models, not just Claude, but also GPT, Gemini or basically any other AI assistants, they are all trained on data up to a certain point in time. So they are frozen in the past and they don't know about your specific infrastructure, your private documentation or the latest updates to all the tools that you're using. And sure, you could try to solve this with something like RA, retrieval, augmented generation where you feed the AI some context from your documentation, but this is also pretty limited because the AI can read information, but it can't actually do anything with it. It can't make any API calls. It can't execute commands. It can't interact with your systems. That's why it needs additional tools and where MCP comes in. So, think of MCP like a universal translator for AI. Some people call it the USB standard for AI models. Yeah. So, your AI speaks one language, the tools and the services speak another. And MCP servers are somehow the translator between these elements and they let them communicate and work together. First, you have your user that's sending a prompt to an AI client, which can be a program like the warp terminal or raycast. And then this AI client will send an MCP request to the MCP server. And the MCP server will then send an API request or any other form of communication to the end system. And it uses tool discovery to tell the AI client, hey, what are the functions and the parameters of all of the tools that it has access to. And the AI client later on can figure out on its own when it needs to call what particular function with what parameters to execute the user's request. The good news is that there is already a massive and growing ecosystem of MCP servers out there. First of all, many many companies are currently building official MCP servers for their own products. For example, Anthropic, the company behind MCP. They have built servers for things like file system access or GitHub, Google Drive. So, these are well-maintained, well doumented MCP servers and usually the best option. But of course, there are also many community maintained or community developed MCP servers for basically every tool and every service you can imagine. Yeah. And there is an awesome GitHub repository that catalogs some of them. I just want to show you this. This is called awesome MCP servers. And here you find a long long list of many different MCP servers organized in categories. So you can better find uh stuff that you're looking for. For example, if you search for context 7, you can find this here the update code documentation for LLMs and AI code editors. That's what we've just used in the raycast prompt. So just click on something that interests you and that will usually take you to the git repository of the um MCP servers developer and then you probably will find some instructions on how to add this to your AI client. But of course you don't find everything in here. Yeah. So first of all, I would check out the official MCP server by going to the vendor's website and find out if there is any officially maintained MCP server. And only if you don't find something, then you can search for community websites. Check out GitHub to find something. For example, the GitLab MCP server that I'm using in Raycast is not the official one because for the official one you need to pay for. But of course, there's also a community-maintained MCP server. That's the one that I'm using. No, it doesn't have all of the features that GitLab offers, but as this is a community-maintained project, you never really know how good it is and how well it works with your AI agent or your platform. So, be careful when using community projects. So, again, depending on what AI client you're using, the configuration and the setup might differ. uh but because it's a standard protocol they uh follow some kind of standard configurations and uh communication methods we will later take a closer look at this but just to show you how that's working in warp. So when you open the sidebar the warp drive you have something here that's called MCP servers and that opens up the settings menu you can also reach it from the general settings menu and then you can click on add. So this will open a JSON object where you put in your configuration your authentication tokens if the MCP server needs. Some of them don't need an API token such as context 7 you can use with or without an API token. But of course if you want to access systems that need some form of authentication you of course need to add your API token into the environment variables or arguments. So here for example if I want to use the npx command I can just copy this paste it here. I probably don't need the API key so I will just remove it and then click on save. And then you should see context 7 in your list here. Uh it should automatically start in warp but if not you can click on stop or start. And in raycast you can also very easily install this when you go to install server and then you need to put in uh the name for the MCP server. Use the standard input output format or HTTP depending on what the MCP server requires. and then put in the command and the arguments and the API key if you need. So, okay, so before I want to show you some other examples how you could use this, I uh still want to talk a little about the MCP server execution modes because you might have noticed in the context 7 documentation that there are different ways of executing MCP servers. So, the ones that are the most common ones are NPX execution mode. So, you might have seen this here uh when the command says npx this is using Node.js JS and supports auto updates. It's super easy to use. It just runs a simple program on your local device and it already has all the dependencies and functions in it and you configure it using environment variables. But there are also other MCP servers. You might need to start as a Docker container. So that um has some advantages of providing an isolated environment through the Docker engine. It has also easy dependency management and configured the same way. know that of course requires you to install a docker host or docker desktop or maybe stack on your local device to get this MCP server up and running. For example, here context 7 you can see in my warp terminal is using npx but the GitHub one for example is using a docker command. So you can run a different MCP servers with different technologies doesn't actually matter. And there are also some others that might use any executable command on your device. So this might be a Python script, a bash script or whatever. It's also using standard out and environment variables, but there's also a new category of MCP execution modes. It's using HTTP. And this um is a remote execution. So there the program is not running on your local device. It is running somewhere else in a cloud environment or on a provider. And that offers you to offload the processing to a different service. you communicate through HTTP or HTTPS and it also supports some other authentication alternatives. So sometimes you even don't need an API token. Uh you can use OOTH 2 to authenticate to the actual application that for example the notion MCP server does it like this. So the key thing here to understand is that the first three methods run on your local machine and the last one is just sending out web requests to a remote server. But the protocol is actually the same. So you can connect all with any AI model or AI client that supports MCP servers no matter if it is using local AI models or cloud hosted AI models. That's all depending on the client implementation of the MCP protocol. Now enough theory. Let's uh open my warp terminal and let's go through some practical use cases because I'm heavily using the GitHubs MCP server. As some of you might know, I've recently started my own CLI project where I need to program my own tool that you can use to create battle tested templates for Docker, Terraform, Anible, Kubernetes, and some others. === How to run Traefik in Docker Swarm? ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zg-rL7n_t-A

Hey guys, this is Christian and in this video I'm going to show you how to run traffic, my absolute favorite reverse proxy and load balancer in Docker Swarm. This is the setup for production ready high available load balancing with trusted TLS certificates for all of your applications in Docker Swarm. But I have to be honest with you guys, it wasn't an easy task because when I first tried to combine traffic in Docker Swarm, I ran into some practical challenges. for example, how do you handle and maintain a consistent configuration across all of the different nodes or how do you store tier certificates and also what happens when the node where traffic is running goes down. Today I'm going to show you how exactly I've solved all of these problems following two different approaches. So, one that is a bit simpler and in my opinion perfect for smaller home labs and setups using local Docker volumes and another one that is truly production ready with zero single points of failure using shared storage. Plus, to make your life easier, I've built all of this using my new boiler plates CLI tool so you can generate everything, the Docker Compost stack and all of the traffic configuration files with proper security settings in just a few commands. Now before we jump right in, I also want to mention that if you're managing containers, clusters, and maybe other infrastructure services in your environment, it is also important to monitor all of these systems and get notified about any bottlenecks or problems. So that's why you definitely should use Czechm, the sponsor of today's video. Czechm is a comprehensive IT monitoring platform that is scalable, automated, and highly extensible. With over 2,000 maintained plugins, it can monitor nearly all of your network components across all the different manufacturers, and it has many advanced capabilities like auto discovery with preconfigured thresholds and rules, custom plugins. The features are just insane. And furthermore, it creates stunning visualizations, has a dynamic dashboard with logs [music] and event monitoring that really allows you to drill into all of the details and get sophisticated alerts and notifications. It is really super cool. I'm personally using the free and open source raw edition of Czechm in my home lab which I already did some videos on. So check them out if you like. And if you want to use Czechm for your company's infrastructure to monitor your production systems, they have many options to deploy a self-hosted solution or use their cloud service offerings. Of course, I will put your link to Czechm and my tutorials [music] in the description box down below. All right, so before we start setting up traffic in Docker Swarm, let me quickly recap what we've done in the past and what are some of the challenges that I needed to solve. So a few videos back, I showed you how to set up traffic on a single Docker instance. So we got TL certificates for HTTP and HTTPS reverse proxying for local applications and services. And we also covered how to store the TLA certificates in a persistent Docker volume as well as mounting the configuration files and connecting let's encrypt and it really worked great. Yeah, for a single server of course. So then in another video I showed you how to set up a Docker Swarm cluster. So that brings high availability and some other advanced capabilities. But we are also facing a few challenges with this because as you might have learned from my Docker Swarm tutorial, local Docker volumes are only persistent on that one Docker host where they are created and they are not automatically replicated in the cluster such as the configs or secrets are. So that's a problem we need to solve. And also what actually happens when we deploy traffic on one of the Docker hosts and that one Docker host goes down. How can we make sure that there is always a traffic instance on each docker host running? So we will also take a closer look at that. To tackle these challenges, there are two different possible setups. So there is one with local docker volumes on a single node. We're just deploying a traffic instance on one of the docker hosts and we can use the docker overlay network to connect that one traffic instance to any application in the entire cluster. That is really a great setup in smaller home lab environments where you might not have a shared storage volume. The only downside this setup has is when the node one goes down, so the node where traffic is running, we can't access any other applications in our cluster even if they might be running on the other two remaining nodes. So if you really want to have a reliable and solid production ready setup, you have to do it this way. You need a shared volume on all of the nodes. And that for example you can achieve with an NFS server. So that can be a truness or an unrated server that is somewhere running outside of the docker cluster where you switch the deployment mode of your stack from replicated to global. So that means that the docker swarm cluster will make sure that each of the docker node has one instance of that container running. So that [snorts] traffic is running on all of the hosts and when one of these goes down the applications might be restarted on the other remaining nodes but the traffic instance is always up and running. So these are the two deployment methods that at least in my humble opinion make the most sense. It is up to you what you plan to use. If you want to have a more sophisticated setup for true high availability or if you are okay in your small home lab that you might need to restart a docker host that is again up to you and how complicated you want this setup to be. Now, if we take a look at the traffic documentation, honestly guys, I'm not the biggest friend of the traffic documentation because there's a lot of different fragmented areas. It has improved uh a lot in the past. Yeah, don't get me wrong. But of course, it is a quite complicated setup with many different things you need to keep in mind. And traffic in general is in my opinion not a really intuitive solution. So it really takes some time to get up to speed and to understand all the things that you need to set up and how they work in combination, especially on Docker Swarm. So that's why I thought instead of just going through this documentation, I'm going to create my own boiler plate that is like a template that contains all of the best practices and things that we're covering in my tutorial. And you can use my newly created boilerplate CLI tool to easily create a docker compos template for traffic running on a single node server or on docker swarm uh using the one or another deployment method. It is completely up to you. And that is uh the reason for this is that I've set or configured the DNS name for who am I.Crave. My local DNS server to only resolve to the first IP address. So then we still get a connection from at least one of these IP addresses where traffic is running. Now this way you can have a reliable and truly high available deployment of traffic reverse proxy on docker swarm. Now, there's still one more thing that I need to address at the end. And these are the final thoughts or my ideas for future videos because this might introduce uh some problems if it still resolves the IP address of a node that is down and it still tries to connect to that IP address. Now in our case we are just picking a different IP address but if it would connect to this IP address we would still run into a timeout and uh it might uh take some time until the client tries to connect to a different IP address and that still is a problem. So although the traffic reverse proxy setup is truly high available, our Docker Swarm cluster is not. This requires an an additional video about keeper lifed because I think otherwise it would be too much for this one tutorial. However, I still hope that it helped you to get a reliable and truly high level setup of traffic running in Docker Swarm. And if you really want to make this production ready, you can now use an external load balancer to check if these nodes are up and running and route the incoming client requests to the online servers or you're using a floating IP address with something like keeperd. But again, we'll cover that in a future video. All right, guys. So, now it's your turn. Please tell me, did you like this video? Did it help you? And what do you think about the boilerplate CLI tool? By the way, I would really love to hear your opinion about this. And if you feel this is a promising project that helps you to get up to speed quickly. And then also consider supporting this or at least give it a star on GitHub. That always helps. And thank you so much for watching. Thanks a lot to the people supporting this project already. And of course, I'm going to catch you in the next video tutorial. So, have a nice day, guys. Take care.

=== Block stupid Ads and manage DNS // Pi-hole Tutorial ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e2QgG0Z8KqY

Hey guys, it's Christian and today we're taking a look at how to block stupid ads and manage DNS in your home network with Pi Hole. I just recently deployed this in my home network. And guys, I can just tell you Pi Hole is absolutely incredible. It's an easy to use networkwide ad and internet tracker blocker that protects all of your devices in the network from unwanted content without having to install anything on the client devices itself. So that means it works on all of your computers, all of your laptops, your phones, and even smart TVs. And it is super small and efficient. This thing runs on any device, just like a small Raspberry Pi, but of course also on all of your Linux- based HomeLab servers. And it provides you an easy to use web interface with statistics about all of the DNS queries with DNS configuration options and even DHCP functionality. And the best, it is free and open source. So, in this video, I'm going to show you how to easily deploy Pi Hole to your Docker host or Swarm cluster with optional traffic reverse proxy integration so you can get rid of any ads and trackers for all of the devices in your entire network. I think that sounds pretty interesting, right? So, let's take a look together. Before we do that though, I quickly like to show you how you can easily manage all of your tech and app projects because if you're juggling around with different tasks and projects like me at the same time, you can lose track pretty fast. So, that's why you should definitely use monday.com, the intuitive all-in-one work platform where you can collaborate efficiently with a team, manage complex projects, and even automate repetitive tasks. You can easily create to-do lists with next actions that you're planning, or create custom fields with additional notes, manage priorities or plan timelines. If you know me, guys, I absolutely love the Canv style. So, that gives you a perfect overview of all of this stuff that's going on. But of course, you can also do some pretty advanced stuff like connecting different boards and adding dependencies between the [music] tasks. It is really super cool. And of course, it doesn't only work for your personal projects. Actually, it gets more interesting to use such a platform when you're managing projects in a team. You can start with a free plan that gets you up to two seats with free boards, unlimited docs, and eight column types, which is great for smaller projects. But you can also upgrade to other plans to increase the features and capabilities of monday.com to automate coordinating emails, sending out files and Slack messages, and organize your team's projects and collaborate effectively. To easily get started, check out monday.com. Of course, I've put your link to this in the description box down below. All right. So now let's get started with Pi Hole. First of all, it might be interesting for some of you to know how it actually works. And to understand this, we need to talk a little about DNS and DNS black holes. If you're already familiar with how DNS works, then bear with me for a moment here because I just need to make sure that everybody is on the same page. But I'll make it quick. Yeah. So DNS, the domain name system, is essentially the phone book of the internet. So when you type in a website's address in your browser, like Christian. Your device doesn't actually know where this website lives and which server to connect to. So, it needs to convert that human readable name into an IP address to establish a connection. And here is how that normally works. Your device just sends out a DNS request, a DNS query on port 53 to your DNS server. That is usually the DNS server that gets configured by your DHCP server on the network which might either be provided by your ISP or by a public DNS service like Cloudflare or Google depending on what you have configured. Then that server responds with the IP address of that website and the device connects to that address to load it and this happens constantly in the background. Yeah. for every website that you visit. And every website itself might contain additional elements from other sources as well that need to be resolved, including legitimate ones like images or scripts that are loaded from other locations. But also, every tracker, every advertisement block usually has its own DNS name because it's coming from a different server than the original website. And your devices are making hundreds or thousands of DNS queries every single day to load every part of the website that is included. And now here comes the interesting point where Pi Hole jumps in. Because when you configure Pi Hole as your primary DNS server, it starts intercepting all these DNS queries, Pi Hole checks the requested domain against its blacklist. So which is basically just a massive database of known advertising and tracking domains. And if the domain is on such a block list, then Pi Hole doesn't forward that query to an upstream DNS server. Instead, it responds immediately with a null address, typically 0.0.0.0, which is also called a black hole, where the original DNS query from your device disappears into a black hole. So, basically telling your device, hey, this domain doesn't exist or it points to nowhere. Now, this sounds really great as it would be the best option to block any ads and trackers. And yeah, it is a great technology. However, the crucial thing to understand here is that this happens at the very beginning of the network processing. So even before any HTTPS or HTTP connection from your device is established. So Pi Hole is making the blocking decisions based purely on the domain names not on the actual content that's being served. And this actually comes with a few advantages. Yeah. Such as speed improvements because your device is making less requests and less connections to load a website. You will by the way notice that some websites even load faster than before. However, it also has a few downsides because first of all, these lists need to be highly accurate and of course up to-date. Otherwise, you will run into false positives or false negatives where some elements of the website don't actually load or some advertisements aren't blocked because that domain isn't updated on these public lists. But it also is not able to block everything because bigger services like YouTube, a very prominent example, will load trackers and advertisements from the exact same domain as the rest of the content, which is the main reason why Pi Hole isn't able to block advertisements in YouTube videos. So, it isn't a silver bullet for everything, but I think it's blocking like 90 95% of all the ads of any websites that I've at least seen. So it is better than nothing to have pi hole in place. Now excuse me for the long introduction but I thought it might be interesting to know how the technology works uh to get a better understanding of the capabilities and also the limitations of this program and then you can make a better decision if you want to use it or not. But just like I said in the beginning, I recently deployed this in my home lab network because I thought it might be good to have some better statistics and some better insights into the DNS queries and literally just have more control about what's going on. And of course, it's also blocking 95% of the stupid ads and trackers without having to install anything on all of my devices. So, you actually don't need any browser extension like ad block or something like that. And you can just install this thing on nearly every device that's running in your home lab. I think if you're watching or following this channel, you're pretty familiar with how that actually works. You might have a HomeLab server in place. Maybe you're just starting out and you want to run this thing on a small Raspberry Pi that's just running 24/7. Uh but yeah, uh you mainly have all the options to deploy this anywhere you want. You can see that the advertisement blocks are still there, but there is no element loaded. And if we return back to Pi Hole, you can see that 77 queries have been blocked just by opening this one website. Yeah, just imagine you're browsing through the web. So, let's take a closer look at the Pi Holes interface. It's actually kind of interesting uh what happens here. You can see the total queries, the queries blocked, the percentage blocked, and the domains on the list. So, there are over 100,000 uh domains on the block list. You can also manage them here in the lists menu, by the way. So currently there's only one list that's the Steven blacklist. So that's probably the most prominent blacklist uh that's communitydriven and public. But uh you can also add more lists if you want. Uh but you can also add domains manually using a domain filter or a regular expression if you want to disable the blocking feature if you encounter any problems with specific websites or things that are not working. So you can further troubleshoot. Now one thing that might be interesting to understand is what actually happens to the valid requests. So where are they sent to? Because we just changed the primary DNS server to Pi Hole. But where does Pi Hole send the valid requests to? So that you can configure in the settings when you go to DNS and here you can configure multiple upstream servers. So by default it is set to Google but you can also change this to Cloudflare enabling DNS sec or to quad 9 uh filtered by the way which will also filter out um any harmful websites malwares and stuff like that. This is also by the way a super interesting service. This is an open DNS recursive service for free security and high privacy. This is a nonprofit organization that gets it block list from various security vendors that provide their insights to quad 9 and they provide this free service. So then your DNS queries are being sent from your device to Pi Hole and the valid ones are being sent to these upstream DNS servers. Now you probably noticed that I did not configure this on my production DNS server. And the reason for this is that I still need uh my other authorative DNS server, the bind 9 server for the local homelab uh domain resolution. So that is when I need to connect to some of my servers like server one.home.ccrave.de or also my other domains um that my servers or services are using. So that's the reason why I configured the upstream DNS server to my open sense firewall and from there it's going to be sent out to the other upstream DNS server. So I kind of have added open sense into that DNS query chain. Don't be afraid of adding too many DNS uh servers in the chain that it's going to hurt your performance or something like this because DNS queries are very efficient. they are very fast and they're also being cached on all of the DNS servers. So don't be afraid of um losing any performance when you're adding too many elements in the chain. But yeah, this is how you usually can build your DNS query chain depending on what devices or servers you need in your environment. But if you don't have a bind DNS server, if you don't have an open sense firewall and you just want a super simplified setup, you can also use Pi Hole for adding static DNS entries. If you go to local DNS records. So here for example you can add a records or CNAME records. Uh for example if you have like server pro one dotome.crave.de and you need to resolve this to 10.20.2 or 192168 whatever IP address you have. You can add these entries to Pi hole. And if this becomes your primary DNS server, you can even manage simple DNS entries with that. However, I'm personally not a friend of this. As you might know, I'm using Bind 9 uh as my authorative DNS server because it has a lot more features and is more capable as an internal DNS service. I really just use Pi Hole as a DNS black hole for any ads and trackers and that's it, right? But just let you know, you can do all these things with Pi Hole if you want. You can even manage DHCP settings here. You need to specifically enable this, which I would not recommend you to do if you have a second DHCP server in your network. It will cause problems. So, just do this when you exactly know what you're doing. But you can do it if you want. === CLI vs GUI, what is better? ===

Hey everybody, this is Christian. Today I've got a hot topic for you. It's about CLI apps versus GUI apps. So terminal applications versus graphical applications. What's better? So, in this video, we'll unpack some of the fundamental differences between CLI and GOI apps, explore some of the strengths and weaknesses of each, and I will show you my workflow with some very specific tools and examples where I believe the one or another is better for. Of course, take this with a grain of salt here because it is highly subjective. Yeah. So, feel free to disagree with my tool choices or even with my entire approach at all. I don't really care. But hopefully it helps some of you guys to find some inspiration or maybe understand a bit better how to efficiently and effectively work with graphical or terminal applications. Before we start with my favorite CLI tools, let me quickly show you one of my absolute favorite graphical tools, AIFlow, the sponsor of today's video. Because AIFlow simplifies your entire project and time management by combining time blocking and integrations into one intuitive platform. This is really great for somebody like me who is working on many different projects at the same time. And what is also pretty cool in Echiflow, it has an AI co-pilot that really learns your habits and helps you to auto assign tasks to save time. And it is also very flexible. You can customize many things in the platform, add morning rituals or cool down reviews. So, if you'd like to boost your productivity and organize your tasks and projects, then definitely check out Echiflow. It's a great tool and of course, I'll put your link to it in the description box down below. All right, guys. So, now let's get started. As you can see, I've already fired up my terminal program because first of all, I want to explain why so many tech people prefer CLI applications over graphical applications. And the main reason for this is that CLI applications represent some form of direct communication with a system. So there is much less abstraction between you as the user and the system or the actions that you want to perform. So when you're typing in commands, it is a very precise instruction what you actually want to achieve and you usually get an immediate and less filtered feedback. And that has several advantages. Like in my project directory, I've stored many different projects that are docker compos files. And for example, I want to find out in each of the compost files where I'm using traffic labels. Yeah, in a graphical application, I would have to go to this directory. I would have to go into each project. I would have to find where is a compose file actually existing. So here probably I have to open this application and oh yeah there's no traffic label in here. So I can go to the next one in a terminal with powerful Linux tools. I could just use grap for example to search for a text in a directory and it's automatically searching all the subdirectories finding all of the files in here and listing out the files where this text or the search pattern is located in. This is super super powerful and you can even combine these tools when you pipe this to another command like for example if you want to count how many occurrences there are. I can just pipe this to a w that will count uh all of the objects. So 25 objects and I can even go on and for example search for this and just include the file name and instead of piping this to a counter I can pipe this to X arrs which is a small tool that takes the output of the first command as an argument to pass it to another command. For example, I could use an sedd command to replace the existing traffic enable true and replace it with traffic enable false for example. So I can replace with one single command any occurrences of this text here in any of the files of the subdirectory with something else. So you can see if you would do this in a graphical program this probably would take me like 20 30 minutes. what I can easily do within just a few seconds in one single command. And for example, I can also show the output of all the files. If I pipe this to XRX bat, for example, which will print the output of the entire text file, so where the search pattern is found. And when you learn to use and combine these terminal tools and programs, you're just much much faster and more direct when you want to make changes or you want to work with any system. By the way, this is also another great example where the CLI shines and that is when managing remote servers. This is really where the efficiency and the lightweight nature of CLI tools becomes absolutely critical. So when managing infrastructure, I'm just doing this uh at my local device. But when I want to do that at scale, you just need to use these type of tools. For example, if I just want to know what is the uptime of one of my servers, I could just execute this SSH uh uptime command on my remote machine and it's telling me uh okay, my server is up for 24 days. Probably need to restart it at some point. Or if I want to know what's the current um disk utilization, I could also just use the df-h command. So you can see it's much much faster, more direct instead of logging into an administrative program and then going through menus to find out what is the disk consumption of my servers. And of course what you can type into a single command can also easily scripted and repeated million times. Yeah, that's the real superpower of CLI. This is automation because once it is scripted you can schedule it you can have version control you can test it and you can share it with your team. I've also uh giving you some examples here for let's go to my anible directory. Uh by the way anible this is a powerful automation and configuration management tool that's uh really interesting because it uses YAML based language for their playbooks. For example, here is a playbook um that I have created which will um query all of the running containers on my servers and will output some of these statistics about it. Yeah. And if I execute it, it will automatically connect to my remote servers. It will gathering all of the uh statistics and querying all of the containers. It will give me the output of all the existing containers. Here you can see some of the details. So it's really really fast. Of course, that also comes at a price here. Yeah, I'm not telling you it is all great. Yeah, because uh in order to use these tools and to be fast and efficient with your terminal, um you just have to learn it. Yeah. So, the learning curve of many terminal programs might be a bit harder to climb at the first place because you need to memorize many commands. You need to understand the flags and all of the options. you need to learn the syntax and maybe shortcuts and so on. However, I think just in the recent years, this has become dramatically simpler because with the rise of AI, you can just describe what you want in a prompt and then AI translates this into the correct command syntax and that's ultimately making the CLI more accessible than ever. It dramatically lowers the barrier to entry while still giving you the full power of the CLI. I just will give you a very quick example here for so you can see um this is an output of the automation task where and here you can uh for example see that I usually don't set any memory or CPU uh utilization limits for my containers because honestly I don't think that's really needed here. But yeah, um, of course, I don't want to say that CLI tools and this way of working in your terminal is best for all use cases because before you think I'm a complete CLI fanatic, yeah, let me show you where graphical applications are actually superior. I think it comes from a dogmatic belief that CLI is always superior because it is so great to work with this but it is simply not true. So there are entire categories of problems even in tech where visual representation and graphical interaction provide fundamental advantages. Uh something that text based user interfaces just cannot provide especially when you need to understand relationships between different elements or visualize more complex data. The reason for this is that on most um deployments or most servers, the default SSH port 22 is most likely in use by the internal Open SSH server that is running on your server machine. It actually doesn't really matter. And when you create a project, you can clone this to your local device and uh then you can make changes on your local machine and push the changes to GitLab. and it's constantly maintaining a file history. So, GitLab always remembers every change you make in a single file line by line if you want. So, if you just change a single word in a line, it's memorizing there was a change in this one particular file and you could revert back to older versions. So, this is super super useful on my production instance. You can see I've created all of the different projects for all of the technologies or applications or things that I'm doing. So this is a very simple uh file that I'm storing here. However, I'm using GitLab to not only store this but also do automated updates. I will show you later how exactly that works. But if you're completely new to this, I would recommend you to create a new group. Call this home lab or you can create multiple groups for different environments or stuff like that. That also makes sense. And then start creating projects for each homelab project that you're doing. It doesn't have to be for every application. Um it could also be a project for monitoring stack uh where you collect deployment templates for graphana, Prometheus, Loki or stuff like that. But don't forget to spread them out a little and organize them in a single group. I will just show you another one for example authentic. So, so here I'm managing the deployment files which is uh basically helmchart values file and some kubernetes manifests for the ingress routes that's needed for traffic or the certificate uh definition and stuff like that and of course some terraform projects here. So there I'm managing uh the authentic applications the outpost the DNS records and I also created a gitlab CI. So this is an automated CI/CD pipeline. I will later show you how exactly that works. But the reason why I organize everything in one single home group is all these automated deployments um for example the Helm values in here will require some secrets. For example, there's a postgress server that requires a database password and authentic also requires a secret key that is used to encrypt all of the information in the database. And it is never really a good idea to store these type of secrets in plain text files. It's even worse to store them in a git managed repository because if you remember what I said earlier, git remembers all of the changes in a file. So even if you once added a secret or a password in here and then you delete it with an update, it's still stored in the git history and someone could still look it up. So that's really super important. Never store any credentials or secret variables in Git repositories. Super dangerous. No, what you should do instead is use the GitLab's integrated secret variables. If you go to setting CI/CD, there is a setting variable where you could create secret variables where the value is hidden or you can just add other variables that store certain settings. There are some secret variables that are only required for authentic to run. For example, the secret key. This is only consumed by authentic. However, if you want to add some other variables, for example, managing terraform uh stuff like that or connecting other APIs, you don't want to always manage these variables for each of your repository. So that's why you can create group variables. So these are inherited from the group and then each of the projects in the homelab group can use these variables and then you don't need to store this sensitive information inside deployment templates or stuff like that. So this is also super important. And what I also just recently found out that that is super useful is the Gab CLI tool. It's pretty nice. You can um check all of the repositories that or the projects that you running. For example, uh a repo list will list out all of my repositories on my personal GitLab instance. So here um I can see all of the CI/CD pipelines that were executed and what was the status and so on. So you can really automate a lot with this GLAB tool. I really like this and sometimes it's even faster also because you can use this in warp terminal and tell AI to use the GLAB tool to get access to some of the CI/CD pipelines. troubleshoot this with the AI tools. That is also super useful. Just go to the documentation, search for Gab. There you can see some installation instructions how to do this. I think it's even available in Homebrew if you're using Mac OS. Of course, also Linux, Windows, uh, and subsystem for Linux. All right. So, uh, if you use Gab, you first of all need to authenticate. So, just type in Gab of login. Just go to GitLab self-managed and it will guide you through some of the settings. you just need to add the URL. Um, and if you want to clone repositories using SSH, I would always recommend you to use SSH for git cloning because then you can add your SSH key and then create a personal access token for API requests. Uh, by the way, let's let's go through this uh once uh because I think that might be also quite important. So, let's go to the um testing instance here. So when you go to your account preferences, you can set up um SSH keys. So that's quite important. Just click on add new SSH key. And I also going to disable expiry key. And then I can use this to authenticate with SSH to my GitLab instance. The same for access token. So that's a bit annoying because you have to specifically set an expiration date here. That's a bit annoying. And I think one year is the the longest time. Actually, a few days ago, my personal access token in GitLab expired and all of my CI/CD pipeline stopped working and I couldn't authenticate and I was wondering what the heck is now going on. So in the context of the Proxmox backup server, this refers to a situation where the backup server attempts to back up or interact with itself in a way that creates some recursive or self-reerential condition, which is not really a good idea. So in simpler language, if you try to back up the backup server itself, it's going to create a problem. And that's why I thought it might be better to run this in a small container. But also, if you're like me and you're a crazy dude just having one simple NVME drive in your physical Proxmox server, you can still use this storage for the Proxmox backup server as well. and I later want to synchronize the local backups and send them to a remote storage location using NFS, then I can benefit from both. I can have super fast, quick and small uh backups on my local Proxmbox server discs and still have a separate physical location where I could do some disaster recovery if the entire server goes down or anything is damaged with the discs. That is what I definitely would recommend you to do. First of all, let's take a look how to configure a storage locally. Of course, if you've got a separate device like a storage controller with separate discs that you didn't configure on Proxmox already, so they are just there for backups, you can forward the entire storage controller. But I think a mount point is also fine because there you can select a storage disc. So in my case, I always just use /mount and then slashbackup local so that I know that this location is stored on my local Proxmox server and then I just give it a disk size. In my case, 200 GB. What you probably should deselect is this small backup checkbox because then this mount point will automatically be excluded from any backups. And once you created this mount point, you should see it under your storage location under CT volumes that there is a new virtual disc created that the Proxmox backup server can use to store the backup files on. All right. So now we need to switch to the Proxmox backup servers UI and go to data store and add a new data store. So here we just give it a name. In my case, I just picked the same name as the backing path. So the /m backup local and then you can select two settings here. The GC schedule which stands for garbage collection. So this is a process that helps to manage and reclaim storage space by removing some data of the backup system that is no longer needed. And the prune schedule which applies retention policies. So how long or how many you want to keep your backup files. These jobs you can run daily at midnight by default. You can also adjust the time if you want to, but I think once a day at midnight is fine. And then in the prune options, you can define if you want to keep the last X backups or if you want to keep one daily, weekly, monthly. I personally tend to keep one daily backup, one weekly backup, and one monthly backup so that I got enough flexibility. But adjust this according to your needs. Because it's an incremental backup, you can really keep many different backups. So you have more options to go back in time because you might not immediately recognize if you have deleted or broken something the next day. So better keep more backups than less. So you got more options to go back in time and revert to an older state if that's needed. Especially when doing file level recovery that is becoming really handy because then you can restore just a specific file that you have deleted 20 days ago. So adjust this according to your needs and how frequently you want to keep these backups. All right. So now the data store is created. When you go to the data store summary, you can get more details and insights about the data store. You can see the usage. By the way, here you should see the maximum disk capacity of your mount point. If you don't see that and you still just see 10 GB or so of the root disk of the Proxmox backup system, you should double check if you've used the correct path. And here you can also see how many container backups or host backups you stored, what is the dduplication factor. So the higher the dduplication factor, the more disk space you're saving. And here you can also inspect the content. So what are the different backups currently stored? Of course, this is uh still empty. The prune jobs and GC configuration, you can add synchronization jobs and also verification jobs. And a verification is quite important when it comes to backups because when you're just taking backups and you're seeing, oh, the backups are arriving and everything is fine and then you want to restore something and then you only notice there is something wrong with it, well, you are in big trouble. So therefore, a verification job is always always important when taking backups. So therefore I would definitely switch to the options and enable the verify new snapshots. So this will always verify any new backups immediately after completion. This is definitely is super useful. Then you don't need a separate verify job. All right. So now we can configure the Proxmox virtual environment to connect to the backup server. And before we do that, I want you to create a separate user account on the Proxmox backup server. So the Proxbox v can securely connect to that but it doesn't have all the root privileges. So just go to the configuration access control create a new user and then when you added the user go to permissions here you can add a new user permission to the path / data store. So then the user will only have access to that data store location. Select the Proxmbox user you have created and select the role data store admin. Now we can switch back to the Proxbox virtual environment and add the Proxmbox backup server as a separate storage location. Just give it an ID for example in my case it's just PBS backup local. The server will be the IP address of the backup server or the DNS name and then use the credentials you have just created. Also enter the data store that we have created in my case backup local. And now we need to grab the certificates fingerprint. That might be a bit weird, but just go to the Proxmox backup server under configuration certificates. Select the self-signed certificate that should have been automatically created and then copy the fingerprint. You just need to put that into the field when you're creating the Proxmox backup server. Also, you could configure backup retention here, but usually um this is configured on the Proxmox backup server. So, just leave that default. And then if you want you can create an encryption job. Now of course if you do this you should never lose your encryption key otherwise you won't have access to the backups anymore. So this is an option that I personally I didn't need to do but of course it is recommended if you're storing this on a separate location and for security reasons you could create a client encryption key might be useful but again in a small home lab I don't know if you need that. Okay. [snorts] So once you added the storage location, you can now um create your backup jobs or what you can do is you can edit your existing backup jobs if you stored that previously on an NFS share. Just go to your backup job, click on add or edit your existing one and then change the storage location from local to your Proxmox backup server. Just select the virtual machines that you want to back up. Select your time schedule and then usually there is nothing else you need to change or configure. Then this should already be working. You can test if the backups are working. If you click on run no, this will immediately start a new backup job. And as you can see here, that is a backup job that is currently running. Now you might have noticed that this is kind of like the same speed as the regular backup jobs. And you might be wondering why the heck, Christian, you just said the Proxwork backup servers was so much faster and so on. So why I'm not seeing a difference? Because the incremental backups at first will require one full backup of the entire virtual machine discs at day one. But the next backups you're taking, they will only store the changes. And if your retention policy applies, so the prune job runs on the Proxmox backup server and the last day one with a full backup needs to be removed, it [snorts] will merge the full backup with the next incremental backup so that it always has at least one full backup of the entire virtual machine and then it only stores the changes of the files or the file system of that virtual machine. And this alone is saving so much disk space, especially if you're keeping a high number of backups. I didn't really realize that the usual backup job on Proxmokes doesn't support incremental backups. Now, with Proxmog's backup server, I don't really need to care about this and I can keep I don't know 30, 40, 50 different backup timestamps and it's not really making any huge difference on my disc. So, this is really really incredible. Now, incremental backups are one thing where the Proxmox backup server really shines. Uh, but there's also the next feature that is called ddduplication. This will be automatically done on the Proxmox backup server and it works the following. Without a dduplication, the backup server would have to store each chunk of the data. Like if you represent chunks with a b cde e f and so on, these chunks from time to time repeat. And that regularly happens depending on the type of the data. So when a chunk contains exactly the same data as the other chunks without dduplication that would be stored as a separate chunk on the disk. Now with dduplication the Proxbox backup server will know that the chunks are repeating. It just stores a small reference. So here for example you can see A B and D were chunks that were already saved on the disk and the small reference doesn't take as much disk space as the entire data. Now I think it's worth noting that dduplication requires CPU power because this reference and calculation stuff takes some resources. So not only when you're storing the data also when you're restoring data. However the dduplication job is running in the background as far as I know. So it's done after you've taken the backup and the disk savings can vary from 20% to 90% of disk space depending on what is the type of data. And the good news is that virtual machine images or backup data they have a huge duplicate data which is leading to higher savings when using dduplication. So you can really benefit a lot from this technique. Just to show you an example, this is my Proxmox server and you can see I'm storing a few virtual machines. one that has a disk size of 400 GB of data. That would already be double the amount of disk space that my Proxbox backup server has. And then I'm also storing some 60 68 68 GB of discs on the backup server. So that alone is like 600 GB of data. But the Proxmox backup server can still store this on its backup local disc that only takes 163 GB of data, which is in my case a dduplication factor of 4.24. So this is indeed quite impressive. And if you go to the content, you can still see all these backups here. You can see the last time stamp that they were verified. And if you click on that drop- down button here, you could see the individual backups here as well. So don't let yourself be confused when more backups appear. They stack up and they always show you the maximum size of the disk of the VM, not what actually is taken on the Proxmox backup server disc. So just how large the disk is on its own. But I can show you how uh it works when I go back to my Proxmox server and for example, let's run a backup job now. And you can see how fast it is. So just go to data center, go to backup and let's say run now. So here you can see it's using fast incremental mode storing 384 GB of data in just 14 seconds and it's even faster for the other virtual machines that have much lower disk volumes. Yeah. All right. So that was it. It didn't took a minute to store all my virtual machines on the Proxmox backup server. So if we switch back here and reload, you can see the backup appears. And uh here you can see what I mean. It's the same disk size, but it's indeed not taking much more space on the uh data store. If we go back to the summary, you can see it just increased a little and I still have enough space to store all the other increments. And when it's applying the retention policies and the prune jobs and the garbage collection jobs, it even compresses this more. The dduplication factor might increase. And I even have more disk savings on the Proxmox backup server. The cool thing is you can even do file level restore. Now one thing that might be a bit confusing here, you cannot really do this from the content of the backup server. So here you still see the blobs of data. That doesn't help you much. But if you go back to the Proxmox server and select the backups here, you have an option file restore. And if you click on that, you can see the individual files on the discs. And you could really restore a specific directory or just one specific file that you need like a database or I don't know some other files that you might have lost. Now one thing that is a bit unfortunate you cannot easily restore them in the virtual machine itself because the Proxmox backup system is an agentless um backup system. It's not like something like vmor so where you can put that back in the same location. So you have to download this as a zip file, extract it, then shut down the service on your server and restore that particular database. So it might not be as intuitive and simple as professional backup system. But honestly, uh I think it's still great because it's it's an open source solution. So why would you argue about that? And I think this already is such a big plus and such a strong argument to use the Proxmox backup server. This is why I never would go back to storing backups on a separate NFS share. However, as I said in the beginning of this video, it is still a good idea to use NFS or a remote storage location to synchronize these local backups too because if something might happen with this local device, you're having a problem. Let's [laughter] let's say it this way. But the good news is you can still do this with the Proxmox backup server even when it's running in an alexe container. But uh if you want to follow this tutorial and use a remote NFS location for synchronizing the backups, you definitely need to run the Alexi container in privilege mode. Otherwise, it won't work. I've tried it a bunch of times and it's not as intuitive as the other settings because first of all, we need to open the terminal and log in via SSH to our Proxmbox backup server. That's why I said you should enable remote access. Then it's going to be much easier. And then first of all, you should create a separate folder where you want to mount the NFS share to. Because I've already created a mount point for the Lexe container under /m backup local, I thought it might be a good idea to just call this /m backup NFS. And then you should change the ownership to set this to the backup user. That is what the backup server will use internally. and adjust the permissions to 775 so that the Proxmox backup server will have the correct privileges to save to this location. Now the next thing that we need is the NFS common packages on the Debian system of the Proxbox backup server. Usually by default they are not installed. So just run the apt install NFS common. Also you can install Vim at the same time because to mount the NFS volume automatically so that it always is mounted when you reboot the backup container you have to edit the file system table by using vim etc fap. So here we need to enter the IP address of our NAS or the remote location where the NFS server is running. In my case I want to store this on my unrated NAS. By the way, if you don't know what that is, you can check out my unrated video. That's pretty cool. So, this is my bigger storage server with a redundant uh storage array where I can better store backups for a longer time. So, the strategy that I'm following here is I'm just keeping a daily backup on my local Proxmox backup server and on the remote storage location, I'm keeping more backups so that I can go back in time. The recovery probably will be not as fast if I would do it from the local device. But anyways, this is more for an archive backup restore option. Here on the unrated server, I had one issue that I needed to solve because currently I only have a rule created that anything from my production local area network from with the 10.20/16/24 network can connect to this server. But the flex in the parenthesis are wrong. So I needed to add another one that is the no root squash. So this is an option that allows the root user on a client machine to have root privileges on the NFS mounted directory as well. This is needed for the backup server to correctly initialize the data store. Without this option, it will not work. So this is what I needed to adjust. But when we go back to the Proxbox backup servers shell in the ftop file, I just needed to enter the IP address. Then the mount point on the remote NFS server. So my unrated NAS, this is usually under the /mt/ user because it's a user share uh NFS backups. And then I mounted this to the local folder that we have just created on the backup server/MT/backup NFS. Of course, we're using the NFS protocol with a version 4. And then I needed to add the correct flags. I just copied this from my tutorial. So you can probably just use the same settings as well. They just work. And then you need to reload systemd with a systemct ctl demon reload. This should automatically mount the remote NFS location. You can check um if you do an ls on this mount point if there are any files that were coming from the remote system. As you can see here, this is currently full with the old backups of my old NFS uh backup location that I used before. I needed to remove them all so that when I created the data store on the Proxmox backup server, it was initialized successful. What was a bit weird is this error message in the background and that it took a bit longer to create this data store, but once that was complete, it indeed worked and then you can create a sync job. Now you have to do this on the backup NFS uh data store. Create a sync job and select the location local. So then it will pull the backups from the local data store and upload it onto the remote NFS store. And I've also configured this to run daily, maybe not at the same time as the regular backup jobs run. So most likely you will have to configure this once these are complete. So 1 hour later should be enough. and then it's running automatically and copying over everything from your local data store to the remote NFS location. You can still restore your virtual machines from both locations if you need to. All right, guys. So, that's it about the Proxmox backup server. === Docker Swarm Mode: EASY Tutorial ===

Hey guys, this is Christian and in today's video I'm going to show you how I upgraded my Docker containers to the next level using Docker Swarm or more precisely the Docker Swarm mode because some people say that if you want to have container orchestration, you should not really care about Docker Swarm because it's dead. And that was also part of the reason why I never really used it in my home lab. However, you should not confuse the Docker Swarm mode with the Docker Classic Swarm project, which is indeed not actively developed anymore. The Docker Swarm mode is something different, and it's directly integrated into the Docker engine, and allows us to create and manage a cluster of Docker nodes, orchestrate containers with fall tolerance, load balancing, and rolling updates, and also some other cool features for credential and config management. And it's really damn simple. Once I started looking into it, I realized this is maybe the perfect middle ground for people that want to have something more sophisticated as the simple Docker engine, but not as heavily complex and overwhelming as Kubernetes. And if you're one of these guys, you should definitely keep watching this video.
