And by the way, this is super damn simple. Let's learn a bit more about the different features and capabilities. So the docker swarm mode transforms all your separate docker engines into a single virtual docker host and that gives you a centralized and unified cluster of the different docker engines called nodes in the swarm mode and provides built-in feature discovery with automated DNS assignment for services and internal load balancing so you can easily distribute incoming requests to your containers evenly across all the different nodes and that of course gives you fall tolerance and high availability. The swarm mode also has a scheduleuler that automatically restarts failed tasks on healthy nodes and ensures your applications and containers are always running. And it also has some interesting security capabilities like there is a secure node to node communication with mutual TLS encryption and it provides an integrated secrets management to safely handle sensitive information like passwords or API keys. I will later show you how that actually works. And it does things like rolling updates and roll back. So you can easily revert back to an older version of a container configuration. And the scheduleuler is intelligent enough that it places containers based on resource availability in the cluster like CPU and memory. And you can even configure so-called constraints optimizing cluster resources. And there are many many cool things here. people that already worked with Kubernetes like me, uh the first question that I had is why the heck should I care about Docker Swarm when I have Kubernetes? And sure, Kubernetes is kind of like the more sophisticated solution that even has some other interesting capabilities going beyond the feature set of the swarm mode. However, if you once tried out Kubernetes, you probably will agree that it has a kind of steep learning curve. And trust me guys, the Docker swarm mode is just so much easier to set up and I think even easier to use because you can use some tools that you already know. That is not really the case for Kubernetes where you need to learn a completely new technology. So I hope I got you hooked and you want to learn more about the swarm mode. So let me show you how to easily set up this. And first of all, I want to prepare three new virtual machines where we install the Docker engine. For this I'm going to use my new Python scripts that I recently developed using AI. So, I'm going to create three new virtual machines. I'm just going to call them VM tests. And I'm pretty sure while you're watching this script now, you definitely want to check that out. So, don't worry. I'm going to make a video about it. So, this way I can just distribute new virtual machines for demonstration videos like this in a few seconds using Cloudinit and the Proxmbox templates. All right. So, now the creation of the VMs is complete. They should already contain the Docker engine. So there I explain everything and you probably already know it from my other videos. So I'm not going to spend too much time on this. Now, if you want to create a Docker Swarm cluster, I need to start with one machine that will become the new leader of this cluster. Ideally, this is the first virtual machine. So I'm just going to open a new SSH connection to this uh IP address and verify that we can execute the docker commands. So now we can create a new cluster with the docker swarm init command and also add the flag advertise IP address where the other swarm nodes will try to connect to. In my case it's the 10.2030.1 the IP address of the first node and that's it. Uh now it is technically a swarm cluster already and it generated a new join token with a command that you just need to copy and execute on the other two nodes as well. So let's open a new connection to the VM test 2 and then just paste this command and know this node has joined the swarm as a worker node. So what that means I will cover in a second. Let me just finish the first node. And now we can go back to the leader and execute the docker node ls. So that should give us all the docker swarm nodes. As you can see here, we have VM test one, two, and three. But as you might have noticed, only the first node is a leader. And when you try to execute the docker node ls command on the second server, for example, you will get the error message. This node is not a swarm manager worker nodes. So that is probably a concept that I have to explain because it's really important for high availability later. Technically, you could just run this cluster with one leader and add as many nodes as you want as worker. If you don't have three Docker nodes, in my home lab, for example, I'm running this cluster with just one leader and one worker, so two machines. But it will not have fall tolerance because if the leader goes down and you only have workers in the cluster, the Docker Swarm cluster will not be functional. So it requires an odd number of leaders and more than half of the leaders have to be online that the swarm cluster is functional. It's kind of similar to Kubernetes. There is also the same. So you can't have two leaders. You need at least three to achieve high availability and fault torance or you accept that if the first node goes down everything is not working anymore. So you can really decide how you want to set it up. But I would recommend you to run a docker swarm mode with at least three nodes where you promote the two worker nodes as additional leaders as well. The way how you do this is you need to do it from the leader. So just run docker node promote and then VM test 2 and VM test 3. When you now execute the docker node ls, you can see that the two nodes have the manager status reachable. So the first one is still the leader. But if that one goes down, one of the other nodes will become the new leader. And now once you promoted the other nodes, you can now execute the Docker node LS commands from all the other leaders as well. All right, so that's how you set up the cluster. It's super damn simple. Now let's start creating a Docker service because Docker services are kind of different from Docker containers. The Docker containers, these are the standalone versions that you can just run on the Docker engine. But when you enable the swarm mode, you probably want to create these Docker containers as Docker services because Docker services can have more than one container running on the same cluster. So if you define that a Docker service has two replicas, it will make sure that at least two containers with that service are running on the entire cluster and it automatically tries to find out where is the best node that has some resources left to run and start up some uh required instances. So you can easily do this with the docker service command and then just create a new service. For example, let's create a new engineext server. So I'm not really rushed by anything. I can run them side by side. These are all of the great things about Docker Swarm mode. I absolutely love it. However, there are also a few challenges that come with that. Of course, you're using volumes in Swarm mode. These volumes are local to each node by default. So they are not automatically shared across the cluster like the configuration. Which means if a task that is running on the first node has a volume and then it needs to shut down and start up on the second node, the data is not the same. The good news is that Swarm supports storage drivers. By the way, I've recently done a video about Docker volumes using NFS. That is a solution that you could use to store the data outside of your Docker Swarm cluster or your Docker services. Run this on a separate NAS device. Mount the volumes using NFS. This way you could have scaled um services or scaled stacks across different nodes by still having a persistent shared storage. This is also possible. However, I know that NFS is not really the best technology when it comes to that. It's also not the most performant and you might run into some issues with databases and so on. I personally never had issues but people have been telling me that this is not a good solution. So maybe there is a better way of handling this but I will look into this. I will take a look at storage drivers and I wanted to do this anyway for other topics. So I might just experiment with a seph or other um distributed storage systems. We will see. But for now I'm using constraints uh and slowly start migrating some of my containers to docker stacks or swarm services. But yeah, Docker volumes is definitely an area that you should carefully look at. However, uh Docker Swarm mode also have some other interesting capabilities when it comes to networking or credentials and config management. And I want to talk about these things as well. First of all, let's take a look at the overlay network driver. So, this is by far one of the most interesting features for me because the overlay network driver allows us to create a Docker network among all the Docker Swarm modes and overlay networks. Uh this is often used to create a connection between the swarm services, but you can also use it to connect standalone containers running on different hosts. And uh by the way, what is pretty cool, an overlay network by default is actually made for swarm services and swarm stacks, but you can still connect Docker standalone containers, have these containers or standalone projects communicate with a Swarm server. So that's pretty cool. Let's also take a look how to do this. Actually pretty simple. You just need to execute docker network crate and then make sure that it has the driver overlay and that you add the attachable flag. So only when you enable attachable for this docker network, standalone containers managed in docker compos projects can be attached to this overlay network. Otherwise it won't work. And you can by the way also add an encryption with opt encrypted. However, this encryption uses IP sec. It will have a performance impact. So don't use this for any performance heavy critical services like database operations over the network or whatever. And uh then you need to define a name for the network. For example, let's create the network uh with the name proxy. So then we can list all the networks. You can see that um the docker stack also created a default network with the type overlay and docker swarm also created a default ingress network. However, this is not attachable. So that's why I think it is good to create a custom one that you manage externally and then in your compos files you can of course say networks can say proxy this is external true and then define that this container for example is connected to the proxy network. So the overlay network driver is really a big plus for swarm mode. But swarm mode also introduces two other superpowers that I want to show you called secrets and configs making credential and config files super safe and simply uh distributed across your cluster. Because the problem uh with standalone containers in Docker is that we commonly store sensitive credentials uh or data or also config files directly in either the compos or using environment variables using files that are stored in projects on the docker host and in a cluster. It is much better to manage these things centrally and the docker swarm mode um has the feature to do that internally in its encrypted database that is shared across the different nodes. So you can centrally manage credentials and configs and transmit them to only those containers that need to have access to it. And this is really great uh for storing API tokens or SSH keys, certificates to and other things that are sensitive. Let me show you how to do that. So you easily just create um a secret something like super safe secret and pipe this to the docker secret create command. No, you need to give it a name. For example, uh secret one. I'm not really creative as you might know. And also list all the secrets that you have created. And then if we go back to our compose file, uh we can use this secret uh when we append the secrets section here. Um okay, it's not the engineext config, it's secret one. This is managed externally. You could also manage a secrets or configs directly in the compost file, but I'm not really a friend of it. Um I think it is better and more safe to create them manually using the shell and then just include them to your project as externally managed. And now here we need to add the secret section. So you might wonder where these secrets are actually located inside the container. Now, Docker uses um a file that is created in the /r run/se directory and this you could also access for example if you have environment variables something like API token file then you can use this location here run secret secrets one and this file will contain the actual secret that is stored in this object inside the cluster. One problem I ran into, especially with some services that accept only string variables in their environment variables. This is a problem because this file contains the secret, but it's not loaded automatically in the environment variable. So you can't do it this way. You have to use container images that support reading secrets from a file. So you can't do it like this. You can't define the token and using this here. then it would just contain this string. So you have to specifically make sure that the container supports loading these tokens or secrets from a file that you can define in the environment variable. So this is a challenge that I'm also running in uh when using traffic or migrating traffic to docker swarm but I will cover this in my traffic video. It's actually not a big deal but you need to be aware of this uh small problem. And the next thing is that you can also add config files that you manage in docker swarm and also import it to the docker services. So assume we would have a configuration file in here that we want to um pass to the individual containers. === How to structure your HomeLab network? ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C3Cagcs3r9s

Hey everybody, this is Christian and in today's video we're talking about how to create a well ststructured HomeLab network. Because I know this is unfortunately a topic that's often overlooked because IT and tech ners, we sometimes just love to tinker and put different bits and pieces together to make it just somehow work. So you can take this as an example how to structure a home network, but I really want you to build your own topology. All right. So now let's dive a bit deeper into some of these technologies that you see here. So first of all I want to start with segmentation because this is a crucial thing and the names that you have seen here above these areas these are called network zones. So a network zone is just a logical group of network interfaces or devices. And I think this is also for a small home lab, a pretty important technology because you can use it to better control what devices or what users have access to your critical infrastructure to your servers. You can apply specific firewall rules or access controls. And honestly, it's also much better for organization because I can directly see where my home lab servers are and I cannot confuse them with other devices in my private network and so on. So here above you can see the WAN zone. This stands for wide area network. And I use this to define anything that is beyond my firewall. So my home router and also the public internet. Here you can see I'm using a network starting with 192 168 810 with a 24 subnet mask. I will later explain in a bit more detail what that means. Then we have my LAN zone. This is for my private network again where I have my TV, Xbox, all the other just regular home devices that you might have. This has the network 1010.0016. My pro network uh this is my actual production home lab so to speak where all of my servers and other stuff that you see in the videos is located. It starts with 10.20/16 2000/16. And then I'm also thinking about adding some devices in the future to a DMZ, which stands for demilitarized zone. And this provides a separate layer of security by isolating the public accessible servers from the internal network. So when I would host my private website from a server in my home lab, I would usually not put this into the same production home network where all the other testing devices are. I would put all the devices where people can access this from outside my protected network into a separate network zone so I can much better control what is going in and out of that zone and enforce stricter security policies when something is coming from the internet to my servers instead of when I'm just experimenting and trying out something. But yeah, I haven't actually used this. I will at least make one or two um adjustments to this structure to be able to effectively do this. But yeah, that's a topic. We'll talk in a few minutes about it. So these are the network zones that I've created. And by the way, if you're confused by these IP addresses and most people will be familiar with the 1921 168 whatever/24, this is what many many home routers use by default. And then the other networks like 1010.00 00 10.20.0016 are also IP networks in the private IP range but they look a bit different and they have a different subnet mask. This slash16. So what does that actually mean? Well, I don't want to make a complicated video about subnetting but just to show you how that generally works is the slash24 is the C notation of the 255.255.255. 255.0 subnet mask. So that means that the first 24 bits of the IP address are used for the network portion. Only the last eight bits are used for the host addresses. IP addresses assigned to devices in that network can vary from 1921 1680.1 to 254. And when you take a look at these networks that I have created, let's also do the IP calculation for 10.2016 for example. Here you can see that means the first 16 bits are the network portion and all the rest is available for host addresses. So a device that has the IP address 10.20 uh 53145 is in the same network as the 10.20 10.20 20 12 for example my servers usually start with 10.20 and my Kubernetes cluster for example it starts with 10.2020 so then I can immediately see oh this IP address is part of my Kubernetes cluster and I can better assign dynamic IP addresses in that Kubernetes cluster's IP range for any load balancers or something like this. So when I see an IP address in my home lab, I can directly recognize where this is coming from and what's the purpose of this device. But yeah, that's maybe just my crazy habit. Um, you might just want to keep it simple and stick to the 192 168 whatever 24 subnet mask. You get so many host addresses. I think that's more than enough for a small home network. So don't let yourself be confused by this. But I personally just find it a bit easier. Now let's also come back to the main question. Why the heck did I do this? Again, as I said, it is really practical to enforce security policies because if you're creating different network zones in your hobblap network, a device from one zone cannot necessarily access another zone. And the way how I control this is with my firewall. I just recently switched my main firewall to open sense. You might have seen that video. If you didn't, then go and check that out. But just to show you how I'm doing this here, you can see my firewall has IP addresses in all these networks. So it's the main controlling device and the main gateway where traffic coming from one zone going to another zone has to pass through. And of course this main controlling device, the firewall can then say what device or what user has access to what service or device in another zone. So here for example you can see when I go to my firewall rules I have created some rules for the LAN zone here you can see my Mac studio is currently in the LAN zone having this IP address and it's allowed to go anywhere so any zone any port any destination because I'm the administrator of my network and I have full access to everything but of course uh I don't want my regular private devices like my TVs my iPhones and whatever to have access to any server just to a few services that I specifically define in the firewall rules. For example, here you can see my DNS server that is currently located uh on the server production one under the 10.202 uh IP address. By the way, the 32 C notation means this IP address is really just that one host. So all the devices coming from the LAN network can access this IP address but only on port uh 53. And the other firewall rule will provide access to all the LAN devices to this IP address on port 443 which is my home assistant server. So I can control the lights on or off. And then I created a rule to block anything else that is coming from my LAN zone and is trying to get access to the trunk uh network, the pro network and the management network. As you can see here in the network topology, these refer to these elements. I've not created one for the DMZ because as I said it's a work in progress but yeah that's how I'm applying this. Um the next topic that we also need to talk about and I know it's getting a little more complex but the technology that allows me to do this and which is super practical is called VLANs which stands for virtual local area network because without VLANs you would have to buy a separate physical switch for each network zone and connect it to a separate physical port on the firewall because only then the different network zones are physically separated from each other and the firewall is the only main gateway where traffic needs to pass through. But as you can imagine, buying separate physical switches for a home lab is absolutely insane. It is super costly. Not only that you need to buy the hardware devices which cost a lot of money, it also costs um in terms of power consumption because each of the devices consumes power and as you can imagine this is a super important technology in professional IT networks. Everybody is using VLANs there because it allows you to virtually split your physical switch into multiple virtual switches where you can define for each port on the switch which VLAN uh it is on so that devices from one VLAN are really separated from devices in another VLAN. Let me show you on my sofa switch how exactly that works. So here I've created all these different VLANs. So every VLAN needs a unique identifier which is also sometimes called the VLAN tag. Just imagine this VLAN ID stands for a separate physical device. You see it a little better on the software switch when you go to VLAN settings. So here you can see every part of the upper row of my network switch is assigned to my private network and these ports here are assigned to my production homelap network. The same for DMZ. So you might have noticed that some of these ports have a different symbol. Uh most of them are untacked ports. So that means packets that are sent out to these ports and received from the devices are just treated as normal packets but they are just routed in between all these ports and not sent out to other ports. And one port is assigned as a tech port. So the tech port is something like a transport port for um sending out or receiving traffic from multiple VLANs. So you can see the same port 22 is assigned to the other VLANs as well. Whenever the switch is receiving a network packet that is coming from these untacked ports and has to be sent out to this tech port, the switch will add a small 12bit VLAN identifier to each of the network packets that are sent out to the tech port. So the device on the other end will know from which VLAN this network packet is coming from. That of course requires a configuration on that device as well. So on the device you also have to configure all these VLANs and the VLAN ids of course have to be matched. As you can imagine this port 22 this is exactly the network port where my opensense firewall is connected to. Here you can see all these VLAN interfaces with the same VLAN tag as I have configured on the switch. In this way I have connected my switch to the firewall uh transporting all these different VLANs and then the switch is segregating the traffic coming from the firewall out to the individual unteed ports. Now this is really great to achieve a solid network security concept. I know it might be tough to understand VLANs because I know it from my own experience. A long time ago, I was working as an IT service technician where I needed to go outside to customers and setting up networks, setting up computers and stuff and one customer bought three new HP switches with VLANs and it was the task to refactor the existing network structure and set up VLANs for this customer because they wanted the same. They wanted control of separating the servers where the critical and important company data was uh stored on and the other employees computers. I had no idea about VLANs and it was a complete mess. It took me the whole day and some of my weekend to find out how to connect these devices and how the untacked and tacked ports work and it was so frustrating. But I can tell you after that weekend when I finally understood how this works and I could set up the company's network, I was so happy and I was so excited because I had learned something new and I just wanted to try it out at home and apply the same security concept. And yeah, that's also what I'm using right now for my home lab. The next step that I'm aiming to achieve is to add this DMZ for the public facing services and add the Proxbox nodes to the VLAN concept as well. So that means what I'm trying to do is the ports where my Proxmbox servers are currently connected as unteed ports through the pro network. I want to make these tacked ports to uh the DMZ as well. So that would allow me to create a virtual machine on the prox nodes and put this into a different VLAN. So for example, I could have a server in the DMZ zone with an IP address 10.302 for example. And whenever this device has to send out traffic somewhere, it has to be routed through my firewall where I can define, hey, this DMZ server is not allowed to go into my production homelap network even though it is running on the exact same physical hardware. Using VLANs, I can separate these two networks out. And I think this would also be a great tutorial to show you how to set up VLANs on Proxmox, but I need some more time researching this. But that's definitely what I have in mind for the future. So, as you can see, every Holip network has some room for improvement. And before we wrap up, I also want to talk about another topic. Don't worry, it's not as complicated as VLANs, but it is also quite interesting. I hope you're still up for some more stuff. So, let's also talk about IP ranges because these help me to further split a single subnet into multiple logical sections. And this is super super practical for DHCP to avoid running into some IP address conflict problems and so on. DHCP by the way stands for dynamic host configuration protocol and this is the main network protocol used to automatically assign IP addresses and also other network configuration parameters to devices in a network. When you plug in a device like a laptop or a computer to your switch, it will send out a broadcast packet to locate a DHCP server saying something like, "Hello, I need an IP address." And the DHCP server responds to the device and assigns a new IP address that is not used by any other device and it tells it where the gateway is, where the DNS server is and so on. How you can easily manage a different IP ranges and DHCP. Um I'm also using open sense as my main DHCP server because what would be better right? So let's go into services DHCP and let's go to the LAN interface. So here you can see I have enabled the DHCP server on the LAN interface but I didn't assign the full network as an IP address range. I'm limiting this to 1010.10 so I can easily remember this to 1010.199. So these are all IP addresses that are used by the DHCP server. And when I go to leases you can probably see some of these addresses here. Ah yeah. So here's my iPhone for example the 1010 the watch. Okay, that's probably the uh Apple Watch or whatever. It has this address 1718 and it goes just on and on until 199 and then it's finished. And I usually create all my static IP addresses. So when I uh statically configure an IP address on a device in a completely different range, so this usually has 10.10.0 or one or two. I could just use any other uh number than 10 because if you would just have the DHCP range in the same range as you uh configure a static IP addresses, it um you might run into a pretty common problem. That is when a DHCP server tries to assign an IP address to a device that is already taken by a device that is statically configured. So the DHCP server doesn't know the IP address and it will result in an IP address conflict. Now, of course, the DHCP server isn't really dump. So, it usually checks if an IP address is already taken by a statically configured device before it's giving this IP address to another device. So, in reality, these problems don't always often occur. But it could be that you have a device with a static IP address that is offline for quite some time and the DHCP server hasn't seen that in a while. So, it assumes, oh, this IP address is free and is assigns it. And once the statically configured device with the same IP comes online again, you run into this problem. So I had this into some professional networks back in the days when I was working as a service technician. So I definitely learned my lesson to configure the IP address range from DHCP in a different range where I configure static IP addresses. Uh also another best practice tip I can share to you is when you have devices you want to configure with DHCP but you want to make sure these devices always get the same IP address and not dynamically. You can configure them in the DHCP server under static mappings for this device. And here you can enter the MAC address which is the worldwide unique identifier for this physical network interface. So no other device in the world will have the same address and you configure this to have a static IP address that is ideally outside of the regular DHCP range. So here for example I can directly see any device uh that has an IP address 1010.10 anything beyond 200. This is coming from a DHCP server, but it is statically mapped to a specific device as in this example, my Mac Studio, which always has this IP address. And this allows me to create firewall rules specifically for this Mac Studio. And no other device might accidentally receive the same IP address. All right. Again, it doesn't have to be that complicated. So you could also think about if you're using a 192 168 0 to uh make the static range from 1 to 20 and then have a DHCP range starting at 21 to 199 or whatever. So there are many different uh configurations possible according to your infrastructure and your requirements. But yeah, that's basically how I organize and structure my HomeLab network. And I thought it might be a good example to show you how you can easily repeat some of these uh techniques and and maybe improve your network infrastructure and organization to achieve a strong security concept and just have a better overview and can remember IP addresses better. Might be I hope that really helped you. But yeah, that's it for today. If you want to learn more, please also check out my other videos about excell DNS tutorial and of course my open sense firewall tutorial. I will drop you those in the description box down below. And as always, thank you so much for watching. A big thanks goes out to all my supporters and YouTube members. You guys are really amazing. And I'm going to catch you in the next one. Take care. Have a nice day. Bye-bye.

So before we jump straight into the automation flow that I'd like to show you, let's first take a quick look at the project plan so you get a better understanding of what exactly I have automated using which tools and how the different techniques and platforms all work together because otherwise it can get quite complicated. So first of all, as you might know from some of my older videos, I'm mainly using notion as my note takingaking and project documentation platform. I think notion is really a great tool that is also commonly used in the tech space and it's really great to store data. That's the reason why I'm also using this to manage all my YouTube video projects. So here I'm uploading the thumbnail for the video, the description, the link to my video on the YouTube platform when it's published. Basically any information that I later also want to publish as a new post on my website. And because notion recently added a new feature to send web hooks to any thirdparty system, I've created a new automation for this YouTube video database that will run every time when I change the status of one of my video projects to published. Then notion will send a web hook to my Castra automation platform where I'm using this data to process it and publish it on my website fully automatic. By the way, if you're not familiar with web hooks, these are a pretty standard technique in IT. It's basically a term for a simple HTTP web request that it's mainly used in serverto-s server communication. So to connect two different systems and send data based on an event or a trigger to another system where it's processed and yeah doing some other useful stuff with it. So in notion for example, you can select the specific properties of your database item that triggered the automation flow like the name of the video project, the title of the video, the video URL, the thumbnail and so on. And all this information is then included in the web request that gets sent to my Castra's automation platform. One challenge I had to solve here uh because Castra is running in my local network that I of course don't want to expose to the public internet without any security or authentication. I also needed to add two authentication credentials. One for the Cloudflare zero trust. So the Cloudflare tunnels that I'm using to securely connect to my local network and a second one to authenticate uh to the Castras platform via my authentic service. In notion, you can just easily add these authentication credentials as custom headers. By the way, I've also done separate videos about Cloudflare Zerorustrust and authentic. So, if you want to learn more about authentication in your hub app, you can also watch these videos. And then in the Castros platform, when the web hook is received from notion, it of course needs to do some stuff. It needs to first pass the request first. So transform all the necessary fields and variables that are sent with this request into a usable format and then create a new markdown file from a specific template that will work with my Hugo static website. And then also finally it needs to push these files to the git repository on my self-hosted GitLab instance where the website source code is located. By the way, when anything changes on this Git repository, it automatically rebuilds my website and publishes it on the Cloudflare's worker platform. If you're interested in how that actually works, I've already created two videos about my new website. That's why in this video, we really just focus on the Castra automation logic. All the rest is covered in these other videos. Of course, I will link you them in the description box down below. So, check them out if you want to learn more about these topics. All right. So that's basically the whole plan and the automation logic. Let's now take a look at the code of my automation flow and I'll explain to you what's actually happening in here because as you can see it got quite a long document. So let's first start with how Castro receives and parses the web hook request from notion. To receive web hooks in Castro, you just need to add a new trigger from the core plugins called web hook. Here you just need to define a key which is a secret. You also have to put in the URL of the third party system that will send the web hook in the notion automation. You might have seen this URL already. So the first part is the location of my Castro's platform. Nothing special in here. But then after the API endpoint, you have to refer to the specific automation flow so that Castra knows which flow to execute when it receives the request. And at the end you also have to put that secret in that you defined. You can view the specific format that you have to use for the web hook in the castras documentation that will also automatically open up when you select the trigger in your flow code editor. So just replace these placeholders after the slashes with a name space, the flow ID and the secret key. To demonstrate how that works, let's go to my notion platform and switch one of the video projects to publish that I need to update on my website anyway. And in the Castras web UI, you should see a new execution that will then run the entire pipeline logic. As with all executions in the Castro platform, you can easily view the entire execution log in a Gant diagram where you can simply see what step took how long and you can also inspect the logs. You can see the topology of the flow or inspect the outputs and metrics of your execution. I think Caster really provides great visibility into all these processes. So you can easily troubleshoot when something is not working the way you expected it. And the outputs are particularly interesting for my automation flow because as I said earlier, we didn't want to tell Castra just hey there is a new video project that was published. We of course also want to send all the necessary information of this video with that request like the title, the thumbnail, the video URL and so on so that the automation flow can create the markdown file for the website with the real information. So the first challenge was to find out in which specific format the notion web hook actually sends the database properties because I didn't really know what were the names of the fields and so on. And in the cast web UI you can very easily find that out if you go to the execution overview and when you scroll down you should see an object for your web hook trigger. In this web hook trigger, there is a field called variables. And in here, you will find all the details about that web hook request that Castro received like the HTTP headers it was sent with as well as the body which usually contains all the content that was sent with this request. And in my example with notion, for each database property that I enabled in the automation, it created a new field under the data properties object like the date, the name, the title, video URL, and so on. So I just needed to click through that to find out where the content that I'm looking for is located. Honestly, Notion does it a bit complicated. It has a lot of nestings and arrays because it supports different formattings of text which is a bit annoying and sometimes weird to pass. And that's why I thought the first step in my automation flow was to store these variables in Castra output so I could easily reuse them later in my other tasks. And this is I think the recommended way in Castra because it's a native uh variable type that you can use to pass data between tasks and other flows. So what I've done is I've added a new task from the type output values. So this I've used to store the variables from the nested body object of the web hook into easy rememberable names like the name will later just contain the name of the database object. The title will contain the video title, the description, the description and so on. Just one thing that was a bit more difficult is the thumbnail because the thumbnail is not a simple text value. It's an image. And when you use notion web hooks to send images, it doesn't really send the actual payload inside the request. It sends a specific shortlift link to a storage location that you can use to download the thumbnail from. But this is something I will come back to later. It is just important that we have all the necessary values from the web request stored in Castra outputs. So now we can use these values to create the markdown files for the video project. Of course, if you've watched my first videos about the Hugo static website framework, you probably remember how this is working. Again, this is my git repository stored on my self-hosted GitLab instance. This contains the actual source code of the website. And whenever I want to publish a new video project on my website, I just need to create a new folder with that video project name, put in the markdown file, fill in the values, and push these changes to the git repository. And to do that, we of course first of all need to clone the repository into the Castras automation flow. I'm just using the Castra's Git clone plug-in for that. It's pretty straightforward. You just put in the URL of your git repository and then it gets cloned to the workspace folder. So in my case, this is the URL of my self-hosted GitLab instance where the website source code is located. What might be important to know at this point is that I'm using the SSH protocol. I first tried that with cloning using HTTPS, but that caused a problem later and that's that's why I needed to switch to SSH, put in my private key to securely authenticate and so on. Also quite another important step in Castra when you're working with Git repositories and you want to access the files from that repository and other tasks later is that you need to store them in one persistent working directory. So at least it is persistent for that actual execution. After that it can get deleted but by default Kestra will always create a new temporary folder for each individual task in your flow. which means if you clone a repository first, the other tasks wouldn't have access to it. So therefore, I needed to use the working directory task and add all the other tasks as sub items to that. So they all happen in the exact same temporary directory where the first task can clone the source code and the others can also do something with these files. Now that I had this structure, I cloned the repository, I got the variables from the web request, I could finally start creating the markdown file in the videos folder of my website where I put all the variables that we've earlier stored in the cast output. Kra has a new feature implemented in some earlier versions that is really interesting for running scripts. It has now a new tab in the web UI editor that is called files which is basically a file storage in Castra for each nameace where you can upload any type of files that you need to use or access in your flows. In my case, I've just created a new folder called website where I want to put all the files or scripts that are necessary for my website automation such as the video generate Python script. So that will generate the actual markdown file and a video arc type markdown file which is the template that defines how exactly the markdown file for the video project on my website has to look like. So here you can find also placeholders for all the variables, the title, the name, date, description, and a video element. I will later show you in a bit more detail what that video element actually is. But um these files you can pretty easily access in any Castra task by just using the attribute name space files and then specify which files you want to include to that specific task. Here you can also see how I'm executing the Python script plugin where I first I want to install some Python libraries that I need for this script. The Ginger 2 library by the way is necessary for the template and the file creation and then I just execute the video generate Python script. And what is also important, I of course needed to pass the castras outputs which contain the variables and information for that file from the web request as simple environment variables. So I could easily access these environment variables in my Python script. Might be a bit complicated for anybody who is not deep into Python programming and so on. I'll try to summarize it at a high level. So by the way, I have also not written most of the script myself. I've actually used artificial intelligence in my warp terminal to do that for me. But um what this Python script will basically do is it parses the variables from the cast output. And there you finally can see that the file successfully is created with a new directory with the video project name/index.md. So this is the actual markdown file that got generated from that template. Now one thing that I probably should also explain when executing Python scripts in Castra is that you can do this in two ways. So this is also part of the new feature that was earlier introduced in a castra version that is called the task runners because you can specify if you either want to execute a script as a simple process. So this is really the same as you would just execute these scripts commands on the server shell where caster is installed. Alternatively, you could also use a taskr runner called docker. So then you can run these scripts in an isolated Docker container where you can specifically install any tools or use specific Docker images that contain the tools you need for the scripts. And it's really executing these tasks in an isolated process space using the Docker engine. But um again with this task I'm totally fine with executing it as a simple process. We will later run have an example where I used the docker task runner because it made more sense there. I've also used the taskr runner process for the next step in the cast flow. So this is just there for downloading the thumbnail because as I said earlier the notion web hook doesn't actually contain the thumbnail image. It just contains the URL where you can download the image from. It's just a simple shell script in Castra that uses the curl command to download the thumbnail from that image and puts it into the same video project folder as a feature.jpeg image that is automatically used by the theme of the Hugo website as a thumbnail for this post. But after executing all these tasks in the Castra flow, I had all the information that the website needs to create the post. The only thing that was left were pushing these changes to the git repository. And this is where I thought about some different solutions. One idea that I had was to fully automate this by just pushing the changes to the main branch using Git. That would automatically trigger a complete rebuild of the website and fully automated the publishing process. However, there was one argument against that because even my sophisticated scripts sometimes have problems and there will be of course some errors or things that could go wrong. Maybe that could even break the entire website. So, I still wanted to have some sort of manual control over the content updates that are actually published on my website. So that's why I thought about not just pushing the changes to the main branch of the Git repository, but instead creating a merge request which I can then see in my GitLab instance and I can review it. I could even make changes or small adjustments if the automation script got something slightly wrong, maybe in the description text or the URL or whatever. This way I could still see what's going to happen before it actually happens and I manually have to confirm it. That of course introduced another challenge for me because merge requests are very much dependent on the actual git platform that you're using. So that means merge requests on GitLab are different. The pull request on GitHub and Castra didn't really have a specific tool or plug-in to easily create merge requests on GitLab. So that's why you probably can imagine I needed to write another script or a few commands. And this time I wanted to use the Docker task runner in Castra because the tools that I need for creating the merge request in GitLab. This is the Gab CLI tool that also exists in a simple Docker container. So I thought instead of installing this on the server where Caster is running, it might be better to execute all these commands of the task in a Docker container with the image that already contains the necessary tools. So that's exactly what I've done. And here you can see all the commands that I've executed in this temporary docker process. Um I needed to pass through some environment variables to the container for the GitLab host and the authentication, my username and email address because this is needed in git. Here you can see the two files that were added to the git repository by my castras automation flow. The thumbnail and the markdown file which contains all the necessary information in the exact format that I want. And yeah, when I see this, when I'm happy with the content that should get published, I just have to click on merch and then it just takes a few minutes until the Cloudflare worker has rebuilt the website with the new content and all the changes and everything gets live on my public website. === The BEST alternative to Docker and VMs! // Proxmox LXC ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iIfRchzYSzM

Hey everybody, this is Christian and today we're diving into Linux containers or shortly called alexe on Proxmox. I know Docker has dominated the container conversation here on my channel for a long time, but alexe is a powerful alternative that's often overlooked. In one of my latest AI projects, I was forced to use a Linux container on Proxbox for the very first time. And that really made me think about if I should use it a little more often in the future. Because alexe is a fantastic technology on Linux. It's great when you need a small environment that behaves just like a virtual machine to test or deploy applications, but without the overhead of a full-blown operating system. These containers are incredibly efficient and fast, but still provide a robust isolation layer. Especially on Proxmox, it's super easy to manage them. And another cool thing I just discovered recently, there are even ways to simplify the application deployment in Alexe with community scripts or automated in infrastructure as code tools like Terraform. Trust me guys, even if you are a huge fan of Docker or virtual machines, the capabilities of Alex Prox might surprise you. So, let's check it out together. Before we do that though, I quickly like to show you how you can easily manage all of your tech and home lab projects. Because if you're juggling around different tasks and projects like me, you can lose track pretty fast. That's why you should use monday.com, the intuitive all-in-one work platform where you can collaborate efficiently with a team, manage complex projects, and even automate repetitive tasks. Monday.com is super simple and intuitive to use. You can easily create to-do lists for the next actions that you're planning in your projects. Create custom fields with additional notes, manage priorities, or plan timelines. As you might know, guys, I also absolutely love Canvan boards because that gives you the perfect overview of all the stuff that's going on. But you can also do some pretty cool advanced stuff like connecting different boards and adding dependencies between the tasks. For example, if one of your projects is to deploy a new application, but that requires the installation of a new server or adding certain DNS records first. For these type of workflows, monday.com is just perfect. And you can even create stunning dashboards, reportings, documentation pages. It's super cool. To get started, just check out monday.com by using the link in the description box down below and start streamlining your work like never before. All right, guys. So now let's take a look at Alexe. Before we jump straight into some examples on Proxmbox, let me first of all give you a little background about Alexe, how it works and how it competes to other virtualization technologies you might be familiar with. So Lexe simply stands for Linux containers and was released in 2008. So it's already been around for quite some time. And at its core, Alexe uses Linux kernel features like Dave spaces and croups to achieve something that we call operating system level virtualization. And that allows you to run applications on the same Linux kernel as the host, but in their own isolated process space. So that's a pretty big deal compared to virtual machines because virtual machines have their own kernel, their own drivers, therefore provide a stronger isolation layer than Lexe. But that also means that virtual machines are much heavier and require more resources. By the way, containerization might be a concept you're already familiar with in case you watch many of my other videos because there I mostly use Docker and Docker is also a similar technology like Lexe. Uh in the early days of Docker, it even used Lexe under the hood. But nowadays, Docker and Alexe diverged significantly since Docker built its own containerization engine and stopped using alexe. And Docker also focuses a lot more on virtualizing applications, application packaging and so on. And honestly, I have to say it, Docker even got a lot more attention in the recent years than Alexe. But still, Alexe, in my opinion, is a viable alternative to Docker, especially on Proxmox because Proxmox allows you to easily create and manage alexes directly in the web UI or even through automation. And it's really great in these situations where you just want to run some workloads in an isolated environment on Proxmox, but you don't want or need a full-blown virtual machine with its own operating system. In these use cases, it's often better to just use an Lexe. But even though technically it isn't, Alexi still feels like a virtual machine. So for example, instead of Docker, you can easily connect from your local machine to your Alexi over SSH, log into the shell, install applications, even update the packages, whatever you want to do with it, just like in a virtual machine. You can, by the way, even run Docker containers in an Alexia as well if you want. And so it's in my opinion a super interesting technology that you definitely should try out if you haven't done it already. But of course you don't have to do it alone. So let's do it together on my Proxmox instance. It's super simple to create alexis on Proxmox. You first of all have to download a container template in Proxmox. These are the container images simply packed in tarball archives and contain everything that a container needs to run. You just go to your Proxmox node where you want to start the alexe. Then select the local storage or any other storage where you have enabled CT templates. Then go to the templates and then go to download from URL if you have any custom location or you can even use the Proxmox maintained images because Proxmox provides a variety of basic templates for the most common Linux distributions. You can find things like Ubuntu in here, open sus, Dian, but you can also download turnkey Linux container images. By the way, turnkey is a project that creates pre-built ready to use virtual appliance images. Essentially, they just take a Linux distribution, typically Debian or Ubuntu, then pre-install and configure popular software packages, so you don't have to do it yourself. And this way you can easily deploy and install applications like GitLab or Postgress, EngineX, Drupal, Jenkins. So just go through that list. You'll find some really valuable stuff in here. But we are not using Turnkey today. I haven't really looked into this to be honest. I will later show you another community project that I personally prefer using. Once you have downloaded your desired container templates on Proxmox, you can then start creating a new Linux container. So just simply go to create CT which is right next to create VM and then it's opening the familiar Proxmox menu just like you would create a virtual machine. So select the node where you want to create this container on. Give it a unique identifier. In my case I'm just using this ID. I think that's fine. You also need to give it a host name. For example, Alexe test one. Then you also can decide if you want to run this as an unprivileged container. So uh I probably have to explain this. Historically alexes ran with nearly full root privileges on the host operating system. So this meant they had access to a wide range of host resources and kernel features. And this of course presented a significant security risk. Yeah. If a container is compromised, the attacker could potentially gain control of the entire host. That's why Alex's started using or started running as unprivileged containers by default. isolating the containers as much as you can from the host kernel limiting the potential damage uh in case of a compromise. And nesting is also another flag that is used in unprivileged containers that basically mounts the /rock and/sis uh directories inside the container. So you can decide if you want to enable this or not. Maybe some applications might need access to these directories for managing processes or quering kernel options. Yeah. And even some workloads might require privileged containers. So then you have to disable it. But I would strongly recommend using unprivileged containers. Then you also need to give it a strong password that you can later use to log into the shell. And you can even upload an SSH public key. I probably do that because then I can demonstrate how that's working. Then you just select the template that you've downloaded. In my case, let's let's spin up an Ubuntu uh container. I think that's the best. Then you can create a new storage disk for the root file system inside the container. Give it any size that you want and uh a storage location. You can even decide how many CPU cores the container should consume and how much memory it should use. And of course you can also select the network interface, enable or disable firewall and if you want to use DHCP or a static IP address. And also select the DNS domain. In my case, I just use my local domain. and my main DNS server in my home lab. I think that's fine. And then confirm. This is super super fast. It already completed in 2 seconds or so. By the way, when you go to the settings of the container, you can even do some other cool things like PCI passrough for example. Now, that though works a bit different than in a VM. That's just because the technology is different. And a virtual machine has a separate kernel with its own drivers while a Linux container shares the kernel and the same drivers with a host. So instead of selecting a specific device, you have to enter a device path. So /deaf/xyz. I needed to do that once in my AI video where I needed to pass through the graphics card of my Proxmox host into the uh container so it could be used by the local LLM. So, if you want to have an example for that, then check out my video where I've installed Olama in an alexec. I'll link it to you in the description. But yeah, for this example, we don't need to do that. So, let's start the container and go to the console. So, let's log in with a root user and the password that I've already created. Here we are. By the way, of course, I can also open a shell. I just need to find out the IP address. It has the 18 at the end. Root at the IP address. Yes, my public key should be already uploaded. And here we are. So you can see this is also working. You can update the app packages, install new packages. Don't treat this like a Docker container that always gets recreated when you update it to a new version. Think of it more like a virtual machine. So yeah, that's basically everything you need to know to get started with Linux containers. As I said, it's super simple and you can now do whatever you want with it. Start installing Linux applications. You can even install the Docker engine and run containers on it if you want. Yeah. But I want to share with you a super cool community project that I just recently discovered and that is even making things a lot easier for you. And this is called the Proxbox helper scripts. === Passbolt 5 (Self-Hosted Password Manager) got revamped! ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QPagt720HI4

Hey everyone, it's Christian and today we'll check out the new version of the open-source password manager, Passport. I've already reviewed Passport here some time ago on my channel and I absolutely love its unique features like the extremely secure architecture or that you can completely self-host it for maximum privacy. And what is also really nice about it, you can easily share passwords with other team members in Passport. probably one of these features that stands out the most. Super interesting for enterprise companies and maybe tech support teams. Now, the new version 5.3 of Password was just released and it is packed with some exciting new features such as the encrypted metadata that enhances the security even more and it has many more options to customize your credentials like adding new icons and colors. It now has custom fields and multiple URIs. So, I thought it might be a great chance to review it again. If you're looking for a secure privacy focused password manager that you can easily set up in your home lab or maybe company environment, this might be super interesting for you. So, let's get started. And a big thanks goes out to Passbolt for sponsoring this video. All right, guys. So, first of all, if you want to have a full installation tutorial on the free community edition of Passport, then please check out my first video about it because there I'm teaching you how to simply install this passport manager in your own self-hosted environment using a simple Docker compos deployment. Of course, I've also updated the templates on my boiler plates GitHub repository for it. And if you want to update your existing passport installation to the latest version 5.3 that we are reviewing in this video, then well just change the docker image tag to the latest version. Restart your container and then you will be ready to go. But of course, if you prefer a different installation method, feel free to check out some guides on their official website. There you can learn how to install this on Kubernetes, any Linux distribution, or even use it in the cloud. And of course, you can also upgrade to the business and enterprise plans, which by the way works both on cloud and your self-hosted environments to even further enhance the feature set. But again, in this video, we'll just focus on the free community edition of Passport that I have deployed in my home lab as well. So, let's log into my self-hosted Passport instance to check out the latest features that were added. By the way, I still have to switch to the Chrome browser because the Safari browser extension for Passport is unfortunately not ready yet, but I've already spoken to the team at Passport about this problem. So, it's definitely coming soon. And of course, they might add some other cool features as well, like pass key support is coming later this year along with more content types. So, I'm really excited about this. All right. So once you upgraded your passport instance from an older version 4 to the new version 5, the first thing that I would strongly recommend you to do after upgrading is to go to the organization settings and enable the new features to use the encrypted metadata. And also I think it's a good idea to switch the default meta data types when you're creating new credentials because only this will unlock some of the new features that I'm showing you today and it also increases the security and privacy for all your stored credentials that you migrate to the new encrypted metadata type. And just to give you a little more background on what actually is encrypted metadata and what that means. So in the version 4 of passold all the secrets such as passwords, descriptions or tootps were already protected with a robust end to end encryption but other sensitive metadata like the resource names or the URIs were still accessible by the server where passport is running. And while this may have been sufficient for many use cases, it certainly raised some concerns by people that were seeking a stronger privacy model, especially in cloud environments where the Passport instance is not self-hosted. This was a major issue the team wanted to address. The version five of Passport introduced a new hybrid encryption model that uses clientside decryption. By the way, in this video, we'll not dive into all the technical details. If you're interested, you'll find a great blog post that was created by the Passport team about their new security architecture for encrypted metadata, and it even explains some of the decisions and thinking process behind it. I absolutely love this. Of course, I'll put you a link to this blog post in the description so you can check it out. But I think it's worth saying that this approach by passport is pretty unique because password managers usually don't encrypt everything by default because it's making things like searching or account recovery or even system auditing much harder to do. That's why I'm really honoring the passport's approach to focus even more on the needs of enterprise organizations and professional teams and provide a good balance between security and usability. I think for everybody who is cautious about security and wants a password manager that is super transparent with how it's storing and processing credentials then passport is definitely a nice option. However, if you still want to use the legacy metadata type in passport for whatever reason you can still do this. So when you're activating this new encrypted metadata in passport all the existing credentials are still stored in the legacy format. So that's why I said you first have to migrate them and you can very specifically decide for each credential if you want to update it to the new metadata type or maybe if you just want to migrate all of your existing credentials or only shared credentials. Passport gives you complete control of everything and it is even fully backwards compatible with existing API automations or implementations by using the legacy metadata type. So that's it about the new secure architecture. I think this is really a great improvement just to show you what that looks on my system. So when I'm opening a credential that is still in the legacy format, passport will show a small upgrade button at the bottom. So when you click on that, you immediately see all the new functions and features of the encrypted metadata type that now appear in the menu. For example, the new appearance functions. So this allows you to change the color and the icon of your credentials. And I really like this because now it means you can better differentiate between the different types of credentials in a larger database. Before your only way to separate out things was using folders, which you still can use, but I just like to have a little more options for coloring and icons. And one thing I still would like to see though is the ability to upload custom icons. Yeah. Or maybe that passport automatically downloads the fav icons from the URIs. Uh but yeah, that's it about icons and colors. I also really like that you can now add multiple URIs. So before we only were able to add one URI per credential and this is really great because you often have multiple interfaces and portals for the same credentials. Just think about Microsoft where you have your regular Microsoft account website but also one drive, Azure, Office 365 and whatnot. So adding multiple URIs makes it just easier to use your credentials in these scenarios because you then automatically have autofillin for all these different addresses and portals. They also added a new description field which honestly I'm not really sure why you would need this. So because the legacy metadata type already had a node field but yeah maybe there is a technical reason for it. Uh but yeah, despite all these uh customizations and the security and quality of life improvements, for me personally, the most interesting feature they just added in the version 5.3. So this is really brand new. This is the support for custom fields. And this is absolutely amazing because now we are not just limited to the username, password, and note fields. Now we can add as many key value pairs as we want for each credential. And this is actually solving a big problem for me that I had with passport because I often want to store additional information for my credentials like API keys for example or databased passwords. For example, here you can see I'm storing the credentials for my Cloudflare account. When I want to log into the dashboard, I need username and password. But of course, I've also created many API tokens and secrets that I need for connecting my Terraform automations or for authenticating the DNS challenges when I'm issuing TLS certificates. In the past, I always had to create separate objects for these tokens and use the password fields. So now I have just one credential object and I can add as many custom fields as I want to store this type of information. This is really making things a lot easier for me. So yeah, that's why I absolutely love the latest version 5.3 of Passport. I'm really super excited about this password manager because it really gets better and better with every new update. To be perfect, there are just two more features that I'm desperately waiting for, which I already said in the beginning of this video. I just need the Safari browser extension. So please, Passport team, continue working on it. I need it. And I also want to see the new content types like pass keys or maybe SSH keys because this is something that already many other password managers can do. And I think this will make passport even more interesting for any home lab or tech people like us. But yeah, this just my personal opinion of course uh you might have a totally different view on this. So please tell me what do you think about the latest version of password. Have you ever thought about the security architecture of password managers and how important is self-hosting to you and how they are processing credentials? Let me know in the comments. And as always, thank you so much for watching, guys. A big thanks goes out to all the supporters on my YouTube channel. You guys are so amazing. And of course, I'm going to catch you in the next video. Take care. Bye-bye.

=== Publish a Static Hugo Website the EASY way // Cloudflare Workers ===

Hey everybody, this is Christian and today I'm finally publishing my new website. Remember the one that we built from simple markdown files using the Hugo static website builder in one of my recent videos. Of course, when it comes to publishing static websites, there are tons of ways how you could do this. You could just upload this to a basic web server, something like EngineX or Apache. But I wanted to go a step further and use the new serverless compute platform made by Cloudflare called Workers. Using this doesn't just make sure my web content gets delivered incredibly fast. It also allows me to automate the entire building and publishing process all integrated directly into a self-hosted GitLab instance. It's absolutely insane. So, I hope I got you interested. Let's see how this all works. Now, before we jump in, I quickly want to show you my absolute favorite tool for remote access that is called Twinate, the sponsor of today's video. Twing is always my first choice when I need to log into my HomeLab servers over SSH or access my administrative tools because it is a secure, fast, and simple solution that just works like magic. It's what we call ZTNA intake, a zero trust network access platform that establishes secure connections between your devices where every connection always have to be verified and authorized. So otherwise, Twing won't let the request go through. And this technology even works through Nut devices and firewalls without any port forwarding or firewall exception required. So you can just securely log into your devices no matter where you are or where you're connecting from. If you would like to try it out, then check out my tutorials on how to install Twinate in your network and how to integrate it into your Docker, Kubernetes, or even DevOps environment by using Terraform. It is super cool and completely free with up to five users and connects to 10 different remote networks. So, start making your network more secure and accessible with Twin. So everything uh that I need to host or upload to the Cloudflare's uh infrastructure has to been first built from the source code using the Hugo Steady website builder and everything that I need for that is again in this git repository. So here you can see the basic configuration files for the Blowfish Hugo theme that I've used to design my website. Remember uh in my first video about Hugo, we've gone through all the details. So if you're interested in the first part, then check it out. I'll put it to you in the description. And here you can also see the content of my website or the video tutorials or pages that I want to publish are existing in simple markdown files that the Hugo Study website framework will use to create the final website code from. I've tried to add as much as I can from my recent YouTube videos that I've done in the last few months is so that my new website isn't empty in the beginning. And certainly I'm also thinking about adding more content to it or even automating the content creation process for new videos and pages as well. I haven't really decided how exactly I'm going to do this. Maybe I could integrate this into my project management platform with notion. So, we'll see how that's working. I'll keep you updated about it. And uh by the way, one thing that I have not shown you in the previous video, which I probably should have done, is in the Hugo tutorial, we only used the Hugo server command, which is great for developing on my local device and testing how the final website would look like. But of course, when we want to host the website, we cannot do it this way. We first of all need to build the final HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files and only upload these files to the web server. In our case, the Cloudflare workers infrastructure. We don't need the whole source code there. To build the website with a Hugo static framework, you just simply run the Hugo command without the server flag. It's really super easy. And this will create all the necessary files and put them into the public folder. That's basically all we need to upload somewhere. But of course, as promised, we'll also take a look how to automate this building process in Cloudflare Workers. Because as you know guys, I'm lazy and whenever I change something on my website, I don't want to always rebuild it and upload it. myself. Yeah. So that's why I added this public folder and also resource folder to the git ignore file and not include it in the repository. But yeah, I hope this gives you a good understanding of the project um and how I'm planning to build the website. Now when it comes to hosting the final code, as I said again in the intro, you have many options. And I have decided to use Cloudflare Workers because I'm already using Cloudflare for many of my projects. Yeah, it's my primary public DNS provider. I'm using it for uh the DNS challenge when I'm creating TLS certificates and I'm also doing the one or another project with Cloudflare Zero Trust. If some of you might ask now why I'm using the more complex server architecture Cloudflare workers instead of Cloudflare pages which is pretty well known and you find many tutorials on the internet on Cloudflare pages. Well, the main reason is that uh once I tried to do that, Cloudflare started recommending using workers instead of pages for new projects, which probably implies that they are going to replace pages somewhere in the future. And of course, I didn't want to use a technology that is not recommended by the vendor anymore. And also I really like that Cloudflare Workers has a free plan for personal use and simple applications that includes a bunch of free requests and computing resources. Of course, if you need more, you can still upgrade to the paid plans, but I think for a simple static website like mine, the free plan in Cloudflare Workers should be totally sufficient and a great way to do it. I get global CDN performance, automatic scaling, and the ability to easily customize my site's behavior without managing the actual servers. So we are not spinning up virtual machines or containers or we are not managing any complex infrastructure. Let just Cloudflare handle all that stuff that they are really good at. So this just works and again that is for free. So what would be a better choice? Now let's get started and let's talk about how we actually get my website source code onto Cloudflare Workers. The most streamlined way to do this is through a Git integration. Yeah, Cloudflare workers can automatically build and deploy your web project whenever you push changes to a git repository, which really fits well into my preferred workflow. However, there is a bit of a limitation in cloud for workers because currently there is only a direct integration into GitHub or gitlab.com existing the hoster platforms. That means if you like me running your own self-hosted GitLab instance, you cannot directly connect it to Cloudflare workers. But don't worry, there's a simple workar around. We can use what's called a mirrored repository in GitLab. So in addition to the Git repository on my Salforce, the GitLab platform, I've also created a repository with the same name on the public platform gitlab.com, of course, as a private repository. And then I just need to configure a mirrored repository that pushes any changes from my local git repo to the public git repo. As you can see the public repository on githlab.com is currently empty. So we first of all need to configure the mirroring. Go to settings repository and then there is a section mirroring repositories. Here you can add a new repository where you want to push the changes. By the way the mirror direction currently is grayed out. In the paid versions of GitLab you can also pull any changes from a public one to your local one. But anyways, we just need the push u mirror direction anyways. So I'm totally fine with that. So first of all, we need to input the remote repository URL and that can be done by either using HTTP or SSH or git. So depending on how you want to authenticate. If you want to authenticate uh using a username and password, then use HTTPS. You can also use SSH keys. So that way you just have to switch the authentication method to SSH public key. And then you will need to download the public key that is generated on your local GitLab platform. Upload the public key to gitlab.com and then you should also be able to authenticate.
