=== Manage Checkmk IT monitoring in Ansible ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N2E7k7s0I44

Hey everybody, this is Christian and today I'm going to show you how to automate the IT monitoring platform Czechm by using Ansible. Because if you remember in my first Czechm tutorial, we had covered some of the basics about installing the Czechm monitoring solution in my home lab. We've added some hosts, activated some services, and while that approach was really great for a smaller setup like my home lab, some of you reached out to me asking how to actually handle this at scale. So if you have a larger infrastructure with many different servers and things you want to monitor, installing and configuring everything manually becomes a huge pain and a lot of work. So that's exactly why in this video we are taking a look at the Czechm anible collection. For this video, I've created some anible playbooks that basically take everything that we did in the first tutorial, but turn them into a fully automated process that you can easily replicate for your entire infrastructure. Trust me, once you see how easy it is to automate this entire process, you will never want to go back to clicking through the web interface again. So, let's dive in. And of course, a huge thanks goes out to Czechm for sponsoring this video. All right, guys. So, just a few words before we start with this tutorial. So, this here is the GitHub page for the Czechm collection. You can see that it's basically using the Czech case rest API in the background which means it shares a lot of similarities to the API when it comes to the fields values and structure of the requests which you will later see in this tutorial is pretty useful. It's also worth saying that this collection is an open-source community project of Czechm. So there is no commercial support for it and Czechm does not guarantee proper functionality. But on the other hand, everybody is welcome to submit issues or pull requests for changes or new features to this community project. And I can also say from my personal experience that I didn't run into any issues. Overall, I had a great experience with the Ansible collection for Czechm and the team was really super responsive and helpful whenever I had questions for reviewing this video. However, in case of NVME drives or SSDs, those can take much higher temperatures. NVME is sometimes over 60 or 80°. And this is completely normal for this type of drive which is by the way um a problem I don't seem to be the only person is having. === Building a static website in Markdown with Hugo ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MX4yy1dTVYg

Hey guys, this is Christian and today we're taking a look at the new website that I'm currently building for my YouTube channel. Yes, I'm finally working on a new web project where I'd like to publish all of the videos that I'm making, including some notes, resources, and links. And I just thought it might be interesting to document this entire process for you and show you exactly how I'm building this in a brand new video series. Because if you had asked me some time ago about how to create a simple website, I probably would have told you to just use one of the most commonly used content management systems for websites called WordPress. But these days that has changed a little. Not just because of all of the news that were around WordPress a few months ago. No, honestly, I've never been a huge fan of the WordPress framework. I just didn't know any better way to build a website because WordPress is really a huge pain to manage in my opinion and you have to find ways to constantly optimize and maintain it. It is really horrible and takes a lot of time and effort. So for creating my new website, I've recently been searching for a much better framework that would allow me to build a website that is much simpler, requires less maintenance, and is out of the box super fast and reliable. And I found it. The magic word is static websites. And that is exactly what I'm going to show you in this video. Today, we'll build the foundation for my new static website using the Hugo framework. I tell you everything you have to know to simply get started and create beautiful websites that are super fast. And don't worry, you don't have to be an expert in HTML or CSS at all. This is all working by just using simple textbased markdown files. Sounds great? Well, then keep watching and let's take a look at the Hugo framework together. Before we do that though, I quickly want to show you another incredible tool that I use for remote access called Twingate, the sponsor of today's video. Whenever I'm connecting to one of my remote servers over SSH or accessing my administrative tools in my home lab, I use the zerorust network access platform Twinate because this is just so much better and more secure than traditional VPNs. And it is even easier to deploy because Twing allows me to securely connect to my local network from anywhere, even through Nut devices and my local firewall without any port forwarding or firewall exceptions that I would have to enable. It is super smart and furthermore it verifies each access request that leaves my devices. Everything has to be authorized otherwise Twinate won't let the request go through. I've already done many tutorials and showed you how to install it using Docker or integrated in Kubernetes or on your DevOps environments using Terraform. So, it's a huge recommendation from my side. And if you want to check it out, it is completely free for up to five users and connects to 10 different remote networks. So, what are you waiting for? you start making your network more secure and accessible with Twin Gate. Of course, I'll drop your link to this in the description box down below. All right, guys. So, now let's get on with building the foundation for my new website. Before we start, I quickly want to give you a little more context about static websites and why I'm doing it this way. Because some of you might not be familiar with the distinction between a CMS, a content management system like WordPress, and a static website. Now at its core, every website consists of three main technologies. HTML, the hypertext markup language, which tells your browser what the content of the website is like text, images, links, forms, etc. Then we have CSS cascading stylesheets that controls the presentation or styling of the website. And nearly all modern websites also have a third technology called JavaScript on board. So this adds behavior and interactivity to your website allowing you to dynamically add content updates, add animations, user interactions, these kind of things. And generally, if you want to host a website somewhere, you also have to have a web server, of course. So that delivers all of these files to your browser where they're finally interpreted and presented to you as a user. So this is what we already call a static website where the entire content consists in these free file formats. However, if you dynamically want to make updates to these websites, like if you have multiple people working on publishing articles or if you want to store any kind of user information like loginins, email addresses or maybe products uh in an online shop, you also need a database and that often comes together with a CMS, a content management system. So this is a system that takes the request from the user and then first makes a query in a database where all of the information and the content is stored in and then it generates the actual HTML code using its processing language and then finally the CMS delivers the HTML code together with the CSS and JavaScript to the browser. Before anybody says it in the comments of course this is a very simplified workflows. Technically, there can also be other technologies involved like CDNs and so on, but I just wanted to keep it simple to focus on the stuff that it actually comes down to when comparing a content management systems to static websites. By the way, WordPress is, as I said, one of the most commonly used CMS, but there are of course also others existing like Typo Free or Drupal that are probably a bit more wellknown. all have their pros and cons, but they all kind of share at least one big disadvantage over static websites. And some of you can already guess it because um it involves a database query and a processing language where code or files are generated whenever a user requests something. A content management system will always run slower than a static website. And furthermore, any content management system also add a lot more complexity to it. Like a database server alone might require additional computing resources and maintenance, more CPU power, more memory depending on how many user requests your web server gets and how well it is optimized for that. This can add some heavy latency to any websites, which is the reason why many unmaintained WordPress websites are ridiculously slow. Of course, it is important to say at this point that I know not all content management systems are slow by design. Yeah, there are many ways to optimize a CMS to make it nearly as fast as static websites. We already touched on CDN. You can also do caching, database query optimization and stuff like that. But you have to keep in mind this often requires still more resources, a better knowledge of the system, and regular maintenance of your database and content management systems. So that is basically the main difference. Yeah, keep in mind that it doesn't mean that you should stop using CMS at all and only deploy static websites. Yeah, there are obviously many good reasons to use databases because it's the only way to actually store user information somewhere on the server side. But if you're just building a a simple website that doesn't really need to store any user information, which by the way also makes a website better for privacy, a static website is in my opinion the best way to do it. However, I'm pretty sure that nobody wants to build or write static websites completely from scratch like HTML, CSS, JavaScript completely on your own. That would be horrible for maintaining it and super complicated to create and publish new content. So, so to work around this uh problem, there is a solution and that is called static website frameworks. Something like Hugo is. So this handles some of the complexity of generating the HTML and CSS code for you but without requiring a database and serverside processing when the user requests the page. So whenever you change the content of the website the build process is happening automatically in your CI/CD pipelines when you make the change and only the final HTML, CSS and JavaScript files are uploaded to the web server. So when a user requests a website, no processing or database query need to happen. Uh it will just deliver the latest files that were uploaded to the server. So this is super cool, super fast and also interesting to integrate it uh with CSC and DevOps practices. Now the last part we don't cover in this video otherwise it probably would be 2 hours or 3 hours long. But of course I'm working on a part two for this project because I want to build my website. Yeah. So in part two we will cover exactly this automation and how to publish the website and deliver it to the worldwide web. Uh today we'll just start with the basics and we'll check out the Hugo framework on their official website gohugo.io. So there you can get access to all of the files and the documentation. Hugo by the way is an open source project that offers great speed and flexibility. I absolutely love this and it really makes website creation super simple and fun. Of course, there are many other aesthetic website generators like Hugo around like GitHubs Jackal for example, but um I just absolutely love Hugo because I found it to be the most simple um framework for creating my website. All right, so let's check out how to build a website with Hugo. I'm sorry for the long introduction, but I think sometimes it is useful to have enough background to understand why I'm actually doing it this way and not use a content management system. Anyway, so to build a website with Hugo, you first of all have to install the Hugo framework to your local device. So you can install it on any operating system like Mac OS, Linux, Windows, or even BSD. Uh in my case, I'm using Mac OS as you probably should know. And it is super easy uh to install because you can use the homebrew package manager, but there are also other ways possible like building it from source and so on. One thing that might be important to mention at this point is that Hugo comes in different types of versions and it has an extended version that has many features that some themes require to function properly. So when you're using Homebrew in Mac OS that automatically installs the extended version of Hugo to your local device and this is also the way that I would recommend it to install. So just run brew install Hugo and that's it. You can also add a specific version but um the latest version should also be fine. All right. So once we have installed the Hugo CLI tools, we first of all need to go to one directory where you want to store the website. And to install a new website, you simply just run the command Hugo new uh site and then just give it a name. That will also be the name of the folder that will be generated. In my case, uh I'm just going to call it my my new website. I think that's fine. And then we cd into this directory and open it in VS code so we can easily add content and change the settings of the website. Yeah, the public and the resource directory. In simple terms, it contains everything you just have to upload to your web server to publish your website. And then there's also the themes directory. The fast renderer feature is a performance optimization that Hugo uses during the development. But sometimes it hides errors or shows incorrect previews. So if you want to make significant changes, if you're building the website completely from scratch, uh disabling the fast renderer guarantees that uh the website that you see is 100% accurate preview of what the final website will look like. And also the no HTTP cache is important. If you make changes to CSS, JavaScript or image files that ensures that you always see the latest version of those assets in your browser. Without the browser might continue to use the cached versions. So you wouldn't see the changes that you're making and that might be a bit confusing here and there. === NEW GitLab CI CD components explained ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pwRWXCdbDVo

Hey everybody, this is Christian and today I'm going to show you how to simplify all of your CI/CD pipelines in GitLab by using components. This feature was introduced in GitLab version 17 and it allows you to create reusable pipelines that can be used across your entire GitLab platform. So if you have similar types of automation jobs that you always want to use across different projects, for example, deploy a docker compos file on a remote server or manage your infrastructurees as code with terraform, you can define these type of jobs in a gitlab cicd component and just use this in all your project pipelines without having to rewrite the same code all the time. And this is really great to reduce redundancies and it simplifies updates to all of your pipelines because if you want to change something in your GitLab CI/CD jobs, you just have to update this one component and it automatically becomes live for all the projects where this is used in. I just recently found out this super cool trick and I thought I just going to show it to you because it will make your CI/CD live in GitLab much more enjoyable. I mean if you're enjoying weird things like this at all. But either way, I think it is a super interesting project where you can learn a lot of cool stuff and valuable skills about IT automation in GitLab. So, let's check out CI/CD components. Before we do that though, I quickly want to show you another interesting component that I'm using for IT automation in my home lab called Kestra. Kra is a powerful and simplified workflow orchestrator that helps you to run automations for all of your favorite tools and APIs in one centralized platform. For example, you can use it to run scripting languages like Python, Node.js or PowerShell. It supports cloud providers and automation tools like Anible or Terraform. Even AI technologies and many many more. It has a declarative language where you can build more powerful workflows by using triggers, loops, and logical operations. It is super cool. Castra supports over 600 different plugins allowing you to build automated workflows with unlimited possibilities and visualizes this all in a realtime monitoring dashboard where you can drill into execution metrics, debug logs, timebased diagrams and so many more things. And the best is Castra has an open source version that you can just deploy using Docker, Kubernetes or any other tool in your home lab completely for free. I've already made a few video tutorials about Castra. So definitely check them out for a full deep dive. And if you're ready to use it in your company environment, then check out the Castra's Enterprise Edition that has many more practical features for professional environments. So just talk to them. They really have a good support. And of course, I'll link you the website and my video tutorials about Castra in the description box down below. So check it out. Okay guys, so let's get started with GitLab CI/CD components. I hope you've all watched the first video tutorial about GitLab CI/CD where I've covered all of the basics such as creating the GitLab CI.jml file stages and jobs. So if you're unfamiliar with how GitLab CI/CD works in general, it might be challenging to follow this one. So definitely check out the first video tutorial if you haven't it already. Of course, the link is also in the description. But now let's get started. And uh if we take a look at the official documentation about GitLab CI/CD components. So you can see that as I've told you this is a relatively new feature that was introduced to create a reusable single pipeline configuration unit which can be used as a smaller part of a larger pipeline and it can be configured with input parameters for a more dynamic behavior. So something like variables that you can define separately for each projects with a different value where you're using the component. These components are used in other GitLab CI pipelines by just using the include keyword. So this is similar to how you do it with GitLab CI templates if you know this. But um compared to templates, components have several advantages. So first of all, a component can be listed in the GitLab CI/CD catalog. So for example, if you check out the catalog of the public gitlab.com platform, for example, you can see all the components that other people have created and published to the catalog. And in case of a self-managed GitLab CI/CD platform, so for your home lab, you can also use this, but it is then of course just existing across your local GitLab environment. Now, the second advantage components have over templates is that you can release components with a specific version. So, whenever you're including CI/CD components in other pipeline jobs, you can always refer to a specific version tag and that gives you much better control over update management of the components. So, suppose you want to update your CI/CD component that does something in your pipeline. In that case, the project pipeline will still run with the version that was configured, which is excellent if you want to ensure that components update aren't breaking anything inside your critical project pipelines. But you can also use it to simplify your update procedure because you can also use the latest version tag in your include instructions, which means whenever you release a new version of the CI/CD component, it automatically updates to the latest version in all of the job project pipelines. And this is what I personally do in almost all of my projects because then I only need to make this one update to my CI/CD components to change something and I don't have to touch all the other 30 or 40 projects where I'm using this and that really saves a lot of time especially in a self-managed GitLab platform where you're working on multiple different projects. Okay, so this is how components work in general. And if you remember the GitLab CI/CD catalog that I just showed you, um, there I had one question regarding the self-managed GitLab platform. So, do I really have to write all of these components myself or could I also just import existing CI/CD components from the public catalog on gitlab.com to my self-managed platform? And unfortunately, this does not work, at least yet at the time of recording this video. might come as a new feature in the future, but uh for now this does not work. What I've done is I've written all of my CI/CD components myself. Actually, there are just three of them, but it was really a great learning experience and also the reason why I'm making this video to show you how the process works. Okay, so here you see myself manage GitLab platform. As you can see, some of these projects here have a tag called CI/CD catalog project. And let me walk you through the process how to create these components. First of all, you have to create a group in your self-managed GitLab platform that is called components. So just create a new group. It doesn't matter if this is a private or public or internal repository. Just make sure that you name it components and then if you have that group, you can jump in there and create new projects for all of your components. Uh by the way, I will show you all of these three components and how they work in a lot more detail. But first of all, let's go through some of the basics how to create such a component. === Tech project management and documentation in Notion ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D93lUj5Mj0U

Hey everybody, this is Christian and today I'm going to show you how I organize all of my tech projects and documentation for my home lab. This is super important to me because as you might have seen in my last home lab tour, I'm working on so many different things using various technologies and without keeping track of everything, I would be completely lost. Let's be honest. And I thought I can't be the one and only guy struggling with this, keeping an overview of my tech projects and documentations. So, I made this video to show you all of my tricks for taking technical notes and my brand new task tracking system with a timeline in Canon board, which helps me to plan everything much better and work more efficiently. I just have to warn you, some of it might be a bit overkill for a smaller home lab, but I can just tell you from my own experience, to me, it has been really helpful. And trust me guys, if you can apply some of these tricks to manage projects in your tech company's infrastructure, every product manager will be jealous about it. Before we jump right into it and I show you my system, I quickly want to thank Notion for sponsoring this video. Notion is my platform of choice for tech documentation and project management because it has all of the features that I need. And real quickly, let me give you some tips if you're entirely new to Notion. First of all, you should check out the official website, notion.com, and sign up with an account. Don't worry, they even have a free plan that includes almost all of the important features that you need to manage home projects and taking notes. It even allows you to invite 10 guests to your account and retain a 7 days page history only if you need some advanced features like unlimited blocks for teams or file uploads, a longer page history or synchronized databases with third party apps which by the way is a super useful feature for managing GitHub issues and PRs in notion or if you need custom automations, charts, dashboards or notion AI then you have to upgrade it. So there are really some useful features for notion power users. But just to let you know, the free plan might already be enough for many many people. So just try it out. It's really a great package in my opinion. And also to get up to speed quickly, you should also take a look at some of the notion templates. They have a large collection for various types of use cases where you can learn some of the basic functionalities and elements of this tool. So yeah, just take a look. I don't want to spend too much time talking about the basic features because I'm sure we all know how to use note takingaking tools, right? Instead, I'd like to show you my notion space and give you some insights into how exactly I'm using it for managing my home lab projects and my knowledge base or technical documentation. So, here in my notion space, I've organized everything in pages and databases. Databases, by the way, are in my opinion the most impressive and useful feature in notion. So here on the left sidebar you can see my main structure beginning with notes which I use for writing down my private stuff or keeping track of my workflows and meetings I attend or storing templates. Then we have one section where I manage my YouTube videos and tasks that I have by the way explained a little in my previous notion video. So if you're interested in that, I link you the first video in the description box down below. So then we have my home lab section. We will go into it in a second. You can embed many different types of documents including Google Drive links, PDFs or many other thirdparty programs just like Excalibraw. And whenever these files update, it automatically also updates in notion as well. So this is really nice to integrate Notion into other tools that you love using. Down below, I'm also keeping notes of my IP address management and I write down all of my network zones and IP ranges in my HomeLap network. Some of you might think it is a little too much to write down all of the IP addresses of any device that you're deploying. But I can just tell you from my own experience. Whenever I've worked with any kind of computer network, not just in my home lab, also when I was working as an IT service technician, this is one of the most important things to keep track of, especially in larger infrastructures with many different network segments because IP addresses, they don't frequently change, but any IP conflict can cause huge problems in a computer network. Trust me, I know what I'm talking about. I've gone through this a couple of times. So you definitely you want to make sure that you're not assigning the same IP address to multiple devices in your network. That's by the way one reason why I also differentiate between a static IP range and DHCP ranges. Maybe at someday I need to make a separate video about network planning or management. I'm not going too deep into this today. But anyways, for these type of simple notes, I mostly use the integrated tables feature in notion, which allows you to create new rows and columns in between, move things around quickly, and some of this stuff. So, it does not have as many features at you might know it from something like Excel, but it is definitely enough for these simple things where you just need to write down IP addresses or in other areas of my documentation, I also use it to write down deployments or virtual machine resources. This is where notion tables are really great. But if you want to do some more complex tracking of items with for example different cell formattings or uh visualizing different things or filtering then I would suggest using notion databases instead. So let's return to my main home lab page where you can see these two databases. By the way, you can create databases in notion as an inline database just as you see here or you create them as separate subpages. I personally prefer using inline databases slightly more because it allows you to place multiple databases on a single page which is not possible with full sub databases in my home page. The first database is a comprehensive list of all the projects that I'm currently working on or what I have previously worked on in my home lab sack. I've decided that every technology is its own project which you could of course also do in a different or simpler way. However, I thought uh having a separate project for each technology that allows me to better structure all of my nodes is since each of these technologies have multiple deployments, resources or tasks that I want to track. So, it just made sense for me to differentiate all of these technologies as separate projects. But yeah, feel free to build your own way of organizing things. Notion databases offer you many capabilities to build a structure that fits your style and requirements. For example, this project board here is just the main view of the database that I prefer as a layout. It is actually a gallery using small cards without a preview because I like this quick overview here with the different cards uh where I can just click on something and jump straight into these projects. However, I've also created different types of use um such as the project list here which reveals some additional details or um elements of these items in the database such as a description that I try to keep track for every project or the status or also the link to my self-hosted git repository for this project because I usually also create a separate git repository on my self-hosted GitLab platform for each of these projects. Again, just build it the way you like. Uh, Notion offers many different types of fields like text fields, numbers, selections, date, time, but also some cool AI autofilled fields or relations to other databases. And by the way, you can create as many views for these databases as you want. So, with different layouts depending on what type of visualization makes the most sense here. Furthermore, you can also apply different sorts of filtering, groupings, sortings, and even automations to create a highly customized experience that fits perfectly your project management structure or whatever you want to use notion databases for. I absolutely love this for storing my home projects. And what is even better, you can use this notion database to store additional information about these projects because every item in a database can have its own page and subpages as well. Let's have a look at one of these projects. For example, let's go into GitLab, which is definitely one of my largest projects uh I'm working on in my home lab where I took many notes because GitLab can get pretty complex from time to time. And if you've seen some of my GitLab tutorials, you definitely know what I'm talking about. So here, for example, you can see I'm writing down everything that is related to this GitLab project. Usually I'm starting always with a quick description of what this project is about followed by deployments which in my case usually are Docker compos deployments or Kubernetes charts or manifests. I always do this when I deploy a technology in my home lab stack because I have many different virtual machines, clusters and other devices. And uh when I quickly want to know for each projects where exactly I have deployed what I can just take a look at this notion database and I know exactly where everything is. For instance, when we quickly switch to my traffic project um you can see that I have deployed multiple traffic instances or you can see a similar documentation for the twin gate project. This really helps me to have a good overview of everything and the related resources. And for some of these projects, I also take additional notes. So the first section of the project page is usually for keeping a list of all the deployed resources and references. And the second section is for my notes and knowledge base. I can't emphasize this enough. A good knowledge base or documentation about all the projects that you're doing is so freaking important in it. Every professional should build something like this because sometimes you just have to write down these things and keep track of configuration settings or code snippets, links to relevant articles or documentations that you might need in the future again. And Notion has some great elements and functions for taking technical notes such as code blocks with syntax highlighting or these small call out boxes that you can use to highlight certain elements, things that you need to be aware of. And you can also, by the way, use the markdown syntax um just like you know it from other tools like VS Code to quickly format text into headings, tables, code blocks, and checklists, for example. Also pretty useful. And another great tip that I can give you, whenever you're writing down notes or documentation, you should also always use a proper heading structure because when you move your mouse to the right sidebar, Notion displays an outline of the page where you can quickly jump into the section that you're looking for. And of course, you can better find all these things when you use meaningful names and a structure. It's similar to using variables or descriptions in programming. Don't worry, I think you don't have to do this for all of the projects in the same detail. So sometimes it's just enough to add a simple link to relevant places in the documentation. So just don't try to copy the official documentation. That really doesn't make any sense. But it's useful to just write down things that you believe might be useful for you to remember in future projects. And of course, you also like better understanding stuff when you're doing researching and from time to time of writing some quick notes or bullet points about the stuff that you're learning. When you do this regularly, you also have a great advantage when you're researching new technical topics because the latest release of Notion AI added a brand new research function, which is amazing for creating new and polished Notion documents. Because Notion AI has access to all of your databases and pages, plus the web search. And of course, the more stuff you're documenting about your home lab and projects, the more intelligent and useful the AI answers become. For example, here I'm using the new research mode in notion AI to generate a full documentation report for my current home lab, which includes many details about the hardware and networking setup, software architecture, maintenance, security measures, and anything else that I've written down, which by the way, Notion AI completely figure out on its own what is important and what not. And the cool thing is that because I'm also planning my YouTube videos in a notion database, the notion AI had also access to all of the YouTube video scripts and combining this with my home lab documentation from the database that really generated a comprehensive and correct report of everything that I have currently deployed and that is important in my home lab. It's really impressive. However, you have to keep in mind that this research mode can also take quite a long time uh because it indexes many different sources and it's using a reasoning model to create a plan and it also iterates through this planning process a couple of times. So in my case, it took about 5 to 6 minutes to generate this report. But just imagine you would have to read through all of the pages and databases yourself to generate such a report. It literally saves you hours of time which makes the research mode of notion AI in my opinion the most practical feature for us technical people. But of course notion AI also has other cool features like you can ask it anything about your notion content in databases or pages. And this response is by the way super fast. And you could also use some of the external AI models like GBD or claude for researching and asking questions. I bet you all know how to work with AI in general, right? But also, if you're attending to technical meetings, notion AI has also a nice feature that summarizes everything that was discussed in these meetings called AI meeting notes. And yeah, these are all of the reasons why I personally love to use Notion for my technical project documentation and management and why I'm always taking so many notes on all these projects because then I can later use Notion AI to better research new topics and find the information that I'm looking for. Maybe I'm a little crazy and sometimes I probably go a bit too far. I have to admit it. However, um I've always realized that over time by adding more and more projects to my home lab, it really has become more and more complex for me. And to avoid getting overwhelmed by all the complexity or losing focus on things that I want to work on, I even had to extend this system and add another database to track and plan my activities because technical documentation is one thing, but planning actions and project management is the other. And I will shortly explain why I built this type of complex system for me. Because whenever I have to do things like updating stuff, migrating stuff or fixing issues, the first thing that I tried is to um add a simple checklist on all these individual project pages in the notion database. But the problem with that was that when you try to maintain multiple checklists across different subpages, you totally forget about them because you don't have a good all-in-one overview of all the checklists at one place. You always have to go into each of these subpages and then you just can't really plan any tasks across multiple projects. So therefore, I came up with a different solution. I thought it would be a good idea to extend my first HomeLab project database with a second database where I just track all of the actions and tasks that I'm working on. For this, I'm using another super cool feature in Notion because it allows you to add a relation field to connect items across multiple Notion databases. In my case, each item in the task database below is assigned to a single project. And I always create a new task for any action. uh where I need to write down notes or where I need to plan this in the future such as migrations, enhancements to existing deployments or resources, bugs that I notice or evaluations of new technologies I want to take a look at for making new videos of course and I always prioritize them as high, medium or low items depending on how important these things are to me. Of course, I have also created multiple views for this task database to get a better overview of everything in a list that is grouped by priorities or what I absolutely love to use is a canban board. Yeah, this is my most favorite type of visualization because it allows you to have different cards of these task items and you can put them in different buckets for example based on the status level. That's highly common uh by the way in software development or project management in general. There were mainly two advantages the Unrade platform has over other system that convinced me to use it. And the first one is that unrated boots from a USB drive. So that means in small environments, especially when you're using motherboards that don't have so many PCI Express slots, you can save one M2 slot that you typically would use for an MVME drive that would contain the operating system. Because Unre is booting from a USB drive, you can use that M2 slot for an additional controller card or any other device. This gives you, in my opinion, more possibilities for connecting additional hard drives or adding another network interface card. And the second advantage was the unrid array. So this is a proprietary um array solution that has and I've not seen anything else um like that in other system. I already explained in my unrated storage server video why I love this unrated array so much. But to quickly summarize this gives me the ability to build a large redundant cold storage pool that I can use for backups, archive storage or storing large video files. and it has the ability to spin down the discs that are not in use so they don't consume any power and that means you can build a more power efficient storage server. I agree that unred probably isn't the most mature NAS operating system in the world and many people dislike its pricing and licensing policy but I think for a HomeLab power user like me and has some pretty compelling and interesting features and this is the reason why I'm using it. So, the first two tools that I'm using here, Ansible and Open Tufu, connect many dots in my entire home lab environment. For example, I'm using Ansible as my main configuration management tool. So, this is helping me a lot with the maintenance, configuration, and installation tasks in my home lab. For example, I've created a repository with separate playbooks that install certain core services. This is another automation tool similar to Ansible but it does a few important things slightly different and uh previously I always used Terraform which was created by Hashiop and once Hashiop changed their open source licensing to a business source licensing I later on switched to open tofu but actually open tofu and terraform are mostly even in the way they work. So I heavily use open tofu for provisioning tasks. So whenever I need additional resources in my home app, I would have to create them manually through a web UI or config files that I used an open to terraform plugin to create these resources and define them in code files. Just a quick example, if I need a new virtual machine on Proxmox or I need a new DNS record or maybe I need both, I define these things in a Terraform file and then I use the open tofu automation tool to automatically apply these changes and make them happen on my infrastructure. And this has the huge advantage of that you always have a single source of truth of what's configured and provisioned on your infrastructure in a simple coile. So if you want to find out what's going on, you don't have to log into any web UI and click through graphical user interfaces to find out what you have done. You can just simply read it in the code. And this is in my opinion much easier and simpler to use. So these two automation tools, Ansible and Open Tufu become even better when they're combined with other logical and pipeline tools. For example, Castra is such a tool that I used to schedule certain automation tasks. So there are simple scripts in Castra that are triggered on specific events. For example, when something gets updated in a git repository or when I invoke it through a web hook call. So then I can execute some actions often by using anible or terraform or open to scripts. And this is really super cool. I know this is tough to explain in just a minute or two, but just to show you a very quick example. So I have created a scheduled pipeline in Castra that will automatically run every day at a specific time and this will download an Ansible playbook from one of my git repositories and then it just runs it in a simple anible container and in this anible playbook I have defined an action that will automatically purge some of my orphaned Docker images on my servers and that is solving a big problem that I had because whenever I was updating container images, the old images are still left on the server's discs and over time they take up so much space that the virtual machine discs gets completely full resulting in the server to crash or to stop working and that's why I regularly run this simple anible playbook every day to clean up the old images on my docker hosts. It is a very simple use case but it caused a lot of problems in the past for me and by combining all these automation tools Kestra Ansible and I also have done the same for Terraform tasks and this is really automating a lot of the critical workloads that I would have to do manually in the home lab. So it's really saving a lot of time. However, I have to admit the only problem that I sometimes have with Castra is that it is using its own declarative language. So it has some special functions and in some of the early versions they really changed a lot of the syntax back and forth. So it has some sort of a learning curve especially when you try to write some more complex logic inside a Castra automation flow. You have to learn these functions the syntax and how to use this tool. And the free and open source version of Castra also doesn't have all of the features that I would personally love to use like a secret store or the new application feature. There are some cool things in it, but unfortunately locked behind the enterprise version, but it's still a pretty versatile tool that I probably have to use more in the future. Sema 4 UI, by the way, is also a similar thing like Kestra. It is a bit simpler and has fewer capabilities and functionalities. That's why I personally I'm not actively using Zma 4 UI anymore. I just have it in my stack because if something changes, if they introduce new exciting features, of course, I want to keep up to date with it and I might do another video about it. So, just like in Castra, you can use it to accomplish similar things like running a Terraform automation pipeline that is scheduled or triggered by certain actions. You can even run Python scripts and anible playbooks. But uh the problem with ZMA for UI is that it's more like a a web UI to just execute one script at a time or at a specific event. It's not like Castra where you can define a more complex logic and create your own scripts inside these automation pipelines. So I think Zap for UI is is a nice automation tool, but Castra is certainly much more powerful even the open source version of it. However, both tools also certainly compete with another automation platform in my home lab. And I'm still not sure which tool would be my favorite here, Castra or this one. But of course, I'm talking about my code management platform, GitLab. And yeah, it is in the category code management, but GitLab is uh honestly much more than a code management platform, even though code management is really the primary use case of it. So that's why I put it into this category. So in GitLab, I'm managing all of my deployments, the configurations, and the automations of my Hoblab in certain projects or repositories. Previously, I made a lot of this stuff publicly available on GitHub. So you guys could look into my GitHub repositories and find out what I'm running and how exactly I'm running it. However, I replaced the public GitHub repository of my home lab in favor of my new self-hosted GitLab instance because I just like the idea of keeping everything in my private environment a little more because GitLab offers a super cool automation platform called GitLab CI/CD that I started to explore recently and this already handles a lot of my automation logic for my application deployments. For example, you can see I've created a git repository for most of the video projects and tools that I use in my home lab. For instance, if you just go into my DNS repository. So here you will find the docker compos project for my bind 9 DNS server that I'm using on one of my docker hosts as well as the CI/CD pipelines that will automatically deploy the container or apply changes when I made an update to the compose file or another job that applies changes in the terraform files to the production configuration. Again, um I explained that I'm managing my DNS records with open tofu in simple terraform files. And the cool thing is when I now want to add a new DNS record, I change it in the code and I push this to my GitLab repository. It automatically triggers the CI/CD pipeline that will use a simple open tofu container to make these changes happening. So by the combination of open toufu and gitlab cicd I can literally automate everything and when I need new resources in my home lab I just have to make a simple change in a code file push it and the automation tools are automatically doing this and applying these configurations for me. So yeah just stay tuned for more videos about these kind of topics. I think there's some really good potential to show you some really cool stuff. By the way, um when we were talking about the Docker Compose project automation, I also shouldn't forget to talk about Renovate because Renovate, especially in combination with GitLab CCD, has completely simplified the entire container deployment for me and certainly also Kubernetes because Renovate automatically notifies you whenever a new version of a container image is pushed to the registry and it automatically creates a new pull request on GitLab. For example, if there is an update on to the traffic reverse proxy container images, renderate will automatically create this pull request with that version change to the GitLab management platform. So I can just simply click on merge to apply these changes. And guess what's now happening? Of course, because I'm using GitLab CI/CD automation that now automatically triggers a new job that uploads this new compos file with the updated container image to my remote server, restarts the container, and then my application is updated. So, this allows me to completely automate all of the versioning. So, I don't have to know or I don't need to look whenever there's a new version of an application released. I just get a notification for it. But I still have some manual control over when I want to definitely make this update happen because there are other automation tools like for example watchtower that I covered sometime in the past but they do that on their own. So if there is a new container image watchtower will just restart the container and maybe that's not what you want especially in a production environment. You don't want an application to be restarted in the middle of a critical process. So I think for these type of version updates, you have to have some manual control over it. And with GitLab CI/CD and Renovate, you can really simplify and automate this entire process while still having some manual control over when things are going to happen. So this is super cool. But I'm also running another small Git platform in my home lab called Git T. So this is again similar to GitLab, but in a much smaller scale. So, no, the next item on the list that I certainly will talk a lot more about is Twate. Twate, my main remote access tool. Actually, remote access tool doesn't describe it correctly because it's more than that. It's a full ZTNA solution, a zero trust network access solution. This means it is in contrast to traditional VPNs much more secure because it doesn't only include the encrypted network part. It also does the policy and compliance checks on all the connected devices and you can really fine-tune what user what device and in which compliance state has access to what particular service. Of course, there are also other great remote access tools that do kind of similar things, and I've reviewed a couple of them here on this channel as well, but Twin Gate just keeps to be my favorite solution for remote access and ZTNA because it has been super reliable to me. It never had any problems, outages. It just works on all of my devices. It is super easy to install and manage. And it also supports configuration and management through infrastructure as code IA automation tools. again Terraform, Open Tofu, Anible that makes it a very comfortable solution for me and fits great into my stack with all of the automation tools and it also has among the other tools a pretty compelling feature set even in the free tier. So that's why I'm mainly using Twin Gate whenever I need to remotely log into my HomeLab stack or get access to certain applications. I also absolutely love the next item on the list um that is authentic and this is an identity provider which replaces a lot of different authentication services like LDAP active directory open ID connect and it allows you to have a centralized user management in one single solution that you can integrate in many other applications for example I've integrated authentic to log into my GitLab platform or my potato instance so instead of having to manage different users and different credential credentials in all of these other applications separately. I can centralize everything and it comes with a lot of advanced features to increase the security of the login like it supports passwordless authentication. It supports two factor authentication with one-time passwords by using authentic as an identity provider in a login method to other applications. You can basically add passwordless and MFA to any application that you like. It's super cool. even the ones that don't have a login form by themselves because you can easily also integrate this in traffic reverse proxy to securely expose any application that you like and it also allows me to have a secure machine to-achine authentication. I have used this for something like the Castra web hooks or automation pipelines or even the networkwide access to something like Olama. It is super cool and a super important puzzle piece for my home lab security. But again, I could go on and talk about this for an hour, but I don't want to. So, definitely watch my separate videos about authentic. Uh because I also want to cover the next item on the list, Cloudflare. Another important puzzle piece because this is my main public DNS provider and security gateway, which means all of my certificates that are issued by Let's Encrypt use a DNS challenge and that is managed by Cloudflare. Without that, I wouldn't be able to issue TLS certificates for my traffic resource proxies and so on. and I'm also managing all of my public DNS records with it. Of course, I'm not managing them in the Cloudflare web UI. I also uh use the Cloudflare Terraform provider to configure all of these things in infrastructure as code and I'm also sometimes using Cloudflare tunnels for some very specific things. Cloudflare tunnels by the way is another feature of the Cloudflare Ztna platform. So, this is a competitive service to Twing. uh but I see them as two separate things here because twing is mostly there for providing access to internal resources to managed users like you have to log into Twinate you have to authenticate and get access to it with Cloudflare tunnels you can securely publish a service using ZTNA but to any unauthenticated users as well so to the public internet and this is the way how I'm protecting some of the uh web hook endpoints in my local network that need to be accessed from other public API endpoints. So when I need to publish an internal service but put it behind a strong authentication and protection, I'm using Cloudflare tunnels to do that. And certainly besides that, it also handles the important DNS part, the certificates and so on. So Cloudflare super important. Last but not least, I also have a small instance of passport in my home lab, which is a self-hosted password manager. So, I'm currently not actively using this for my private passwords. First of all, I don't like to have my important private passwords and credentials in a self-hosted environment because whenever something happens to my home lab rack, well, I would be screwed. So, that's why I mainly use Apple passwords. It is better integrated into my Apple devices and it's automatically synchronized. I can easily share it with my wife and my son. So, that's the reason for private things. I'm not using a self-hosted password manager, but I think where passport is super useful for this is really great for storing credentials in a self-hosted environment. It's super secure by design and it's great to share credentials or secrets in a team or company, especially the latest version of Passport 5, which was recently released. By the way, I'm also preparing a new video about Passport version 5 with some of the cool new features that it comes with like encrypted metadata, not only for passwords, but also user accounts, URIs, and notes are encrypted and secured. And they're also working on some pretty cool features like adding a key value store as well. And I think for these type of use cases, a self password manager is great. So to use this as a credential management system for your home lab. So if you need to store the user login for your Proxmox node or the API token for automation web hook tokens and these type of things that's what Apple password is not really great for and passport is much better for these type of things. So on the right side you have the hardware specs of the Zemo board in the first version with the Intel Celeron processor N3450 which already was kind of a bit outdated at the time of release. So now they put a new and much faster CPU in this thing, the Intel N150 CPU. Of course, I've also done some benchmarks on this thing that I later want to show you and we'll also talk about the power consumption, so don't worry about that. Uh, but yeah, you can already see where they're going with the new ZMO 2. Just take a look at the cache and the clock speed of this new CPU. It's much more powerful than the old one. And they also upgraded the memory from 8 GB of DDR4 to 8 GB of DDR5. The internal eMMC disc still remains the same, but again, you can increase the storage of this little device by just connecting some HDDs or SSDs to the two SATA 3.0 ports. What I also think many people will love about this, they increased the speed of the network interfaces from 1 GB Ethernet to a dual 2.5 GB Ethernet interface, which is just incredible if you want to use this thing as a network gateway, router, or firewall. Of course, they also made some other minor improvements, for example, to the USB ports, which now support USB 3.1. And the open-ended PCI Express slot got a huge upgrade from PCI Express 2.0 to 3.0. So, you can really use this thing in combination with an additional controller card or a graphics card. I think you get a really incredible package here with the new Zemo 2. Okay guys, so I think that all sounds very promising and for me this new Zemo 2 really just comes right in time because I currently have a small problem in my home lab with the network architecture and home assistant where the Zobot 2 will be a nice solution for. Just to quickly explain what's the problem here. If you've watched my last video about the HomeLab tour that I gave you, you know that I have separated my home lab into two network segments. The private land zone and the production home lab zone where all of my servers are running. And the current issue that I have with this architecture is that when my home assistant container is running on a production server in the pro network, it does not have a direct connection to all the other devices located in my LAN zone, which particularly became a problem with the Apple HomeKit because if you want to connect some Apple HomePods, these little Siri mods, because with Home Assistant, you have to put them all in the same local network so they can find each other because the Apple HomeKit integration sends and receives some broadcast packets to the local network which is usually not routed across different network segments. So I thought the best decision to come around that problem in my current setup is to just separate the home assistant deployment and put this on a new small home server that is located in the same private network as the Siri HomePods and all the other Wi-Fi devices which is honestly a perfect task for this new ZMA board here. also because it is super easy to deploy these type of self-hosted applications and containers on the new ZMA operating system that comes pre-installed on the Zema board 2. Yeah, Zema just created their own operating system called Zemo OS which was originally introduced for the ZMA Cube. By the way, that device I've also reviewed here on this channel a while ago and at first I have to say I wasn't too excited about ZMOS, especially on the ZMA cube because it was really an early version that was missing some, at least in my opinion quite important features for NAS devices.
