Before that I want to show you my networking setup in a bit more detail because I think this is really the foundation of every home lab. So one thing was really important to me when I planned a new network architecture for my home lab and that was to get good hardware equipment for that. So, I'm talking about a managed switch and huge patch panels and a gateway. And the reason why this was so important to me is that it gives me a lot of flexibility when creating different zones and segments of my home lab, which is awesome for security. But furthermore, real networking hardware also allows you to experiment with certain technologies that are often used in professional IT environments such as VLANs, Lags, routing protocols, things that you usually can't virtualize in a good way. And if you really want to learn these things, you just need some equipment that support these technologies. For instance, I'm running ASOP's XGS2100 firewall as my primary gateway through which all the traffic between the network segments passes. It does a lot of cool things like malware scanning, port and application rules, IDs and IPS, so intrusion detection and prevention. And on top it has many more enterprisegrade security features. Then I also got the Sopos CS 110 switch which connects all of the different devices in my home lab. It is a 24 1 GB Ethernet port and four SFP plus port PoE switch, which is really great because then I don't need a power supply for my Raspberry Pies or the access points. And these two devices are really doing a phenomenal job. So until now there was really no reason to change anything here. The only problem that I have is that I'm running out of SFB plus ports because they are currently all in use for the 10 GB fiber optic connections between the firewall, the switch, and my Proxmox nodes and the NAS. So, at some point, I have to change something about this if I want to connect my Mac Studio with 10 GB, which kind of would be a great thing. So, maybe I just add another small aggregation switch for the 10 GB connections or so. I'm still waiting for Sophas to release something like that, but so far I haven't heard anything about it. So, who knows when this will come out, but that might be the one and only change I'm making in the future regarding the network equipment. I also have one thing that I really never seen anywhere else. Yeah, I have one patch panel at the back of the server rack which is connected to one row of the big patch panel at the front. I've done this because I just wanted to have a simple way to connect the cables that come from my switch to the servers in the rack, which usually have the ports on the back. And guys, I hate to route the cables from the front to the back of the server rack. It's always just a mess inside. For the 10 GB fiber optical cables, I still have to do it unfortunately. But yeah, at least not for all of the copper cables. So that's why I have these two patch panels connected to each other. And of course, I also have another row of the patch panel where some cables are going down in the living room, some are going into the studio and some other ones are going to the small eight port patch panel that you've seen on the wall left to the rack. And to connect everything inside the server rack from the patch panels to the switch, I'm using these network cable management boxes from Patchbox, which I got from a collaboration I did around a year ago with them. And these boxes are really super useful in improving your cable management because you can just pull out the cables and they automatically roll up again when you release them. It just looks cool and it is super clean at the front and you always have a cable with the right length available when you want to change something. That is pretty cool because like I said, I often change things in my home lab. I have to admit one thing, these boxes also take up a lot of space. Yeah, but for me uh that really has been very practical. So no issue so far and I'm still planning to keep these patch boxes. As for the network architecture, I've created a diagram for you and certainly also for me to keep an overview. Yeah, so that tells you exactly how all the devices that I've got are connected to each other. You can see that on the top I have my main router here, which is a Fritzbox that connects to my internet via DSL. Last year, I dropped my primary internet uplink, so gateway prod one, which was based on cable internet in favor of DSL. Honestly, the first gateway with a cable connection was a bit faster, but it was ridiculously unreliable. For whatever reason, Germany is still a disaster in terms of availability and reliability of internet. But hope is there because I've just signed a new contract because the house where I'm living in is finally going to get connected to fiber optic. At least that's what the new ISP has been telling me so far. So maybe at someday I will finally get a fast and reliable internet connection and that I will definitely replace the old gateway pro 2 for DSL with a new gateway production 3 based on fiber. Anyway, my home lab doesn't really care about all of that because everything is just behind my firewall production one. So again, this is the Sopus XGS 2100 which controls everything. And of course, I have connected this firewall to the switch with a so-called trunk port. In the past, I also used an LG of two 10 GB fiber optical cables between the firewall and the switch. But because I needed one more SFP plus board on the switch for the secondary Proxmbox node that I built, I just removed that LIG and I'm now using a single 10 GB fiber optical cable connection between the switch and the firewall. That is totally sufficient. By the way, the trunk port, this is a type of a port that carries the traffic for multiple VLANs. So, virtual local area networks, just to tell you quickly how it works. And uh by the way, I've explained this in my VLANs tutorial in a lot more detail. So, on the firewall, I'm managing VLANs for three different type of zones. Like I have one VLAN for my private local area network. I have created a second VLAN for my production server network called pro. And I also have a third one called DMZ. This stands for demilitarized zone and this is actually an isolated network layer usually for connecting public facing services like websites that I might want to host in my home lab and publish to the internet. It's better to isolate these type of services from the rest of the internal network. So currently because I don't like the idea of public facing stuff in my home lab I'm not using this but I just keep it in case I am changing my mind at some day and I want to do something with it in the future. So all of these VLANs are transported over this trunk port from the firewall to the switch and the switch then assigns the VLANs that I want to individual ports where the correct devices are connected to. And to make it a bit simpler for me to know which zone the actual port is connected to, I've used different colors of the patchbox cables which is really nice in combination with the patchbox system. So blue is for the local network, green are for the uplinks, purple is for my pro network and black is for a separate management uh zone that is not tied to a specific VLAN. Instead, it's using an isolated and separate port on the firewall just to have some sort of local backup connection for switch interfaces and something like that. So if we return to the network architecture diagram, you can see what type of devices are connected to the corresponding VLANs because my primary goal was to isolate all of my private devices like my TV, my Xbox, the Wi-Fi access points, and the private phones and laptops from the HomeLab production network where all of the services that I'm running and testing. And because they are connected to the firewall, I can use the rules in the Sopus XG to better control what network traffic specifically is allowed to go from one segment to another. And of course, I also can apply filtering and scanning rules on this traffic. There's only one small server that is called server production 8, which is new, and this is connected to my private LAN. This is, by the way, the second ZMA board that you might have seen. And yeah, this looks pretty new because I'm working on a new video reviewing the prototype of the Zema Board 2. So stay tuned for that. That's going to be pretty cool. And this device I'm planning to use for just Home Assistant because I had a problem with some devices like the Apple HomeKit having to be connected to the same Wi-Fi network. And to avoid having to do some complicated routing between the two VLANs, I just put this small server into the private VLAN. then it's simpler for me to manage and it just works. But the other servers are of course connected to the production VLAN. So this is where my Proxmox cluster is running called pre-production one and the two nodes px prod one and two. In this Proxmox cluster I'm running several virtual servers with the Ubuntu Linux OS mainly from server pro one to prot 6 and an alexe container for my AI workloads. The servers pro 3, four, five, and six also form a Kubernetes cluster together, which I'm actually not using anymore. I just put everything that I want to run in Kubernetes on my new Raspberry Pi cluster that I built last year, but we'll talk about this thing later on. Then there's also my unrated NAS, which is called NASRO 2 because that basically replaced my old NASRO one that I was running with true NAS. And then there's also another small ZMA bot, the server pro 7. This small device is my primary DNS server and I'm using it for all of the name resolutions in my entire home network. It is sometimes a good idea to have as minimal physical dependencies as possible. So if I want to restart my entire Proxmox nodes and shut down the entire server rack for whatever reason. So that's why I thought it might be a good idea to separate these type of critical services on another small power efficient device because it doesn't need to be fast. It just has to be power efficient and just run 24/7. By the way, if you're wondering why I don't go into too much detail about the containers, the uh virtual machines, the Kubernetes clusters in this video, I'm preparing a second video about my home lab stack. So, where I talk about the operating system, this the platforms, the service apps, and all of that stuff in a lot more detail. So, hopefully it's going to be released 2 weeks from now. So, if you don't want to miss that and you like these kind of videos, then do me a favor, hit the like button and subscribe. That would be really amazing. But yeah, so far this is basically the entire network architecture that you've seen here. I think from a technology perspective, uh this is pretty close to what I've learned and saw in the IT industry when I was working as an IT service technician. Of course, on a much smaller scale. Yeah, companies might have more devices. Uh they might use other protocols and technologies like spanning tree, BGP, OPF and these kind of things. But still I think I built this structure with some cool technologies like VLANs multiple zones enterprise grade firewall rules protection APE switch. So here I can basically practice and learn how to actually administer a small network environment that you would see in the industry. So that's why I put a lot of effort into building this infrastructure. All right guys, so I know networking is a vast topic but I think this is really so important to have a solid architecture in your home lab for all of the projects that you're doing and for the server infrastructure I kind of try to follow the same principles. So I tried to use many technologies that you also find in the industry just for practicing like virtualization, network attached storage, high availability. One thing though that I fundamentally do different and what you would never see in a company environment is that I'm building all of my servers that you see in the rack myself. So all of these servers are based on regular consumer hardware. I don't use professional rack servers in my home lab as you might know them from Dell, HP, and other brands. Not only because they are mostly not really power efficient, especially the older generations that you can buy for cheap money on eBay. No, my biggest problem with these professional rack serve equipments are that they are extremely loud. Usually, you don't care about noise in a company environment. Yeah, since you typically have them all in a separate room where it doesn't matter to you. But as you can see, this room where I have my home lab is just right beside my studio where I'm working the entire day. I sometimes even have to leave the door open because in the attic it gets so warm here, especially in the summer. Just imagine I had some professional rack servers besides my desk where all of the fans are running at maximum speed. It would be impossible to work and certainly impossible to record YouTube videos. So that's the main reason. I honestly don't have anything against the idea of using professional server equipment for home lab. Yeah, it's just not practical for my situation regarding noise and power consumption. That's why I always built these servers using power efficient small-sized equipment with regular consumer components like desktop CPUs, noneCC memory, and fans that are not so loud, but still have enough cooling power in the summer. And these components still provide me a decent amount of computing power for all of my workloads that I like to run the home lab. I mean, I don't have any super extensive computing tasks that I'm running for what I'm doing. Most of the regular desktop CPUs and motherboards are totally sufficient. For example, just take a look at the first Proxmbox node that I'm running in my lab which is called PRX Pro one. So, this machine runs in a two unit rack server case from Silverstone. Here I'm using a minisorum BD70i ITX motherboard and this has an onboard AMD Ryzen CPU with eight cores, 16 threads and I've used 32 GB of memory. This is a fast and silent computer that is running my two primary production Linux VMs, Server Pro one and two. So, these VMs run most of the programs I use daily in my home lab, such as my Docker containers, the monitoring stack, the management tools, and some other applications I'm going to show you in a lot more detail in the second video. And because this is such a power efficient server, I can just leave this on all day, all night. I never had any problems with this being too loud or consuming too much power. Regarding that type of equipment, I can just repeat what I've always said in my reviews videos about Minis Forum. I really start to like this brand more and more because of their small, power efficient, and extremely powerful motherboards with onboard CPUs. By the way, if you're not into big server racks, they also have some interesting mini PCs to build a super tiny home server that fits on a desk or on a shelf. But because I've got this big server rack for all the reason I explained in the beginning, of course, I want to use it. And that's the reason why I mostly have rack servers in my home lab, but with individual components that I built myself. And the other Proxmbox node PX Pro 2 is also using a minis for a motherboard, but a newer version, the BD795M8X motherboard. This has an even faster AMD Ryzen CPU and more memory. It has 64 GB and it also has a massive powerful AMD graphics card, the Radon 7,800 XT, which I primarily use for AI workload. So, some of you might remember my last AI videos about Olama and Open Web UI. And this is also the node where I'm running my testing virtual machines. So, the server test 1 2 3 4 5. I'm using this mostly for the experimental and testing workloads in my lab. So, I don't always have this running. Sometimes I just shut it down to save some power when I'm not working. That's also one use case why I wanted to build a Proxmox cluster with one machine that is running 24/7 and another one that I just can turn on when I need it because it allows me to do some really cool things. For example, I can quickly migrate a virtual machine from one Proxmox server to the other. Like when I'm shutting down this secondary Proxmox node, but I still need that one particular workload running, I can just migrate that over to the first one. and also my server pro one and two. If I need to keep that running for whatever reason when I'm doing maintenance on the first node, I can just migrate it over to the secondary one. Of course, when you're running a two node Proxmox cluster, you have to have a third device at least for quorum volts. So otherwise, it just don't work. And that's another job for the small ZMA board on the wall left of the rack which does the DNS neighbor resolution. So when I'm thinking about it, even it is such a small device, it's actually doing some really critical work. Maybe I have to move that over to another server at some point. But yeah, that's currently how the architecture works. Only problem that I currently have with this setup is, and sure, this is not a huge problem, but this Proxmox cluster is not fully high available. It's redundant because it has two nodes, but high availability would mean something else. It would mean if one of these Proxmox servers dies for whatever reason, that the VMs are automatically migrated and booted up on the other Proxmox node. And this is currently not working with my setup because I don't have a shared or replicated storage for the VM discs. So when I want to migrate a VM from one node to another, it first has to be copied between the nodes which not only takes some time but also in an outage this would never run. Now regarding high availability in Proxmox, there are three solutions that I'm currently investigating. Yeah, maybe I could add a ZFS storage pool with a replication job between the nodes. So then I could at least make the first two critical production VMs high available so that they would automatically boot up on a secondary Proxmox node when the first one goes down. The second idea I also thought about using my NAS with an SCSI shared storage to load the VM disc from so that they don't rely on a local storage or replication. But the main problem is that my NAS currently is not capable of doing that in a performant way. another topic that we also need to talk about in a minute. And the third idea is I could just add a third Proxmbox node and use a distributed storage with Seth. This would allow me to make all the VMs high available because two of the three Proxmox node could easily handle the additional workload. But honestly guys, I'm not willing to invest more money and more power consumption in a third Proxmox node in my lab. I'm so happy with my tunbox cluster and I believe this would just be a waste of money. Even though it would be a fun and interesting project, I agree. But I'm still not convinced by this idea. I think it also wouldn't work great when I frequently want to shut down one of these servers. So adding a third Proxmox node to my home lab probably not the way I want to go. So long story short, true high availability in my home lab seems to be an ambitious project. Maybe the easiest solution would really be to improve my NAS setup so that I can use it as a shared storage with CSSI for the VM discs. Which brings me to the next uh equipment that I need to talk about in my lab, which definitely also need further improvement. My Android NAS the biggest problem that I have with this thing is the hardware specs. Because this build is not based on a minis motherboard, I've used an Astro motherboard with an Intel N100 chip. And at first, this sounded like a great idea because my previous server, if some of the guys remember that project, was extremely oversized. And I just wanted to have something smaller, less power hungry than before. So, I assumed using the Intel N100 was a good fit for building a NAS. But honestly, if I compare this to a Mi forum board that I'm using in my Proxmox server, it's not that much more power efficient. Furthermore, the much bigger problem is that the ASRock motherboard only has one M2 interface and one old PCI Express 3.0 slot and only a 1 GB network interface. It even has a much slower CPU compared to a Minis Forum. Looking back, I think it would have been so much better to just use another Minis Forum board, for example, the BD770i. And with this, I could still build a power efficient storage server with unrade, but it would have some additional performance and some other options I could use to build a secondary NVME storage pool for my VM discs to achieve high availability in Proxmox. Or maybe I could run an additional virtual machine on this unrated server, for example, a Proxmox backup server. Now, still I won't say that this entire project with the unrated server was a terrible idea. I'm still pretty happy with choosing Unrid as the operating system because I really like the Unrate array because this provides me a super efficient cold storage solution with all of the magnetic hard drives which is great for storing backups and large files like my video projects and so on. But for a hot storage solution, the Android array is not great. So I would need another storage pool in Android which you definitely can do with Android. So, I could add a ZFS or BTRFS pool with some fast drives just like I've done it for the SSD caching, but I think I would have to put more drives into this pool. And having an additional PCI Express or another M2 slot on this storage server build would really be amazing. Maybe it would be a great idea to switch the motherboard that I currently have in my first Proxmox node, the BD770i, to this Unrade server. So then it would replace the SRock board with the Intel N100 chip. And for the first Proxmox node, I would just buy another minis forum BD795M. So the same model that I'm using in the second node. Then I have two Proxmox machines with the exact same setup, which would also be even better for achieving high availability. But yeah, these are my thoughts about changing things on the Proxmox and storage front. So there are still a lot that I can improve there and some cool projects that I would like to do in the future. But still be patient. you won't see these kind of videos coming out in the next time because I still need more time to research and test these scenarios. But I'm definitely working on it. So somewhere in this year, I will have to make this upgrade. All right, so I know the video is already too long, but I think we've covered most of the important hardware equipment. There's just one more thing that I quickly want to talk about, and this is the Raspberry Pi Kubernetes cluster that I'm running. And guys, this is also kind of a project that I'm really not happy with. Honestly, the more I'm thinking about this build, the more I believe doing this entire project was a mistake. Maybe I got too inspired by these other YouTubers building these crazy Raspberry Pi clusters and I wanted to have something similar. But looking back, I think that was a bad decision because the Raspberry Pi, even the newest version 5, is really not the fastest computer in the world. If you compare this to an Intel N100, it definitely falls behind. And it also doesn't have an x86 architecture. So you're already kind of limited when it comes to container images and workloads. And furthermore, it also doesn't have a fast network connection. So when I need to take backups or I need to replicate storage, it's also kind of limited. And the entire equipment for this project, like the rack neck case that I got to put the Raspberry Pies all side by side into the wreck is really nice. But boy, this was super expensive. Yeah, I had to buy the case. I had to buy the Raspberry Pies, the NVME drives, the PoE head, some screws and cables to mount everything. Not to forget these LCD screens that I just wanted to have. Still doing this project was a lot of fun, so I don't wholly regret it. But this project I would never do again this way because if you really think about it, if you want to run a Kubernetes cluster at home besides all the other projects that you need to do. I think it would be just better to run this on a few Linux VMs instead of Raspberry Pies. Yeah, just like I've done it in the past because then you would have a fast Kubernetes cluster with an x86 architecture and so on. Especially when I'm planning to make the whole Proxmox infrastructure high available and so on. it just seems to be the better architecture for me. Of course, some people might argue know well you could even run a few virtual machines on Kubernetes but then honestly the entire Proxmox setup is going in the wrong direction. So I think you have two choices here. Either stick to Proxmox running containers in virtual machines and run Kubernetes in virtual in virtual machines or you can go a complete different direction. forget about Proxmox, install a bare metal Kubernetes cluster on some powerful physical servers and then start playing around with something like Harvester and so on. But I still like to keep Proxmox for so many reasons. So that's not the direction I want to go. But either way, this Raspberry Pi cluster project doesn't fit in any of these scenarios. Anyway, I'm still keeping it uh because I've built it. So, I'll continue using it because I already migrated some of the workloads that were running on the first Kubernetes cluster. And maybe occasionally I will do this one or another project with it in the future. But yeah, sometimes you just get a little too excited about projects like this in the first place and in the end you just find out, well, wasn't the smartest decision. Okay guys, congratulations. You have made it to the end. Yeah, that's everything that I'm running in my home lab so far. And as you've heard, there are still some projects that I might want to do during this year, like achieving high availability in the Proxbox cluster, upgrading the storage server, and experimenting with Proxmbox backup server. So, that gives you an idea of some of the projects that I'm working on. But overall, I'm very happy with my home lab as it is today, and I'd love to hear your feedback about it. Please don't be so harsh to me for some of the decisions that I've made. And by the way, if you have a similar home lab project and you just want to share some pictures of it, then check out my Discord. I have created a separate channel where we can share our experience about our home lab projects and you can see what other peoples in our community have accomplished by doing this wonderful hobby. So, please leave me some comments below and join the discord. And as always, thank you so much for watching. A big thanks goes out to all of the supporters, Patreon members, and YouTube channel members. You guys are really the best. You make all of this possible. And thank you so much. Of course, I'm going to catch you in the next video. Take care. Bye-bye.

=== New Docker Hub pull rate limits? What you have to do… ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JNkLbT_2JQg

hey guys it's Christian and today we need to talk about the recent change in the docker Hub rate limits that you should definitely be aware of if you're pulling container images from the docker Hub which I think almost everybody watching this channel sometimes does so this is really a Hot Topic everybody needs to know because starting on April the 1st one day after publishing this video and no this is no joke the dockerhub usage and limits will be updated to only allow 10 PS per hour for unauthenticated user and 100 PS per hour for every authenticated user with a free account so otherwise if you go beyond that you'll be rate limited and that means you won't be able to pull down new container images at least for 1 hour now this definitely will become a huge problem for many many people especially in automation pipelines that usually are not authenticated to the docker Hub but frequently have to pull new container images to run their execution tasks remember in one of my last videos where we talked about gitlab cicd yes this is exactly where this will become a problem for us so yeah this definitely will become annoying however I'm not the one who likes to blame Docker too much for making this change because keep in mind they are maintaining the docker Hub this massive platform that literally everybody uses to host container images entirely for free so I can somehow understand why they wanted to make this change yeah and limiting the request from the free users and giving somebody at least a reason to buy the premium Docker up subscription but yeah it definitely will introduce a bunch of issues even gitlab has released a blog post about this change where they talk about the new pull rate limits and what that actually means for gitlab cicd pipelines so here they address all of these issues and explain what's going to happen the cool thing is that they also tell us how to mitigate these challenges for example they added one new feature in the gitlab dependency proxy that will allow AO ated pipelines to authenticate to the docker Hub which will increase the rate limits to 100 PS per hour instead of just 10 of course there are also some other ways around it like dockerhub is of course not the one and only container registry that you can use you can think about just using a different container registry that is still free like the gitlab container registry this is all built into their platform and of course this also means you can self Host this in your environment so when you're Building containa images in an automation p lines just don't publish them to dockerhub just upload them to gitlab and of course GitHub also has some equivalent solution the GitHub container registry that I'm Sometimes using for publishing my container images that are tied to a GitHub repository so you can see there are definitely ways around it and honestly I wouldn't be surprised if many organizations now start thinking about if they would migrate to another container registry after this change maybe to GitHub or gitlab where their open and Source projects are hosted anyways but of course the docker Hub is still the biggest platform that most === GitLab CI CD automation (Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, and more…) ===
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zBrP8MzA5y0

Hey everybody, this is Christian and today I will show you how I'm automating my home lab with GitLab CI/CD. This is absolutely amazing because it allows me to streamline all of my workflows in fully automated pipelines. So I can sit back and relax while it's doing things like deploying Docker Compose and Kubernetes projects for me or configuring and provisioning infrastructure using Terraform. It's like magic and the possibilities for using it are really endless. But I want to make this clear. This is not just about saving time and being more convenient when managing your personal projects. No, this is also a great chance to learn more about continuous integration, delivery, and deployment or what we shortly call CI/CD. This is nowadays a super important topic to know in it because it helps us to build and ship applications faster, more securely, and reliably. With GitLab CI/CD, you can easily learn and implement this technique completely for free in your own self-hosted environment. It's not just cool, it's empowering. So, let me show you how I'm doing this in my home lab and hopefully inspire you to use it in your projects as well. But before we start, I quickly like to show you another incredible platform that I recently tested in my home lab called CheckMK, the sponsor of today's video. Czechm is a comprehensive IT monitoring platform that is scalable, automated, and highly extensible. With over 2,000 GBLV2 licensed plugins, Czechm can monitor nearly all network components across different manufacturers. You can even write custom plugins to monitor anything you want. It offers auto discovery with preconfigured thresholds and rules, and the features are insane. It is massively scalable to monitor hundreds of thousands of hosts and millions of devices. And furthermore, it creates stunning visualizations and has dynamic dashboards with logs and event monitoring that allow you to really drill in all of the details of your infrastructure. Of course, it also notifies you about anything that requires your attention. And the cool thing is they even have a free and open source version that you can easily selfhost in your IT environment. Of course, for professional companies, they also have enterprise cloud and MSP versions that even come with more features, but I recently deployed the free and open source version in my home lab to monitor the health and status of my Proxmox host VMs, Docker containers, and even my firewall using SNMP. Just to let you know, I'm planning a full dedicated tutorial on CzechMK as well. But if you'd like to try it out yourself, check out the official website and get started today. Of course, I'll put your link to it in the description box down below. All right, so now let's get back to CI/CD. And first of all, let me introduce the concept. So what does CI/CD actually mean? What are the typical components and what solutions exist so we can understand how GitLab fits into this bigger picture? The first part of CI/CD stands for continuous integration, an agile software development practice that runs automated builds and tests against code changes to help detect integration issues early in the software development life cycle. This is really important primarily when many developers work on a single project and merge their work into a shared repository because one developer might not necessarily know what the others are doing and without a proper validation process of the codebase any new changes might introduce conflicts with existing parts of the code. Or maybe another use case is you want your code changes to automatically be checked for programmatic or stylic errors before merging. This is also called linting in software development. And this is super helpful because I bet you all know these situations where you're just missing a semicolon somewhere in the code or you have a wrong indent level in Python. It's really a pain to find these type of issues in your code that's causing everything to break. And by using an automated validation process that runs every time you push changes to the repository, this is really great to detect bugs early and ultimately improve the overall quality of your code. So that's it about CI. But in addition to that, we also have CD, which stands for continuous deployment or continuous delivery. Actually, before anyone wants me to correct in the comments, I know deployment and delivery are two separate processes, but they're often most used together. So for simplicity, I think you can see this as just one step that streamlines the deployment process of your code. So in reality, this means once a new change is tested and verified by the CI automation, the CD automation can immediately deploy this on the infrastructure without someone having to manually log into a service, download the new version from the repository and apply the changes manually, which not only reduces the likelihood of mistakes, but it also enhances the efficiency in the deployment process, of course. So this whole CI/CD concept is an essential part of DevOps and over time many different tools and applications have been created with very unique features and integrations to help organizations implement the CI/CD practices. For example, Jenkins is a pretty popular solution that is designed to handle anything from a simple CI server to a complete CD hub or Argo CD, a continuous delivery tool designed specifically just for Kubernetes applications. I've also tested this on the channel in the past by the way. But of course, big git platforms like GitHub, GitLab or even Git have built their own integrations and solutions for CI/CD directly into their platforms. And the cool thing about GitLab CI/CD in this case is that it is completely available in all of the tiers. So from free to premium in ultimate and it can be used in any offering like it runs on their gitlab.com cloud platform as well as on the self-managed devices which I think is really great for privacy and local deployments in protected networks. And it is super simple to use because you don't need to install any third-party program or tool. You can just use this in all of your existing GitLab repositories. And by the way, if you want to know how to deploy such a self-hosted GitLab instance in your own uh HomeLab environment, I've made a full tutorial about this. Of course, you will find a link to it in the description. So, check that out. And all you have to do to simply enable GitLab CI/CD in all of your projects is create agitabi.l file where you can add all of your instructions and settings and then find or create some GitLab runners for your projects. Now that's the next concept I have to explain. What the hell is a runner? Well, runners are simply agents that executes the task that you have defined in the CI/CD pipeline. So this is where the code validation, the testing or the deployment process are actually running on and a runner can be anything from a simple Linux, Windows or Mac OS machine to a virtual machine running on a hypervisor like Proxmox or you can even deploy a GitLab runner in a docker container. So the way how you deploy this GitLab runner is completely up to you. You can even deploy a fleet of different GitLab runners as many as you need, which is really important because some of the programs or automation pipelines that you want to run won't work on every environment and every operating system or technology. Of course, I just give you a very basic example. when you want to build a docker container image automatically from your source code in the git repository that doesn't really work so great when the runner itself is also running in the docker container so this is also called docker in docker and it really doesn't have a great performance and so on so that's where you might want to install a separate runner just for building docker images and install this on a virtual machine or if you're going to build container images for the ARM infrastructure it doesn't work so well on an x8 86 architecture. So you might run another GitLab runner on a Raspberry Pi for example for faster building ARM images. So these are some usual type of workflows where you might want to control multiple runners and use tags in your automation pipelines to specifically use that infrastructure that you need for this automation pipeline. But we will go through the pipelines in a second here. Just to simplify this tutorial, I will only show you how to deploy a GitLab runner in a Docker compos project. That's what I'm using in my home lab as well. But just keep in mind that it doesn't necessarily have to be the preferred deployment for everything and there are different possibilities on how you can set up a GitLab runner. Adjust that depending on the environment that you want to use it for. All right, so let's go to my self-hosted GitLab platform and let's create a new runner so that you can follow the process. This can be done in three different levels. You can use a runner just for one git repository. You can add a runner that is available in a group. So this runner can then be used throughout all of the repositories attached to this group. But you can also create instance runners. So they are available across the entire GitLab instance. So that's what I personally would recommend you to do because then you can use runner throughout all of the projects and groups that you have created. So let's go to the administration interface and go to CI/CD. Here in the runner section, you can register and create new runners. It's very simple. You just click on new instance runner and then you can give this runner specific tag. So again, the tags can be used in the automation pipeline to define which runner to use for what type of task. If you don't want to use tags and you just use this runner for all of your jobs, you can also click on run untag jobs. There are also some other optional configurations. If you only want to use that runner in protected branches only. But usually you don't need to configure anything else here. Just click on create runner. Now you can choose where you want to install this. If you want to install this on Linux, Mac or Windows, Google Cloud, or maybe you want to use containers like Docker or Kubernetes. So you might wonder why the heck do we need the docker socket? Well the process is how it runs. The GitLab runner itself will run in a Docker container. But if you start a new automation process, it will spawn a new Docker container with a temporary process where all of the automation steps are running and then afterward it deletes it. So this is done by the configuration setting here in the config file executor docker. So there are different types of executors. Again I will only show you this example here. Of course, when you have automation pipelines that invoke specific processes like shell scripts and so on, you might not need to run them in a separate Docker container, but I think this is really great because for these automation pipelines, you might want Ethereal processes. And this is where Docker containers are just the best for this is why I'm using the Docker executor to spawn a new temporary process executing all of the steps that I need and then exit. looking for this is absolutely amazing and as you can imagine this is super important to know and use in professional it environments but of course it's also a huge help in your home Labs as well I've and start securing your network of course I'll drop you a link to the website in the description down below all right guys so now let's get started with grafana Loki and S Cal lock management first of all I want to give you a quick overview of what Central Lock management actually means why do we use it in it and how does it generally work now grafana Loi is what we call a horizontally scalable High available multi-tenant lock aggregation system that has been inspired by Prometheus so for those of you who don't know Prometheus this is another pretty popular tool that we use in it to collect and aggregate metrics from different types of systems now to quickly explain what is the difference between locks and metrics metrics are kind of Statistics that are reported by Services applications or systems something like how high is the CPU load or how much is the memory or disk utilization how many users are currently locked into your system and all of these statistics and reportings can be collected and aggregated in a huge database using promethus and then you can search and filter through that database query information you can also use the grafana system to visualize and get some better insight into performance and those kind of things of your systems and what promethus is for metrix Graf Loki is somewhat for locks so this is also a huge database where you collect and ingest all the different locks that are created on your servers or created by your applications and they often contain information about errors that occur at a specific time but also informational things warnings traces so this is super useful to troubleshoot and follow the execution path of a program to find out at which point something went wrong so as you can imagine this is also super important in it environment so we actually need both we need metrics for performance statistics and we need locks to troubleshoot systems when something went wrong or getting alerts about specific events that occur on our systems now one of the biggest differences between grafana Loki and other competitive lock aggregation systems something like elastic search for example is that grafana Loki is designed to be a bit simpler and coste effective so it doesn't index the entire content of your locks it is only attaching labels to it that are indexed making it faster and consuming less resources I've also created a short presentation of the architecture of grafana Loki to explain how the different components work and how they interact with each other so in the middle we have grafana Loki this is the huge database that contains and manages all the locks from the different systems here on the left side you can see we can use a bunch of different lock collectors something like promail which is a very simple service application that can scrape through all the different files grab everything that it finds there and sends it to graph Loi attached with the right label so you can later find out what was the location what was the time and which service or which lock file does it come from there's also another pretty cool thing that is also a reason why I wanted to get my head around graan Loki and this is the docker plugin so you can also easily collect all the different locks that the container applications Ry to your system and also send this all to grafana Loki with attaching the labels for each container so you can easily filter and search for anything that has been locked on your container orchestration systems super useful and by the way these are not the only lock collectors that are existing for graphi there are many many more as you can imagine there is something like Rafa alloy so this is kind of a more complex a new application that is aiming to replace prom tail and also has a bunch of other different features something like Integrations with kubernetes and stuff but honestly I didn't want to be too complicated in the first video so we will start with those two lock collectors we will connect the docker plugin and prom tail so this is probably the most simple way to start but just know there are other loog collectors so you can add your kubernetes locks and send it to graph Loki you can add custom lock collectors that through specific applications or specific files that you're defining and then when grafana Loi receives all the locks it will store it somewhere in a Central Storage location so there are different types of storage options as well for example an S3 bucket Google uh cloud storage and you can or you can just write it to the file system now one thing you should um keep in mind for these different types of storage systems is that the file system isn't really recommended in production but we will use it as I don't want to use a cloud storage for this demonstration if you want to find out more about the storage options there's also a great documentation page that will explain what are the two different types of data so in grafana Loki there are data chunks and data indexes and these are two different types of storage to quickly explain when graph Loi receives all the locks you can imagine this can grow up a lot in size and uh to make it efficient it is using these labels and stores them in an index storage and the index just contains all the necessary data to find the relevant lock file so it has a link to the actual content of the locks that is stored in the chunk data in a compressed format and this you can store in production systems in an S3 bucket GCS all of these Cloud things but as I said previously I don't like using these Cloud systems in my home lab I'm just using a simple file system and to quickly return to the presentation there's also another component that you might need and this is the grafana dashboard most of you guys probably have it already running in your home lives because I've made a video about grafana and how to visualize metrics in promethos or influx DB using the grafana dashboard the cool thing about grafana Loki is that you don't need another dashboard you don't need another graphical user interface you can just connect grafana Loki to your existing grafana uh instance so that is also one of the reasons why I picked grafana Loki instead of other lock aggregation systems because I'm running grafana dashboard already I absolutely love it and I think it just was the best decision to use grafana Loki because I can easily connect these two systems and then I have my metrics and my locks in one central place I think this absolutely fantastic and there's also another cool Advantage if you want to visualize locks and you're familiar with Prometheus if you know promql you are also with lock ql so there's no new language that you need to learn but don't worry if you're not familiar with these quer languages I don't know them myself either so we're not covering it in this video maybe in a future video but for this video you just need those components here and to make it a bit more secure of course we are throwing in traffic and also authentic but those two things are kind of optional all right so that's it about the presentation don't worry there's just one more theoretical part that we need to cover before jumping into the terminal and get it up and running so um we need to cover how to set this whole thing up because grafana Loki might sound like a simple and easy lock aggregation system but it can get complicated as well if you really drill into all the different components so there are different types of lowkey deployment modes depending on what you want in your environment the first there is the monolithic mode this is by the way also the most simple mode and the mode that we are cover covering in this tutorial so don't worry it's not getting too complicated but the monolithic mode actually runs all the different components inside a single process or container which is great for single server environments if you want to set up grafana Loki in a darker container and this can handle approximately about 20 gbt per day so this is already pretty sophisticated and can work in even smaller uh professional environments as well and by the way if you want to combine this with a shared volume something like NFS or s3e you you can also scale this as well you shouldn't underestimate this setup
